feet stretched out, leaning against the bars as silent and as patient as
if he were a stone statue and not a man of flesh.

pairs.display: Why, wondered Genji, was he, who took such pleasure in quiet and harmless pursuits, completely puzzled, and asked Sancho if it was outside the city he had left them.
pairs.genji_half: Why, wondered Genji, was he, who took such pleasure in quiet and harmless pursuits,
pairs.quijote_half: completely puzzled, and asked Sancho if it was outside the city he had left them.
pairs.genji_source:
Why, wondered
Genji, was he, who took such pleasure in quiet and harmless pursuits,
doomed on every occasion to find himself involved in the most harrowing
and disastrous situations?
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote looked all along the road to El Toboso, and as
he could see nobody except the three peasant girls, he was completely
puzzled, and asked Sancho if it was outside the city he had left them.

pairs.display: After this visit Genji’s first care was to perform the ceremonial Eight he seemed to feel lively and began pawing--for as to capering, begging his pardon, he knew not what it meant.
pairs.genji_half: After this visit Genji’s first care was to perform the ceremonial Eight
pairs.quijote_half: he seemed to feel lively and began pawing--for as to capering, begging his pardon, he knew not what it meant.
pairs.genji_source:
After this visit Genji’s first care was to perform the ceremonial Eight
Readings of the Lotus Sūtra in memory of his father the late Emperor.
pairs.quijote_source:
As soon as
Rocinante found himself free, though by nature he was not at all
mettlesome, he seemed to feel lively and began pawing--for as to
capering, begging his pardon, he knew not what it meant.

pairs.display: He was only eleven years old but was big for his age, three kerchiefs that were worth three cities were missing.
pairs.genji_half: He was only eleven years old but was big for his age,
pairs.quijote_half: three kerchiefs that were worth three cities were missing.
pairs.genji_source:
He was only eleven years old but was big
for his age, and it was already apparent that he was developing an
extraordinary resemblance to his guardian, Prince Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho said they had, but that three kerchiefs that were worth three
cities were missing.

pairs.display: Her one consolation had been that by her death Aoi had been spared the torture and the pair shut themselves up in his room, where they had another conversation not inferior to the previous one.
pairs.genji_half: Her one consolation had been that by her death Aoi had been spared the torture
pairs.quijote_half: and the pair shut themselves up in his room, where they had another conversation not inferior to the previous one.
pairs.genji_source:
Her one consolation had been that
by her death Aoi had been spared the torture which Genji’s disgrace
and banishment would have inflicted upon her proud and fastidious
nature.
pairs.quijote_source:
The niece let him in, and his
master Don Quixote came forward to receive him with open arms, and the
pair shut themselves up in his room, where they had another conversation
not inferior to the previous one.

pairs.display: Genji continued to show her the same attentions as before his exile and the first thing he saw was Don Fernando with Luscinda in his arms.
pairs.genji_half: Genji continued to show her the same attentions as before his exile
pairs.quijote_half: and the first thing he saw was Don Fernando with Luscinda in his arms.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji continued to show her the same
attentions as before his exile and lost no opportunity of going over to
the Great Hall.
pairs.quijote_source:
Cardenio also heard
Dorothea’s cry as she fell fainting, and imagining that it came from his
Luscinda burst forth in terror from the room, and the first thing he saw
was Don Fernando with Luscinda in his arms.

pairs.display: This was Genji’s first daughter, of which a certain share fell upon Maritornes, who, irritated by the pain and flinging modesty aside, paid back so many in return to Sancho that she woke him up in spite of himself.
pairs.genji_half: This was Genji’s first daughter,
pairs.quijote_half: of which a certain share fell upon Maritornes, who, irritated by the pain and flinging modesty aside, paid back so many in return to Sancho that she woke him up in spite of himself.
pairs.genji_source: This was Genji’s first daughter, and he felt quite excited.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this Sancho awoke, and feeling this mass
almost on top of him fancied he had the nightmare and began to distribute
fisticuffs all round, of which a certain share fell upon Maritornes, who,
irritated by the pain and flinging modesty aside, paid back so many in
return to Sancho that she woke him up in spite of himself.

pairs.display: She had sometimes done service at the Palace so that they might all hear it.
pairs.genji_half: She had sometimes done service at the Palace
pairs.quijote_half: so that they might all hear it.
pairs.genji_source:
She had sometimes done service at the Palace and this was not the first
time Genji had seen her.
pairs.quijote_source:
While they were talking Cardenio had taken up the novel and begun to read
it, and forming the same opinion of it as the curate, he begged him to
read it so that they might all hear it.

pairs.display: These fresh tokens of Genji’s affection which, as he himself has told me, is the greatest victory anyone can desire.
pairs.genji_half: These fresh tokens of Genji’s affection
pairs.quijote_half: which, as he himself has told me, is the greatest victory anyone can desire.
pairs.genji_source:
These fresh tokens of Genji’s affection and concern could not fail
somewhat to revive her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Open thine arms and receive, too, thy son Don Quixote,
who, if he comes vanquished by the arm of another, comes victor over
himself, which, as he himself has told me, is the greatest victory anyone
can desire.

pairs.display: The messenger duly arrived, bearing with him the most touching a knight-errant, and he becoming puffed up and exalted in consequence could not contain himself for satisfaction.
pairs.genji_half: The messenger duly arrived, bearing with him the most touching
pairs.quijote_half: a knight-errant, and he becoming puffed up and exalted in consequence could not contain himself for satisfaction.
pairs.genji_source:
The messenger duly arrived, bearing with him the most touching and
gratifying tokens of Genji’s anxiety for the welfare of his friends.
pairs.quijote_source:
Some of Don Antonio’s
friends dined with him that day, and all showed honour to Don Quixote and
treated him as a knight-errant, and he becoming puffed up and exalted in
consequence could not contain himself for satisfaction.

pairs.display: Power was now pretty equally divided between Genji himself and the bachelor, who came towards them with open arms.
pairs.genji_half: Power was now pretty equally divided between Genji himself
pairs.quijote_half: and the bachelor, who came towards them with open arms.
pairs.genji_source:
Power was now pretty equally divided between Genji himself
and his father-in-law, the old Minister at the Great Hall.
pairs.quijote_source:
They were at once recognised by both the
curate and the bachelor, who came towards them with open arms.

pairs.display: In the autumn Genji made his pilgrimage to the Shrine of Sumiyoshi, hitting on the secret of the trick, but all concentrated on the promise, which he regarded as a certainty, of Dulcinea’s disenchantment.
pairs.genji_half: In the autumn Genji made his pilgrimage to the Shrine of Sumiyoshi,
pairs.quijote_half: hitting on the secret of the trick, but all concentrated on the promise, which he regarded as a certainty, of Dulcinea’s disenchantment.
pairs.genji_source:
In the autumn Genji made his pilgrimage to the Shrine of Sumiyoshi,
where, as will be remembered, he had various vows to fulfil.
pairs.quijote_source:
Profound were Don Quixote’s reflections on the reply of the enchanted
head, not one of them, however, hitting on the secret of the trick, but
all concentrated on the promise, which he regarded as a certainty, of
Dulcinea’s disenchantment.

pairs.display: She determined to renew the practice, and it so happened that she arrived and smote him such a blow with it that Sancho dropped helpless to the ground.
pairs.genji_half: She determined to renew the practice, and it so happened that she arrived
pairs.quijote_half: and smote him such a blow with it that Sancho dropped helpless to the ground.
pairs.genji_source:
She determined to renew the practice, and it so happened that she
arrived by boat at Sumiyoshi just as Genji’s magnificent procession
was passing along the shore.
pairs.quijote_source:
One of those, however, that stood near him, fancying he was mocking them,
lifted up a long staff he had in his hand and smote him such a blow with
it that Sancho dropped helpless to the ground.

pairs.display: She was told that this was Prince Yūgiri, they approached slowly until they were near enough to hear distinctly Don Quixote’s voice calling to them.
pairs.genji_half: She was told that this was Prince Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: they approached slowly until they were near enough to hear distinctly Don Quixote’s voice calling to them.
pairs.genji_source:
She was told that this was Prince Yūgiri,
Genji’s son by Lady Aoi.
pairs.quijote_source:
They all stopped, and perceived that it was Don Quixote who was making
signals, and shaking off their fears to some extent, they approached
slowly until they were near enough to hear distinctly Don Quixote’s voice
calling to them.

pairs.display: This she did, while Genji, still unaware that she had been so near him, let himself drop off Dapple at Rocinante’s feet, sore, bruised, and belaboured.
pairs.genji_half: This she did, while Genji, still unaware that she had been so near him,
pairs.quijote_half: let himself drop off Dapple at Rocinante’s feet, sore, bruised, and belaboured.
pairs.genji_source:
This she did, while Genji, still unaware that she had
been so near him, spent the rest of the evening preforming his vows
within the Shrine.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho, lying across his
ass, followed him, as has been said, and at length came up, having by
this time recovered his senses, and on joining him let himself drop off
Dapple at Rocinante’s feet, sore, bruised, and belaboured.

pairs.display: This could not be denied, Don Quixote sallied out of the inn to act as sentinel of the castle as he had promised.
pairs.genji_half: This could not be denied,
pairs.quijote_half: Don Quixote sallied out of the inn to act as sentinel of the castle as he had promised.
pairs.genji_source:
This could not
be denied, and indeed to Genji too a joyful time had succeeded to an
age of sadness.
pairs.quijote_source:
The ladies, then, having retired to their chamber, and the others having
disposed themselves with as little discomfort as they could, Don Quixote
sallied out of the inn to act as sentinel of the castle as he had
promised.

pairs.display: Before the funeral Genji called in person upon us in place of the real husband.
pairs.genji_half: Before the funeral Genji called in person
pairs.quijote_half: upon us in place of the real husband.
pairs.genji_source:
Before the funeral Genji called in person
and sent in a note to the bereaved lady of the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
They have put Tosilos, my
lord the duke’s lacquey, upon us in place of the real husband.

pairs.display: The ladies concerned knew that their doings would probably reach Genji’s ears, Don Quixote without his armour and in travelling gear, and Sancho on foot, Dapple being loaded with the armour.
pairs.genji_half: The ladies concerned knew that their doings would probably reach Genji’s ears,
pairs.quijote_half: Don Quixote without his armour and in travelling gear, and Sancho on foot, Dapple being loaded with the armour.
pairs.genji_source:
The ladies concerned knew
that their doings would probably reach Genji’s ears, and they were far
too anxious to stand well with him to dream of abusing their position.
pairs.quijote_source:
So the two of them took their departure, and Don Quixote and Sancho
afterwards, as has been already said, Don Quixote without his armour and
in travelling gear, and Sancho on foot, Dapple being loaded with the
armour.

pairs.display: At last came Genji’s pardon and he himself would make a fair copy of it.
pairs.genji_half: At last came Genji’s pardon and
pairs.quijote_half: he himself would make a fair copy of it.
pairs.genji_source:
At last came Genji’s pardon and recall, celebrated in every part of the
kingdom by riotous holiday-making and rejoicing.
pairs.quijote_source:
On
this the curate said if he showed it to him, he himself would make a fair
copy of it.

pairs.display: Just let Prince Genji have a look at the place for all would go well and turn out as he wished.
pairs.genji_half: Just let Prince Genji have a look at the place
pairs.quijote_half: for all would go well and turn out as he wished.
pairs.genji_source:
Just let Prince Genji have a look at the place for
himself; he’ll soon know how things stand!
pairs.quijote_source:
Dorothea said she fully believed it, and that he need not be uneasy, for
all would go well and turn out as he wished.

pairs.display: I will go to Prince Genji at once as he saw there was but little more left to read.
pairs.genji_half: I will go to Prince Genji at once
pairs.quijote_half: as he saw there was but little more left to read.
pairs.genji_source: I will go to Prince Genji at once and tell him of this.’
pairs.quijote_source:
All therefore being
appeased, the curate was anxious to go on with the novel, as he saw there
was but little more left to read.

pairs.display: Even so, the ends of Genji’s trousers embraced once more, and renewed their promises.
pairs.genji_half: Even so, the ends of Genji’s trousers
pairs.quijote_half: embraced once more, and renewed their promises.
pairs.genji_source:
Even so, the ends of Genji’s trousers became dripping
wet before he reached the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate promised to comply with his request
carefully, and they embraced once more, and renewed their promises.

pairs.display: At last, just as she had always predicted, not daring to quit the trough and leave his armour unprotected.
pairs.genji_half: At last, just as she had always predicted,
pairs.quijote_half: not daring to quit the trough and leave his armour unprotected.
pairs.genji_source:
At last, just as she had
always predicted, Genji had come back!
pairs.quijote_source:
The comrades
of the wounded perceiving the plight they were in began from a distance
to shower stones on Don Quixote, who screened himself as best he could
with his buckler, not daring to quit the trough and leave his armour
unprotected.

pairs.display: It was well known in society that Genji must not go to Seville until he had cleared all these mountains of highwaymen and robbers, of whom report said they were full.
pairs.genji_half: It was well known in society that Genji
pairs.quijote_half: must not go to Seville until he had cleared all these mountains of highwaymen and robbers, of whom report said they were full.
pairs.genji_source:
It was well known in society
that Genji took little interest in the common run of women.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote thanked them for their advice and for the
disposition they showed to do him a favour, and said that for the present
he would not, and must not go to Seville until he had cleared all these
mountains of highwaymen and robbers, of whom report said they were full.

pairs.display: This jungle Genji now ordered to be reduced to tolerable dimensions; he had the ponds cleared they ransacked the alforjas, and without any ceremony very peacefully and sociably master and man made their repast on what they found in them.
pairs.genji_half: This jungle Genji now ordered to be reduced to tolerable dimensions; he had the ponds cleared
pairs.quijote_half: they ransacked the alforjas, and without any ceremony very peacefully and sociably master and man made their repast on what they found in them.
pairs.genji_source:
This jungle Genji now ordered to
be reduced to tolerable dimensions; he had the ponds cleared and
pleasant streams were made to run in and out among the flower-beds.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote and Sancho dismounted, and turning Rocinante
and the ass loose to feed on the grass that was there in abundance, they
ransacked the alforjas, and without any ceremony very peacefully and
sociably master and man made their repast on what they found in them.

pairs.display: It had at one time seemed as though Genji’s banishment might last indefinitely, as the innkeeper had purposely extinguished the lantern on retreating to his room, he was compelled to have recourse to the hearth, where after much time and trouble he lit another lamp.
pairs.genji_half: It had at one time seemed as though Genji’s banishment might last indefinitely,
pairs.quijote_half: as the innkeeper had purposely extinguished the lantern on retreating to his room, he was compelled to have recourse to the hearth, where after much time and trouble he lit another lamp.
pairs.genji_source:
It had at one
time seemed as though Genji’s banishment might last indefinitely,
far longer in any case than Iyo no Suke’s short term of office.
pairs.quijote_source:
The cuadrillero on this let go Don Quixote’s beard, and
went out to look for a light to search for and apprehend the culprits;
but not finding one, as the innkeeper had purposely extinguished the
lantern on retreating to his room, he was compelled to have recourse to
the hearth, where after much time and trouble he lit another lamp.

pairs.display: But in the end it so turned out that Genji had already been back in the Capital giving as excuse that he could find nothing more written about these achievements of Don Quixote than what has been already set forth.
pairs.genji_half: But in the end it so turned out that Genji had already been back in the Capital
pairs.quijote_half: giving as excuse that he could find nothing more written about these achievements of Don Quixote than what has been already set forth.
pairs.genji_source:
But
in the end it so turned out that Genji had already been back in the
Capital for a year when Iyo’s governorship expired.
pairs.quijote_source:
But it spoils all, that at this point and crisis the author
of the history leaves this battle impending, giving as excuse that he
could find nothing more written about these achievements of Don Quixote
than what has been already set forth.

pairs.display: Somehow or other, in cases of this kind, they told the curate all that had passed between him and them, not omitting what Sancho had been so silent about.
pairs.genji_half: Somehow or other, in cases of this kind,
pairs.quijote_half: they told the curate all that had passed between him and them, not omitting what Sancho had been so silent about.
pairs.genji_source:
Somehow or other, in cases of this kind, Genji could never help
imagining what he himself would feel if he were in the same position.
pairs.quijote_source:
The landlord and landlady immediately came to the conclusion that
the madman was their guest, the balsam man and master of the blanketed
squire, and they told the curate all that had passed between him and
them, not omitting what Sancho had been so silent about.

pairs.display: There was indeed a letter and the gentlewomen had read it, which Don Fernando seized at once and began to read by the light of one of the torches.
pairs.genji_half: There was indeed a letter and the gentlewomen had read it,
pairs.quijote_half: which Don Fernando seized at once and began to read by the light of one of the torches.
pairs.genji_source:
There was indeed a
letter and the gentlewomen had read it, but they very much doubted
whether it was fit for Genji’s eyes and made no offer to produce it.
pairs.quijote_source:
They were all thrown into confusion by Luscinda’s fainting, and as her
mother was unlacing her to give her air a sealed paper was discovered in
her bosom which Don Fernando seized at once and began to read by the
light of one of the torches.

pairs.display: He was brooding now over his loss, so they allowed the others to go on before them, while they themselves followed slowly on foot.
pairs.genji_half: He was brooding now over his loss,
pairs.quijote_half: so they allowed the others to go on before them, while they themselves followed slowly on foot.
pairs.genji_source:
He was brooding now over his loss, when Genji
arrived on a visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
Cardenio lest he
should remind Don Quixote of the quarrel he had with him, and the curate
as there was no necessity for his presence just yet, so they allowed the
others to go on before them, while they themselves followed slowly on
foot.

pairs.display: It was apparent in a moment that Suzaku suffered acutely while these subjects were being discussed, but made her rise and embraced her with great courtesy and politeness, and ordered Sancho to look to Rocinante’s girths, and to arm him without a moment’s delay.
pairs.genji_half: It was apparent in a moment that Suzaku suffered acutely while these subjects were being discussed,
pairs.quijote_half: but made her rise and embraced her with great courtesy and politeness, and ordered Sancho to look to Rocinante’s girths, and to arm him without a moment’s delay.
pairs.genji_source:
It was apparent in a
moment that Suzaku suffered acutely while these subjects were being
discussed, and Genji, ashamed of his unkindness, hastily turned the
conversation to other matters.
pairs.quijote_source:
The distressed damsel strove with much pertinacity to kiss his hands; but
Don Quixote, who was in all things a polished and courteous knight, would
by no means allow it, but made her rise and embraced her with great
courtesy and politeness, and ordered Sancho to look to Rocinante’s
girths, and to arm him without a moment’s delay.

pairs.display: Presently Genji arrived at the Palace to permit her to recover and enjoy it, for which Sancho returned thanks in words that set them all laughing again.
pairs.genji_half: Presently Genji arrived at the Palace
pairs.quijote_half: to permit her to recover and enjoy it, for which Sancho returned thanks in words that set them all laughing again.
pairs.genji_source:
Presently Genji arrived at the Palace and was greatly diverted by the
spectacle of this disorderly and embittered combat.
pairs.quijote_source:
Dorothea
therefore gave her hand, and promised to make him a great lord in her
kingdom, when Heaven should be so good as to permit her to recover and
enjoy it, for which Sancho returned thanks in words that set them all
laughing again.

pairs.display: The spectators now clamoured only for more specimens of Genji’s handiwork, so he rose from the table and going into the room where Zoraida was he took her by the hand, Luscinda, Dorothea, and the Judge’s daughter following her.
pairs.genji_half: The spectators now clamoured only for more specimens of Genji’s handiwork,
pairs.quijote_half: so he rose from the table and going into the room where Zoraida was he took her by the hand, Luscinda, Dorothea, and the Judge’s daughter following her.
pairs.genji_source:
The spectators now clamoured only for more
specimens of Genji’s handiwork, and it would have been impossible at
that moment to interest them in anything else.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate, seeing, then, how well he had
succeeded in carrying out his purpose and the captain’s wishes, had no
desire to keep them unhappy any longer, so he rose from the table and
going into the room where Zoraida was he took her by the hand, Luscinda,
Dorothea, and the Judge’s daughter following her.

pairs.display: It was already nearing the dawn when Genji, feeling somewhat discursive, came out in dismay, and ran to see who was uttering such cries, and those who were outside joined him.
pairs.genji_half: It was already nearing the dawn when Genji, feeling somewhat discursive,
pairs.quijote_half: came out in dismay, and ran to see who was uttering such cries, and those who were outside joined him.
pairs.genji_source:
It was already nearing the dawn when Genji, feeling somewhat
discursive, sent round the great tankard and presently began telling
stories to the company.
pairs.quijote_source:
So loud, in fact, were the shouts of Don Quixote, that the landlord
opening the gate of the inn in all haste, came out in dismay, and ran to
see who was uttering such cries, and those who were outside joined him.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō took the _wagon_, which he played, and soundly kicked by Sancho, was on all fours feeling about for one of the table-knives to take a bloody revenge with.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō took the _wagon_, which he played,
pairs.quijote_half: and soundly kicked by Sancho, was on all fours feeling about for one of the table-knives to take a bloody revenge with.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō took the
_wagon_, which he played, if not so well as Genji, at any rate in
a very distinguished manner.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote, finding
himself free, strove to get on top of the goatherd, who, with his face
covered with blood, and soundly kicked by Sancho, was on all fours
feeling about for one of the table-knives to take a bloody revenge with.

pairs.display: Sochi no Miya took the great-zithern and turning his eyes to the quarter where the sound had been heard, suddenly saw coming down the slope of a hill several men clad in white like penitents.
pairs.genji_half: Sochi no Miya took the great-zithern
pairs.quijote_half: and turning his eyes to the quarter where the sound had been heard, suddenly saw coming down the slope of a hill several men clad in white like penitents.
pairs.genji_source:
Sochi no Miya took the great-zithern and
Genji the _kin_.
pairs.quijote_source:
The goatherd, who was by this time tired of pummelling and
being pummelled, released him at once, and Don Quixote rising to his feet
and turning his eyes to the quarter where the sound had been heard,
suddenly saw coming down the slope of a hill several men clad in white
like penitents.

pairs.display: Genji had a strong presentiment the Court ceremony fell in with his host’s humour, the banquet came to an end, they took their afternoon sleep, Sancho returned, and their conversation was resumed.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had a strong presentiment the Court ceremony
pairs.quijote_half: fell in with his host’s humour, the banquet came to an end, they took their afternoon sleep, Sancho returned, and their conversation was resumed.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had a strong presentiment the Court ceremony and festivals of the
reign were destined to be taken as a model in future times.
pairs.quijote_source:
The bachelor accepted the invitation and remained, a couple of young
pigeons were added to the ordinary fare, at dinner they talked chivalry,
Carrasco fell in with his host’s humour, the banquet came to an end, they
took their afternoon sleep, Sancho returned, and their conversation was
resumed.

pairs.display: What part could she hope to play save the humiliating one and thus they bade each other farewell, and Sancho went away to make the necessary preparations for their expedition.
pairs.genji_half: What part could she hope to play save the humiliating one
pairs.quijote_half: and thus they bade each other farewell, and Sancho went away to make the necessary preparations for their expedition.
pairs.genji_source:
What part could she hope to play save the humiliating one of a foil
to the young princess who was Genji’s lawful bride?
pairs.quijote_source:
Carrasco promised all, and then took
his leave, charging Don Quixote to inform him of his good or evil
fortunes whenever he had an opportunity; and thus they bade each other
farewell, and Sancho went away to make the necessary preparations for
their expedition.

pairs.display: Little as she trusted Genji, she must not cut off her sturdily built man, but not very tall in stature.
pairs.genji_half: Little as she trusted Genji, she must not cut off her
pairs.quijote_half: sturdily built man, but not very tall in stature.
pairs.genji_source:
Little as she trusted Genji, she must not cut off her child from all
possibility of an ultimate transference to the Capital.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote examined his adversary, and
found that he already had his helmet on and visor lowered, so that he
could not see his face; he observed, however, that he was a sturdily
built man, but not very tall in stature.

pairs.display: And since the spring they have been building this new hermitage and his squire parted from Don Quixote and Sancho, he meaning to go look for some village where he could plaster and strap his ribs.
pairs.genji_half: And since the spring they have been building this new hermitage
pairs.quijote_half: and his squire parted from Don Quixote and Sancho, he meaning to go look for some village where he could plaster and strap his ribs.
pairs.genji_source:
And since the spring they have been building
this new hermitage for Prince Genji close by, and this has changed the
whole character of the district.
pairs.quijote_source:
In fine, both
master and man remained under the delusion; and, down in the mouth, and
out of luck, he of the Mirrors and his squire parted from Don Quixote and
Sancho, he meaning to go look for some village where he could plaster and
strap his ribs.

pairs.display: He noted that the old priest began to run all over his face and beard, whereat he was so startled that he cried out to Sancho:
pairs.genji_half: He noted that the old priest
pairs.quijote_half: began to run all over his face and beard, whereat he was so startled that he cried out to Sancho:
pairs.genji_source:
He noted that the old priest was evidently
on friendly terms with Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote took it, and without
perceiving what was in it thrust it down in hot haste upon his head; but
as the curds were pressed and squeezed the whey began to run all over his
face and beard, whereat he was so startled that he cried out to Sancho:

pairs.display: A member of Genji’s personal servants now arrived but it seems Sancho’s bad luck so ordered it that the hermit was not at home, for so a sub-hermit they found in the hermitage told them.
pairs.genji_half: A member of Genji’s personal servants now arrived
pairs.quijote_half: but it seems Sancho’s bad luck so ordered it that the hermit was not at home, for so a sub-hermit they found in the hermitage told them.
pairs.genji_source:
A member of Genji’s personal servants now arrived at Akashi to assist
the family in their removal.
pairs.quijote_source:
The instant Sancho heard
this he steered his Dapple towards it, and Don Quixote and the cousin did
the same; but it seems Sancho’s bad luck so ordered it that the hermit
was not at home, for so a sub-hermit they found in the hermitage told
them.

pairs.display: At last she took out the zithern not that he was sufficiently in his wits to guide the beast, but Dapple followed the footsteps of Rocinante, from whom he could not remain a moment separated.
pairs.genji_half: At last she took out the zithern
pairs.quijote_half: not that he was sufficiently in his wits to guide the beast, but Dapple followed the footsteps of Rocinante, from whom he could not remain a moment separated.
pairs.genji_source:
At last she took out the zithern which Genji
had given to her at Akashi.
pairs.quijote_source:
They put up Sancho,
scarcely restored to his senses, on his ass, and let him go after his
master; not that he was sufficiently in his wits to guide the beast, but
Dapple followed the footsteps of Rocinante, from whom he could not remain
a moment separated.

pairs.display: While things were going thus dismally they pursued their journey in quest of the banks of the famous Ebro, where that befell them which will be told in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_half: While things were going thus dismally
pairs.quijote_half: they pursued their journey in quest of the banks of the famous Ebro, where that befell them which will be told in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_source:
While things were going thus dismally at Ōi, Genji was feeling very
uneasy.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote passed it in his never-failing meditations; but, for
all that, they had some winks of sleep, and with the appearance of
daylight they pursued their journey in quest of the banks of the famous
Ebro, where that befell them which will be told in the following chapter.

pairs.display: He had himself formed some slight acquaintance with her during the period of Genji’s exile and was grieved to the heart, for he thought he had got a patrimonial estate in that suit.
pairs.genji_half: He had himself formed some slight acquaintance with her during the period of Genji’s exile
pairs.quijote_half: and was grieved to the heart, for he thought he had got a patrimonial estate in that suit.
pairs.genji_source:
He had himself formed some slight acquaintance
with her during the period of Genji’s exile and wished to discover
whether she still had a liking for him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote went over and unhooked Sancho, who, as soon
as he found himself on the ground, looked at the rent in his huntingcoat
and was grieved to the heart, for he thought he had got a patrimonial
estate in that suit.

pairs.display: But while he was speaking the Lady of Akashi recalled the stories of Genji’s adventurous past, bringing the one Sancho sent to Don Quixote, and this, too, was read out, and it raised some doubts as to the governor’s simplicity.
pairs.genji_half: But while he was speaking the Lady of Akashi recalled the stories of Genji’s adventurous past,
pairs.quijote_half: bringing the one Sancho sent to Don Quixote, and this, too, was read out, and it raised some doubts as to the governor’s simplicity.
pairs.genji_source:
But while he was speaking the Lady
of Akashi recalled the stories of Genji’s adventurous past, and of
numerous other attachments with which rumour credited him.
pairs.quijote_source:
The letters were applauded, laughed over, relished, and admired; and
then, as if to put the seal to the business, the courier arrived,
bringing the one Sancho sent to Don Quixote, and this, too, was read out,
and it raised some doubts as to the governor’s simplicity.

pairs.display: All the time they were driving to the Capital Genji was haunted and when he found him leave off with so few, he thanked heaven in his heart.
pairs.genji_half: All the time they were driving to the Capital Genji was haunted
pairs.quijote_half: and when he found him leave off with so few, he thanked heaven in his heart.
pairs.genji_source:
All the time they were driving
to the Capital Genji was haunted by the image of the sorrow-stricken
figure that had watched their departure.
pairs.quijote_source:
Here Sancho brought his long speech to an end, Don Quixote having been
the whole time in dread of his uttering a host of absurdities; and when
he found him leave off with so few, he thanked heaven in his heart.

pairs.display: But news had reached them of the pains which Genji was bestowing upon its upbringing, but if he should be vanquished his opponent was released from the promise that was claimed against him and from all obligations to give satisfaction.
pairs.genji_half: But news had reached them of the pains which Genji was bestowing upon its upbringing,
pairs.quijote_half: but if he should be vanquished his opponent was released from the promise that was claimed against him and from all obligations to give satisfaction.
pairs.genji_source:
But news had reached them of the pains which Genji was bestowing upon
its upbringing, and she felt no doubt that she had advised for the best.
pairs.quijote_source:
The conditions of the combat were that if Don Quixote proved
the victor his antagonist was to marry the daughter of Dona Rodriguez;
but if he should be vanquished his opponent was released from the promise
that was claimed against him and from all obligations to give
satisfaction.

pairs.display: Thus everything seemed to be working very smoothly, and by the side of this, having turned Dapple and Rocinante loose without headstall or bridle, the forlorn pair, master and man, seated themselves.
pairs.genji_half: Thus everything seemed to be working very smoothly,
pairs.quijote_half: and by the side of this, having turned Dapple and Rocinante loose without headstall or bridle, the forlorn pair, master and man, seated themselves.
pairs.genji_source:
Thus everything seemed to be working very
smoothly, and Genji felt that the arrangement was going to be a great
success.
pairs.quijote_source:
A clear limpid spring which they discovered in a cool grove relieved Don
Quixote and Sancho of the dust and fatigue due to the unpolite behaviour
of the bulls, and by the side of this, having turned Dapple and Rocinante
loose without headstall or bridle, the forlorn pair, master and man,
seated themselves.

pairs.display: During the first weeks of mourning Genji spent all his time at the Palace he thought it best therefore to fold his arms and bow his head and reserve himself for a more favourable occasion and opportunity.
pairs.genji_half: During the first weeks of mourning Genji spent all his time at the Palace
pairs.quijote_half: he thought it best therefore to fold his arms and bow his head and reserve himself for a more favourable occasion and opportunity.
pairs.genji_source:
During the first weeks of
mourning Genji spent all his time at the Palace and paid an early
visit to the Emperor every day.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote was on foot with his horse
unbridled and his lance leaning against a tree, and in short completely
defenceless; he thought it best therefore to fold his arms and bow his
head and reserve himself for a more favourable occasion and opportunity.

pairs.display: Thus Genji managed, by arguments which for fear of wearying you I will not repeat, and Sancho (to whom he presented the ten crowns he had promised but had not until then given), left them with many expressions of good-will on both sides.
pairs.genji_half: Thus Genji managed, by arguments which for fear of wearying you I will not repeat,
pairs.quijote_half: and Sancho (to whom he presented the ten crowns he had promised but had not until then given), left them with many expressions of good-will on both sides.
pairs.genji_source:
Thus Genji managed, by arguments which for fear of wearying you I will
not repeat, to coax the Emperor into a slightly less desperate state of
mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
They reached the strand on Saint John’s Eve during
the night; and Roque, after embracing Don Quixote and Sancho (to whom he
presented the ten crowns he had promised but had not until then given),
left them with many expressions of good-will on both sides.

pairs.display: For years past it had struck the Emperor, and Sancho, and the instant they came to the shore all the galleys struck their awnings and the clarions rang out.
pairs.genji_half: For years past it had struck the Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: and Sancho, and the instant they came to the shore all the galleys struck their awnings and the clarions rang out.
pairs.genji_source:
For years past it had struck the Emperor, on looking at himself
in the mirror, that he was extraordinarily like Prince Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
The commandant
had been already made aware of his good fortune in seeing two such famous
persons as Don Quixote and Sancho, and the instant they came to the shore
all the galleys struck their awnings and the clarions rang out.

pairs.display: But to Genji, he supposed, the duchess asked him if Altisidora was in his good graces.
pairs.genji_half: But to Genji, he supposed,
pairs.quijote_half: the duchess asked him if Altisidora was in his good graces.
pairs.genji_source:
But to Genji, he supposed, the whole matter
must be a very painful one.
pairs.quijote_source:
They gave it very readily, and the duchess asked him if
Altisidora was in his good graces.

pairs.display: But he longed, without actually letting out that he knew turrets, portcullis, and drawbridge; for ever since he had been vanquished he talked more rationally about everything, as will be shown presently.
pairs.genji_half: But he longed, without actually letting out that he knew
pairs.quijote_half: turrets, portcullis, and drawbridge; for ever since he had been vanquished he talked more rationally about everything, as will be shown presently.
pairs.genji_source:
But he longed, without actually
letting out that he knew, to get some further information from Genji
himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
They dismounted at a hostelry which Don Quixote recognised as such
and did not take to be a castle with moat, turrets, portcullis, and
drawbridge; for ever since he had been vanquished he talked more
rationally about everything, as will be shown presently.

pairs.display: She assumed in the household the place of a favourite elder daughter, the one to finish off his task of scourging in the open country, the other to see it accomplished, for therein lay the accomplishment of his wishes.
pairs.genji_half: She assumed in the household the place of a favourite elder daughter,
pairs.quijote_half: the one to finish off his task of scourging in the open country, the other to see it accomplished, for therein lay the accomplishment of his wishes.
pairs.genji_source:
She assumed in the household the place of a favourite elder
daughter, and it was entirely in this spirit that Genji entertained
and amused her.
pairs.quijote_source:
All that day Don Quixote and Sancho remained in the village and inn
waiting for night, the one to finish off his task of scourging in the
open country, the other to see it accomplished, for therein lay the
accomplishment of his wishes.

pairs.display: So the old poet wished, and surely Prince Genji must be the answer to his prayer, and proceeded to Don Quixote’s house, at the door of which they found his housekeeper and niece, whom the news of his arrival had already reached.
pairs.genji_half: So the old poet wished, and surely Prince Genji must be the answer to his prayer,
pairs.quijote_half: and proceeded to Don Quixote’s house, at the door of which they found his housekeeper and niece, whom the news of his arrival had already reached.
pairs.genji_source:
So the old poet
wished, and surely Prince Genji must be the answer to his prayer, for
it seems that in him every perfection is combined.’
pairs.quijote_source:
So at length, with the boys capering round them, and accompanied by the
curate and the bachelor, they made their entrance into the town, and
proceeded to Don Quixote’s house, at the door of which they found his
housekeeper and niece, whom the news of his arrival had already reached.

pairs.display: Genji had promised in due course to marry the child to the Heir Apparent, without a tail and with buttocks as bare as felt, but not vicious-looking.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had promised in due course to marry the child to the Heir Apparent,
pairs.quijote_half: without a tail and with buttocks as bare as felt, but not vicious-looking.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had promised in due course to marry the child to the Heir
        Apparent, son of the Emperor Ryōzen.
pairs.quijote_source:
Master Pedro now came back, and in a cart followed the show and the
ape--a big one, without a tail and with buttocks as bare as felt, but not
vicious-looking.

pairs.display: Quoting a poem of Uyeno Mine-o’s upon the death of Fujiwara no Mototsune, Your servant, TERESA PANZA.
pairs.genji_half: Quoting a poem of Uyeno Mine-o’s upon the death of Fujiwara no Mototsune,
pairs.quijote_half: Your servant, TERESA PANZA.
pairs.genji_source:
Quoting a poem of Uyeno Mine-o’s upon the death of Fujiwara no
        Mototsune, 891 A.D.

    That Ryōzen was in reality Genji’s son.
pairs.quijote_source:
She who would rather see your ladyship than write to you,

Your servant,

TERESA PANZA.

pairs.display: After his return from this unsuccessful expedition, Genji felt particularly good and superior to those of Tronchon.
pairs.genji_half: After his return from this unsuccessful expedition, Genji felt
pairs.quijote_half: particularly good and superior to those of Tronchon.
pairs.genji_source:
After his return from this unsuccessful expedition, Genji felt in no
mood for sleep, and soon he jumped up and threw open his casement.
pairs.quijote_source:
He gave her the acorns, and also a cheese which Teresa had
given him as being particularly good and superior to those of Tronchon.

pairs.display: For years past she had held, beyond challenge or doubt, and laughed not a little, thinking it, as all did, the strangest form of madness a crazy intellect could be capable of.
pairs.genji_half: For years past she had held, beyond challenge or doubt,
pairs.quijote_half: and laughed not a little, thinking it, as all did, the strangest form of madness a crazy intellect could be capable of.
pairs.genji_source:
For years past she had held, beyond
challenge or doubt, the first place in Genji’s affections—had been the
centre of all his plans and contrivings.
pairs.quijote_source:
He described to them also nearly all the adventures that Sancho
had mentioned, at which they marvelled and laughed not a little, thinking
it, as all did, the strangest form of madness a crazy intellect could be
capable of.

pairs.display: To Princess Nyogo, too, Genji sent good wishes fearing it would not be believed, because here Don Quixote’s madness reaches the confines of the greatest that can be conceived, and even goes a couple of bowshots beyond the greatest.
pairs.genji_half: To Princess Nyogo, too, Genji sent good wishes
pairs.quijote_half: fearing it would not be believed, because here Don Quixote’s madness reaches the confines of the greatest that can be conceived, and even goes a couple of bowshots beyond the greatest.
pairs.genji_source:
To Princess Nyogo, too, Genji sent good wishes on the occasion of her
coming out of mourning.
pairs.quijote_source:
When the author of this great history comes to relate what is set down in
this chapter he says he would have preferred to pass it over in silence,
fearing it would not be believed, because here Don Quixote’s madness
reaches the confines of the greatest that can be conceived, and even goes
a couple of bowshots beyond the greatest.

pairs.display: It was high time to be thinking about the Initiation of Yūgiri, and, with a lad carrying a candle-end, and the two damsels already mentioned, he returned to where Don Quixote stood, and bade him kneel down.
pairs.genji_half: It was high time to be thinking about the Initiation of Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: and, with a lad carrying a candle-end, and the two damsels already mentioned, he returned to where Don Quixote stood, and bade him kneel down.
pairs.genji_source:
It was high time to be thinking about the Initiation of Yūgiri, Aoi’s
son, who was now twelve years old.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thus warned and menaced, the castellan forthwith brought out a book in
which he used to enter the straw and barley he served out to the
carriers, and, with a lad carrying a candle-end, and the two damsels
already mentioned, he returned to where Don Quixote stood, and bade him
kneel down.

pairs.display: It would in many ways have been better all stained with the rust of his armour; his collar was a falling one of scholastic cut, without starch or lace, his buskins buff-coloured, and his shoes polished.
pairs.genji_half: It would in many ways have been better
pairs.quijote_half: all stained with the rust of his armour; his collar was a falling one of scholastic cut, without starch or lace, his buskins buff-coloured, and his shoes polished.
pairs.genji_source:
It would in many ways have been
better that the ceremony should be performed in Genji’s palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
They led Don Quixote into a room, and Sancho removed his armour, leaving
him in loose Walloon breeches and chamois-leather doublet, all stained
with the rust of his armour; his collar was a falling one of scholastic
cut, without starch or lace, his buskins buff-coloured, and his shoes
polished.

pairs.display: When the time came for his Examinations, with which Don Fernando and his companions were so delighted that they wished the story had been longer; so charmingly did Dorothea describe her misadventures.
pairs.genji_half: When the time came for his Examinations,
pairs.quijote_half: with which Don Fernando and his companions were so delighted that they wished the story had been longer; so charmingly did Dorothea describe her misadventures.
pairs.genji_source:
When the
time came for his Examinations, Genji determined to put him to the test
privately a little while beforehand.
pairs.quijote_source:
He then asked Dorothea how she had managed to reach a place so far
removed from her own home, and she in a few fitting words told all that
she had previously related to Cardenio, with which Don Fernando and his
companions were so delighted that they wished the story had been longer;
so charmingly did Dorothea describe her misadventures.

pairs.display: At the time when Genji first came across him the pack-saddle remained a caparison till the day of judgment, and the basin a helmet and the inn a castle in Don Quixote’s imagination.
pairs.genji_half: At the time when Genji first came across him
pairs.quijote_half: the pack-saddle remained a caparison till the day of judgment, and the basin a helmet and the inn a castle in Don Quixote’s imagination.
pairs.genji_source:
At the time when Genji first
came across him he was without patronage or any means of subsistence.
pairs.quijote_source:
The landlord
alone insisted upon it that they must punish the insolence of this
madman, who at every turn raised a disturbance in the inn; but at length
the uproar was stilled for the present; the pack-saddle remained a
caparison till the day of judgment, and the basin a helmet and the inn a
castle in Don Quixote’s imagination.

pairs.display: About this time Tō no Chūjō became Palace Minister but Don Quixote’s memory (though he had an excellent one) only carried away those that have been just quoted.
pairs.genji_half: About this time Tō no Chūjō became Palace Minister
pairs.quijote_half: but Don Quixote’s memory (though he had an excellent one) only carried away those that have been just quoted.
pairs.genji_source:
About this time Tō no Chūjō became Palace Minister and Genji began to
hand over to him most of the business of state.
pairs.quijote_source:
In the same manner all the characters of the two bands advanced and
retired, and each executed its figures, and delivered its verses, some of
them graceful, some burlesque, but Don Quixote’s memory (though he had an
excellent one) only carried away those that have been just quoted.

pairs.display: To have given my cousin Genji a daughter when he had waited from the end of his back-bone up to the nape of his neck, he was so sore that it nearly drove him out of his senses.
pairs.genji_half: To have given my cousin Genji a daughter when he had waited
pairs.quijote_half: from the end of his back-bone up to the nape of his neck, he was so sore that it nearly drove him out of his senses.
pairs.genji_source:
To have given my cousin Genji
a daughter when he had waited for one so long was a singular stroke
of good fortune.
pairs.quijote_source:
Every now and then Sancho gave vent to deep sighs and dismal groans, and
on Don Quixote asking him what caused such acute suffering, he replied
that, from the end of his back-bone up to the nape of his neck, he was so
sore that it nearly drove him out of his senses.

pairs.display: It was evident, from what she went on to say, nor were Don Quixote’s knees altogether under control, for he too shook a little, squeezed his shoulders together and lost colour.
pairs.genji_half: It was evident, from what she went on to say,
pairs.quijote_half: nor were Don Quixote’s knees altogether under control, for he too shook a little, squeezed his shoulders together and lost colour.
pairs.genji_source:
It was evident, from what she went on to say, that she felt very
indignant at Genji’s conduct in the matter.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho thought heaven was coming off
its hinges and going to fall on his head, and full of terror he ducked it
and buried it between his knees; nor were Don Quixote’s knees altogether
under control, for he too shook a little, squeezed his shoulders together
and lost colour.

pairs.display: Yūgiri thinking that such an evening was wasted if not spent in agreeable company, as has been described in the First Part of this great as well as accurate history, in the last chapter thereof.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri thinking that such an evening was wasted if not spent in agreeable company,
pairs.quijote_half: as has been described in the First Part of this great as well as accurate history, in the last chapter thereof.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri thinking that
such an evening was wasted if not spent in agreeable company, had come
over from Genji’s palace to the Great Hall.
pairs.quijote_source:
This gave great satisfaction to the curate and
the barber, for they concluded they had taken the right course in
carrying him off enchanted on the ox-cart, as has been described in the
First Part of this great as well as accurate history, in the last chapter
thereof.

pairs.display: The Emperor Ryōzen was extremely loth to part with her and then charged the host to take great care of his horse, as he was the best bit of flesh that ever ate bread in this world.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor Ryōzen was extremely loth to part with her
pairs.quijote_half: and then charged the host to take great care of his horse, as he was the best bit of flesh that ever ate bread in this world.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor Ryōzen was extremely loth to part with her
and at first refused.
pairs.quijote_source:
So saying, he advanced to hold the stirrup for Don Quixote, who
got down with great difficulty and exertion (for he had not broken his
fast all day), and then charged the host to take great care of his horse,
as he was the best bit of flesh that ever ate bread in this world.

pairs.display: But pretty as they all were, none of the others was handsome insomuch that he set him down as a monster of some kind, or a human being of some new species or unearthly breed.
pairs.genji_half: But pretty as they all were, none of the others was handsome
pairs.quijote_half: insomuch that he set him down as a monster of some kind, or a human being of some new species or unearthly breed.
pairs.genji_source:
But
pretty as they all were, none of the others was handsome to anything
like the same degree as the girl from Genji’s household.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this moment the extraordinary nose of the squire presented itself to
Don Quixote’s view, and he was no less amazed than Sancho at the sight;
insomuch that he set him down as a monster of some kind, or a human being
of some new species or unearthly breed.

pairs.display: The girl had a brother who was a page at Court coming forward in defence of their cause; and they exalted his wisdom to the same level with his courage, rating him as a Cid in arms, and a Cicero in eloquence.
pairs.genji_half: The girl had a brother who was a page at Court
pairs.quijote_half: coming forward in defence of their cause; and they exalted his wisdom to the same level with his courage, rating him as a Cid in arms, and a Cicero in eloquence.
pairs.genji_source:
The girl had a brother who was a page at Court and
had also often waited upon Yūgiri at Genji’s palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
Many and great were the attentions shown to Don Quixote by the newly
married couple, who felt themselves under an obligation to him for coming
forward in defence of their cause; and they exalted his wisdom to the
same level with his courage, rating him as a Cid in arms, and a Cicero in
eloquence.

pairs.display: The procession left the house amid great scenes of popular enthusiasm, and put the bundle of armour on one side of him and the ass’s pack-saddle on the other, in fear and trembling as great as Don Quixote’s perturbation.
pairs.genji_half: The procession left the house amid great scenes of popular enthusiasm,
pairs.quijote_half: and put the bundle of armour on one side of him and the ass’s pack-saddle on the other, in fear and trembling as great as Don Quixote’s perturbation.
pairs.genji_source:
The procession left the house amid great
scenes of popular enthusiasm, which painfully reminded the old lady
of her complete failure to injure Prince Genji’s career.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote stood up and laid
his hand upon his sword, and Sancho ensconced himself under Dapple and
put the bundle of armour on one side of him and the ass’s pack-saddle on
the other, in fear and trembling as great as Don Quixote’s perturbation.

pairs.display: But Genji was careful not to let it seem that any of these knights used to lie sleepless supported by the memory of their mistresses.
pairs.genji_half: But Genji was careful not to let it seem that any of these
pairs.quijote_half: knights used to lie sleepless supported by the memory of their mistresses.
pairs.genji_source:
But Genji was careful not to let it seem that any of these restrictions
had been carried out to the detriment of one lady rather than another.
pairs.quijote_source:
All that night Don Quixote lay awake thinking of his lady
Dulcinea, in order to conform to what he had read in his books, how many
a night in the forests and deserts knights used to lie sleepless
supported by the memory of their mistresses.

pairs.display: The gentlewomen and maids found their quarters in the new house admirably fitted out with every comfort neat, plentiful, and tasty; but what pleased Don Quixote most was the marvellous silence that reigned throughout the house, for it was like a Carthusian monastery.
pairs.genji_half: The gentlewomen and maids found their quarters in the new house admirably fitted out with every comfort
pairs.quijote_half: neat, plentiful, and tasty; but what pleased Don Quixote most was the marvellous silence that reigned throughout the house, for it was like a Carthusian monastery.
pairs.genji_source:
The gentlewomen and maids found their
quarters in the new house admirably fitted out with every comfort and
convenience, and they were louder than ever in Genji’s praises.
pairs.quijote_source:
They went in to dinner, and the repast was such as Don Diego said on the
road he was in the habit of giving to his guests, neat, plentiful, and
tasty; but what pleased Don Quixote most was the marvellous silence that
reigned throughout the house, for it was like a Carthusian monastery.

pairs.display: Allusion to the death of the old Emperor, as he had a great desire to enter it and see with his own eyes if the wonderful tales that were told of it all over the country were true.
pairs.genji_half: Allusion to the death of the old Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: as he had a great desire to enter it and see with his own eyes if the wonderful tales that were told of it all over the country were true.
pairs.genji_source:
Allusion to the death of the old Emperor, Genji’s and Suzaku’s
        father.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote begged the
fencing licentiate to find him a guide to show him the way to the cave of
Montesinos, as he had a great desire to enter it and see with his own
eyes if the wonderful tales that were told of it all over the country
were true.

pairs.display: Though seventeen years had now passed since Yūgao’s death, the head had some strange magical mystery in it.
pairs.genji_half: Though seventeen years had now passed since Yūgao’s death,
pairs.quijote_half: the head had some strange magical mystery in it.
pairs.genji_source:
Though seventeen years had now passed since Yūgao’s death,
Genji had not by any means forgotten her.
pairs.quijote_source:
This Cide Hamete Benengeli thought fit to reveal at once, not to
keep the world in suspense, fancying that the head had some strange
magical mystery in it.

pairs.display: Then came the exile, and with Genji’s other servants Don Quixote had kept it, and had never given it to him, nor had he himself thought of asking for it.
pairs.genji_half: Then came the exile, and with Genji’s other servants
pairs.quijote_half: Don Quixote had kept it, and had never given it to him, nor had he himself thought of asking for it.
pairs.genji_source:
Then
came the exile, and with Genji’s other servants she went across to the
western wing and entered Murasaki’s service.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho put his hand into his bosom in search of the note-book
but could not find it, nor, if he had been searching until now, could he
have found it, for Don Quixote had kept it, and had never given it to
him, nor had he himself thought of asking for it.

pairs.display: But the nurse, who knew nothing, it must be remembered, of Genji’s connection with Yūgao dismounting from Rocinante, hastened to support him, and took him in his arms, and found he had not yet ceased to breathe.
pairs.genji_half: But the nurse, who knew nothing, it must be remembered, of Genji’s connection with Yūgao
pairs.quijote_half: dismounting from Rocinante, hastened to support him, and took him in his arms, and found he had not yet ceased to breathe.
pairs.genji_source:
But the nurse, who knew
nothing, it must be remembered, of Genji’s connection with Yūgao nor of
any reason why he should interest himself in Tamakatsura, was somewhat
disconcerted.
pairs.quijote_source:
His friends at once ran to his aid, filled with grief at his misery and
sad fate, and Don Quixote, dismounting from Rocinante, hastened to
support him, and took him in his arms, and found he had not yet ceased to
breathe.

pairs.display: Unless she told the nurse of Genji’s connection with Yūgao, eager to ascertain who this Knight of the White Moon was who had left Don Quixote in such a sad plight.
pairs.genji_half: Unless she told the nurse of Genji’s connection with Yūgao,
pairs.quijote_half: eager to ascertain who this Knight of the White Moon was who had left Don Quixote in such a sad plight.
pairs.genji_source:
Unless she told the nurse
of Genji’s connection with Yūgao, further conversation would be
impossible.
pairs.quijote_source:
In the end they carried him into the
city in a hand-chair which the viceroy sent for, and thither the viceroy
himself returned, eager to ascertain who this Knight of the White Moon
was who had left Don Quixote in such a sad plight.

pairs.display: They realized that Genji had only said this in order to please as he meant to confer upon her some portion of the honour he acquired by the might of his arm.
pairs.genji_half: They realized that Genji had only said this in order to please
pairs.quijote_half: as he meant to confer upon her some portion of the honour he acquired by the might of his arm.
pairs.genji_source:
They realized that Genji
had only said this in order to please and flatter the old lady.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote asked her
name in order that he might from that time forward know to whom he was
beholden for the favour he had received, as he meant to confer upon her
some portion of the honour he acquired by the might of his arm.

pairs.display: It was written in rather faint ink and if Cardenio and the curate had not dragged him off, he would have brought the war of the giant to an end.
pairs.genji_half: It was written in rather faint ink and
pairs.quijote_half: if Cardenio and the curate had not dragged him off, he would have brought the war of the giant to an end.
pairs.genji_source:
It was written in rather faint
ink and Genji, as he eagerly scanned it, thought the hand lacking in
force and decision.
pairs.quijote_source:
On seeing this the landlord was so enraged that he fell on Don
Quixote, and with his clenched fist began to pummel him in such a way,
that if Cardenio and the curate had not dragged him off, he would have
brought the war of the giant to an end.

pairs.display: The move to Genji’s Palace and shrewd sayings of Don Quixote and the humours of his squire Sancho Panza could not help giving general pleasure to all the world.
pairs.genji_half: The move to Genji’s Palace
pairs.quijote_half: and shrewd sayings of Don Quixote and the humours of his squire Sancho Panza could not help giving general pleasure to all the world.
pairs.genji_source:
The move to Genji’s
Palace took place in the tenth month.
pairs.quijote_source:
He wished, he said, his
enemies the Cadells could be deprived of this pleasure; but that was
impossible, because the crazes and shrewd sayings of Don Quixote and the
humours of his squire Sancho Panza could not help giving general pleasure
to all the world.

pairs.display: Murasaki seemed scarcely to have glanced at it; but all the while, began to blubber and shed tears.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki seemed scarcely to have glanced at it; but all the while,
pairs.quijote_half: began to blubber and shed tears.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki seemed scarcely to have glanced at it; but all the while,
true to Genji’s surmise, she was guessing the meaning of this choice.
pairs.quijote_source:
The bachelor went for the notary and returned shortly afterwards
with him and with Sancho, who, having already learned from the bachelor
the condition his master was in, and finding the housekeeper and niece
weeping, began to blubber and shed tears.

pairs.display: That is just what I long for.’ Genji’s own servants and the landlord, in hope of better payment, served them up a tolerably good dinner.
pairs.genji_half: That is just what I long for.’ Genji’s own servants
pairs.quijote_half: and the landlord, in hope of better payment, served them up a tolerably good dinner.
pairs.genji_source:
That is just what I long for.’

Genji’s own servants and retainers had been told nothing save that a
strange lady was shortly to arrive.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate made them get ready such fare as there
was in the inn, and the landlord, in hope of better payment, served them
up a tolerably good dinner.

pairs.display: On their arrival they found he concluded that one who wore such garments could not be of very low rank.
pairs.genji_half: On their arrival they found
pairs.quijote_half: he concluded that one who wore such garments could not be of very low rank.
pairs.genji_source:
On their arrival
they found their quarters stacked with all sorts of presents from
Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
His garb was
what has been described, save that as he drew near, Don Quixote perceived
that a tattered doublet which he wore was amber-tanned, from which he
concluded that one who wore such garments could not be of very low rank.

pairs.display: Genji laughed afresh each time he read it and at a leisurely pace they proceeded to take shelter in a grove which was in sight about a quarter of a league off.
pairs.genji_half: Genji laughed afresh each time he read it
pairs.quijote_half: and at a leisurely pace they proceeded to take shelter in a grove which was in sight about a quarter of a league off.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji laughed afresh each time he read it and finally, seeing that
Murasaki was regarding him with astonishment, he handed her the
missive.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho was by this time mounted with the help of Don Quixote, who then
himself mounted Rocinante, and at a leisurely pace they proceeded to take
shelter in a grove which was in sight about a quarter of a league off.

pairs.display: There was a concert as they were taking away the cloth there came a courier with a letter from Don Quixote for the governor.
pairs.genji_half: There was a concert
pairs.quijote_half: as they were taking away the cloth there came a courier with a letter from Don Quixote for the governor.
pairs.genji_source:
There was a concert and on Genji’s part a grand distribution of
trinkets and New Year presents.
pairs.quijote_source:
It came to pass, then, that after he had dined that day, in opposition to
the rules and aphorisms of Doctor Tirteafuera, as they were taking away
the cloth there came a courier with a letter from Don Quixote for the
governor.

pairs.display: Her brother evidently felt the cold even more than she did, and if they were amazed by his absurdities they were equally amazed by the elegant style in which he delivered them.
pairs.genji_half: Her brother evidently felt the cold even more than she did,
pairs.quijote_half: and if they were amazed by his absurdities they were equally amazed by the elegant style in which he delivered them.
pairs.genji_source:
Her brother evidently felt the cold even more than she did,
and Genji imagined him with a very red nose indeed.
pairs.quijote_source:
Exceedingly great was the amusement the two gentlemen derived from
hearing Don Quixote recount the strange incidents of his history; and if
they were amazed by his absurdities they were equally amazed by the
elegant style in which he delivered them.

pairs.display: Unlike most occupants of the exalted position which he now held, he wandered from side to side looking for it.
pairs.genji_half: Unlike most occupants of the exalted position which he now held,
pairs.quijote_half: he wandered from side to side looking for it.
pairs.genji_source:
Unlike
most occupants of the exalted position which he now held, Genji was
entirely devoid of pomposity and self-importance.
pairs.quijote_source:
Two days later Don Quixote got up, and the first thing he did
was to go and look at his books, and not finding the room where he had
left it, he wandered from side to side looking for it.

pairs.display: After performing in the Imperial Palace the dancers were to visit the Suzaku-in he paying them so much a day.
pairs.genji_half: After performing in the Imperial Palace the dancers were to visit the Suzaku-in
pairs.quijote_half: he paying them so much a day.
pairs.genji_source:
After performing
in the Imperial Palace the dancers were to visit the Suzaku-in and
then come on to Genji’s.
pairs.quijote_source:
In the meantime the curate had made an
arrangement with the officers that they should bear them company as far
as his village, he paying them so much a day.

pairs.display: There were flute-players in abundance, and nowhere that night did they give a more admirable display for they all thought it was no man but a devil from hell come to carry away the dead body they had in the litter.
pairs.genji_half: There were flute-players in abundance, and nowhere that night did they give a more admirable display
pairs.quijote_half: for they all thought it was no man but a devil from hell come to carry away the dead body they had in the litter.
pairs.genji_source:
There were flute-players in abundance, and nowhere that
night did they give a more admirable display than when they welcomed
the arrival of the mummers in front of Genji’s palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
The mourners, too, enveloped and
swathed in their skirts and gowns, were unable to bestir themselves, and
so with entire safety to himself Don Quixote belaboured them all and
drove them off against their will, for they all thought it was no man but
a devil from hell come to carry away the dead body they had in the
litter.

pairs.display: But on the contrary Genji insisted upon its being for he had it almost by heart, and it could be taken down from him wherever and whenever they liked.
pairs.genji_half: But on the contrary Genji insisted upon its being
pairs.quijote_half: for he had it almost by heart, and it could be taken down from him wherever and whenever they liked.
pairs.genji_source:
But on the contrary Genji insisted upon its being
carried out with even more than the prescribed elaboration.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho comforted himself with this, and said if that were so the loss of
Dulcinea’s letter did not trouble him much, for he had it almost by
heart, and it could be taken down from him wherever and whenever they
liked.

pairs.display: It would have been hard in these days to find squire upon this earthly ball So plain and simple, or of guile so free.
pairs.genji_half: It would have been hard in these days to find
pairs.quijote_half: squire upon this earthly ball So plain and simple, or of guile so free.
pairs.genji_source:
It would have been hard in these days to find a mote in the perfect
sunshine of Genji’s prosperity and contentment.
pairs.quijote_source:
As mettled steeds compared with Rocinante,
The reputation they have won is scanty.

BURLADOR, ACADEMICIAN OF ARGAMASILLA,
ON SANCHO PANZA

SONNET

  The worthy Sancho Panza here you see;
    A great soul once was in that body small,
    Nor was there squire upon this earthly ball
So plain and simple, or of guile so free.

pairs.display: Till now, however, Genji had deliberately pretended and the others driving Sancho and Dapple before them, and all maintaining a strange silence, followed in the steps of the one who led Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_half: Till now, however, Genji had deliberately pretended
pairs.quijote_half: and the others driving Sancho and Dapple before them, and all maintaining a strange silence, followed in the steps of the one who led Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_source:
Till now, however, Genji had deliberately
pretended not to notice that anything was wrong.
pairs.quijote_source:
One of those on foot, putting his finger
to his lips as a sign to him to be silent, seized Rocinante’s bridle and
drew him out of the road, and the others driving Sancho and Dapple before
them, and all maintaining a strange silence, followed in the steps of the
one who led Don Quixote.

pairs.display: The visitors reached the Empress’s apartments in a long procession they had made use of to withdraw him from that Pena Pobre where he fancied himself stationed because of his lady’s scorn.
pairs.genji_half: The visitors reached the Empress’s apartments in a long procession
pairs.quijote_half: they had made use of to withdraw him from that Pena Pobre where he fancied himself stationed because of his lady’s scorn.
pairs.genji_source:
The visitors reached the Empress’s apartments in a long
procession, headed by Genji himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho fetched him his clothes; and while he was dressing, the curate
gave Don Fernando and the others present an account of Don Quixote’s
madness and of the stratagem they had made use of to withdraw him from
that Pena Pobre where he fancied himself stationed because of his lady’s
scorn.

pairs.display: Tamakatsura, after that first encounter on the night that which will be told in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_half: Tamakatsura, after that first encounter on the night
pairs.quijote_half: that which will be told in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_source:
Tamakatsura, after that first encounter on the night when the mummers
danced in front of the palace, had continued her friendship with
Murasaki.
pairs.quijote_source:
As they went along, then, at a slow pace--for
the pain in Don Quixote’s jaws kept him uneasy and ill-disposed for
speed--Sancho thought it well to amuse and divert him by talk of some
kind, and among the things he said to him was that which will be told in
the following chapter.

pairs.display: Genji had very little business at this season, however, put the following lines: A doughty gentleman lies here; A stranger all his life to fear; Nor in his death could Death prevail
pairs.genji_half: Genji had very little business at this season,
pairs.quijote_half: however, put the following lines: A doughty gentleman lies here; A stranger all his life to fear; Nor in his death could Death prevail
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
had very little business at this season, and there was a great deal of
music and entertaining at the New Palace.

pairs.display: These remarks had, however, been at the expense of his usual sureties, his shoulders.
pairs.genji_half: These remarks had, however,
pairs.quijote_half: been at the expense of his usual sureties, his shoulders.
pairs.genji_source:
These remarks had, however, an exactly opposite effect to that
which Genji intended.
pairs.quijote_source:
When Sancho had done drinking he dug his heels into his ass, and the gate
of the inn being thrown open he passed out very well pleased at having
paid nothing and carried his point, though it had been at the expense of
his usual sureties, his shoulders.

pairs.display: But glancing at his letters Genji feared that upon the hill of Love they might reasonably expect to find some inn within a league or two.
pairs.genji_half: But glancing at his letters Genji feared that upon the hill of Love
pairs.quijote_half: they might reasonably expect to find some inn within a league or two.
pairs.genji_source:
But glancing at his letters Genji feared that upon the hill of Love,
where, let it be remembered, even Confucius stumbled, this wise
prince too might easily find his undoing.
pairs.quijote_source:
It so happened that the night
closed in somewhat darkly, but for all that they pushed on, Sancho
feeling sure that as the road was the king’s highway they might
reasonably expect to find some inn within a league or two.

pairs.display: It comes from Captain Kashiwagi, he always addressed his lord with his cap in his hand, his head bowed down and his body bent double, more turquesco.
pairs.genji_half: It comes from Captain Kashiwagi,
pairs.quijote_half: he always addressed his lord with his cap in his hand, his head bowed down and his body bent double, more turquesco.
pairs.genji_source:
It comes from Captain
Kashiwagi, His Excellency Tō no Chūjō’s eldest son.
pairs.quijote_source:
There was Gandalin, the squire
of Amadis of Gaul, that was Count of the Insula Firme, and we read of him
that he always addressed his lord with his cap in his hand, his head
bowed down and his body bent double, more turquesco.

pairs.display: From his manner Murasaki instantly saw that he felt sure that in the way of bestowing rewards on their squires emperors could do more than archbishops-errant.
pairs.genji_half: From his manner Murasaki instantly saw that
pairs.quijote_half: he felt sure that in the way of bestowing rewards on their squires emperors could do more than archbishops-errant.
pairs.genji_source:
From his manner Murasaki instantly
saw that his interest in Tamakatsura had assumed a new character.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this
Sancho listened to and fixed it well in his memory, and thanked them
heartily for intending to recommend his master to be an emperor instead
of an archbishop, for he felt sure that in the way of bestowing rewards
on their squires emperors could do more than archbishops-errant.

pairs.display: To convince himself that Murasaki had the object of Sancho Panza’s fear and dread; but though he would have rather not entered it, there was no help for it.
pairs.genji_half: To convince himself that Murasaki had
pairs.quijote_half: the object of Sancho Panza’s fear and dread; but though he would have rather not entered it, there was no help for it.
pairs.genji_source:
To convince himself that Murasaki had no ground for her suspicions
he frequently went across to the Side Wing and spent some hours in
Tamakatsura’s company.
pairs.quijote_source:
Their dainty repast being finished, they saddled at once, and without any
adventure worth mentioning they reached next day the inn, the object of
Sancho Panza’s fear and dread; but though he would have rather not
entered it, there was no help for it.

pairs.display: Though Tamakatsura was no longer very young, she was still entirely innocent, raising over him the most doleful and laughable lamentation that ever was heard, for he believed he was dead.
pairs.genji_half: Though Tamakatsura was no longer very young, she was still entirely innocent,
pairs.quijote_half: raising over him the most doleful and laughable lamentation that ever was heard, for he believed he was dead.
pairs.genji_source:
