pairs.genji_source:
But though her mind often wandered, she was perfectly well aware of the
tense anxiety with which Genji watched her progress.
pairs.quijote_source:
He at once
concluded that some damsel of the duchess’s was in love with him, and
that her modesty forced her to keep her passion secret.

pairs.display: They felt rather uneasy about the matter, for he knew already what a great friend of his she was.
pairs.genji_half: They felt rather uneasy about the matter,
pairs.quijote_half: for he knew already what a great friend of his she was.
pairs.genji_source:
They felt rather uneasy about the matter,
but thought it their duty to inform Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duchess said they must
leave them with him; for he knew already what a great friend of his she
was.

pairs.display: As for Kashiwagi, his conduct had been of an insolence lauding to the skies, in his rustic phrase, her rare beauty, her graceful gaiety, and her courtesy.
pairs.genji_half: As for Kashiwagi, his conduct had been of an insolence
pairs.quijote_half: lauding to the skies, in his rustic phrase, her rare beauty, her graceful gaiety, and her courtesy.
pairs.genji_source:
As for Kashiwagi, his conduct had been of an insolence and treachery
such as Genji would not have imagined any human being to be capable
of.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho returned to his master mightily pleased with this gratifying
answer, and told him all the great lady had said to him, lauding to the
skies, in his rustic phrase, her rare beauty, her graceful gaiety, and
her courtesy.

pairs.display: Kojijū for her part only wished that they took such pains to make game of a pair of fools.
pairs.genji_half: Kojijū for her part only wished that
pairs.quijote_half: they took such pains to make game of a pair of fools.
pairs.genji_source:
Kojijū for her part
only wished that Kashiwagi took a like view of Genji’s visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
And Cide Hamete says,
moreover, that for his part he considers the concocters of the joke as
crazy as the victims of it, and that the duke and duchess were not two
fingers’ breadth removed from being something like fools themselves when
they took such pains to make game of a pair of fools.

pairs.display: While serving Genji’s breakfast she never came out to see him.
pairs.genji_half: While serving Genji’s breakfast she never
pairs.quijote_half: came out to see him.
pairs.genji_source:
While serving
Genji’s breakfast she never for an instant took her eye off this blue
letter, that lay folded beside his bowl.
pairs.quijote_source:
The whole household of the castle were watching
him from the corridors, and the duke and duchess, too, came out to see
him.

pairs.display: As soon as he was alone Genji pulled out the letter and even had he heard, he would not have turned back if the king had ordered him.
pairs.genji_half: As soon as he was alone Genji pulled out the letter
pairs.quijote_half: and even had he heard, he would not have turned back if the king had ordered him.
pairs.genji_source:
As soon as he was alone Genji pulled out the letter and studied it more
attentively.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho laboured in
vain, for his master was so bent on coming to quarters with these sheeted
figures and releasing the lady in black that he did not hear a word; and
even had he heard, he would not have turned back if the king had ordered
him.

pairs.display: Suddenly it occurred to Genji to ask himself whether this man was at times taken with a mad fit, for if they had known it they would have been on their guard to protect themselves.
pairs.genji_half: Suddenly it occurred to Genji to ask himself whether
pairs.quijote_half: this man was at times taken with a mad fit, for if they had known it they would have been on their guard to protect themselves.
pairs.genji_source:
Suddenly it occurred to Genji to ask himself whether perhaps his
father, the Old Emperor, had not been in just the same fix.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho rose, and with the rage he felt at
finding himself so belaboured without deserving it, ran to take vengeance
on the goatherd, accusing him of not giving them warning that this man
was at times taken with a mad fit, for if they had known it they would
have been on their guard to protect themselves.

pairs.display: But it was only too likely that some of her people had wanted to kill half a dozen of those dirty unmannerly pigs, for he had by this time found out that that was what they were.
pairs.genji_half: But it was only too likely that some of her people had
pairs.quijote_half: wanted to kill half a dozen of those dirty unmannerly pigs, for he had by this time found out that that was what they were.
pairs.genji_source:
But it was only too likely that some of her people
had, in their indiscreet zeal, talked against Genji at Court or in
social gatherings elsewhere.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho got up as well as he could and begged his master to give him his
sword, saying he wanted to kill half a dozen of those dirty unmannerly
pigs, for he had by this time found out that that was what they were.

pairs.display: No doubt he too (though Genji had never before suspected it) knew the barber brought him out something to eat.
pairs.genji_half: No doubt he too (though Genji had never before suspected it) knew
pairs.quijote_half: the barber brought him out something to eat.
pairs.genji_source:
No doubt
he too (though Genji had never before suspected it) knew perfectly
well what was going on, and had merely pretended not to see.
pairs.quijote_source:
They left him and went in, and presently the barber brought
him out something to eat.

pairs.display: That was of course the worst; but the actual and immediate question of how to behave towards Genji and advancing some distance through the shady chestnut trees they came upon a little meadow at the foot of some high rocks, down which a mighty rush of water flung itself.
pairs.genji_half: That was of course the worst; but the actual and immediate question of how to behave towards Genji
pairs.quijote_half: and advancing some distance through the shady chestnut trees they came upon a little meadow at the foot of some high rocks, down which a mighty rush of water flung itself.
pairs.genji_source:
That was of course the worst; but the
actual and immediate question of how to behave towards Genji for the
moment fretted Kashiwagi till he became positively ill.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho followed him on foot, leading by the halter, as his custom was,
his ass, his constant comrade in prosperity or adversity; and advancing
some distance through the shady chestnut trees they came upon a little
meadow at the foot of some high rocks, down which a mighty rush of water
flung itself.

pairs.display: He knew that during the worst period of Murasaki’s illness Genji had went to where the servants were standing and put the question to one of them, who answered him.
pairs.genji_half: He knew that during the worst period of Murasaki’s illness Genji had
pairs.quijote_half: went to where the servants were standing and put the question to one of them, who answered him.
pairs.genji_source:
He knew that during the
worst period of Murasaki’s illness Genji had for months on end been
absent from the New Palace; but this, under the circumstances, was
perfectly natural.
pairs.quijote_source:
Observing this the curate, curious to know who
these people in such a dress and preserving such silence were, went to
where the servants were standing and put the question to one of them, who
answered him.

pairs.display: Suzaku could only hope that Nyosan had the keen appetite they all of them brought with them.
pairs.genji_half: Suzaku could only hope that Nyosan had
pairs.quijote_half: the keen appetite they all of them brought with them.
pairs.genji_source:
Suzaku could only hope that Nyosan had
herself done nothing to countenance such gossip.
pairs.quijote_source:
They
dismounted together by the side of the spring, and with what the curate
had provided himself with at the inn they appeased, though not very well,
the keen appetite they all of them brought with them.

pairs.display: The letter that he now wrote to her happened to arrive on one of the rare occasions so he replied that his master was engaged in a certain quarter on a certain matter of great importance to him which he could not disclose for the eyes in his head.
pairs.genji_half: The letter that he now wrote to her happened to arrive on one of the rare occasions
pairs.quijote_half: so he replied that his master was engaged in a certain quarter on a certain matter of great importance to him which he could not disclose for the eyes in his head.
pairs.genji_source:
The letter that he now wrote to her happened
to arrive on one of the rare occasions when Genji was at the New
Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho recognised them at once, and determined to keep secret the place
and circumstances where and under which he had left his master, so he
replied that his master was engaged in a certain quarter on a certain
matter of great importance to him which he could not disclose for the
eyes in his head.

pairs.display: His father Tō no Chūjō pointed so that he might tell him the mystery of this man in the cage, and other things that would amuse him.
pairs.genji_half: His father Tō no Chūjō pointed
pairs.quijote_half: so that he might tell him the mystery of this man in the cage, and other things that would amuse him.
pairs.genji_source:
His father Tō no
Chūjō pointed out that Genji would certainly be offended.
pairs.quijote_source:
and he himself were trying so hard to
conceal; and under the same apprehension the curate had asked the canon
to ride on a little in advance, so that he might tell him the mystery of
this man in the cage, and other things that would amuse him.

pairs.display: It should be explained that during the period when Tō no Chūjō kept Yūgiri away from Kumoi, even they who were aware of the cause were frightened.
pairs.genji_half: It should be explained that during the period when Tō no Chūjō kept Yūgiri away from Kumoi,
pairs.quijote_half: even they who were aware of the cause were frightened.
pairs.genji_source:
It should be explained that during the period when Tō no Chūjō kept
Yūgiri away from Kumoi, he had carried on a secret affair with this
daughter of Koremitsu.
pairs.quijote_source: The duke was astounded, the duchess amazed, Don Quixote wondering, Sancho Panza trembling, and indeed, even they who were aware of the cause were frightened.

pairs.display: For example, her stepmother had even held her responsible for Makibashira's fall; and Don Quixote went some twelve paces forward to meet her.
pairs.genji_half: For example, her stepmother had even held her responsible for Makibashira's fall;
pairs.quijote_half: and Don Quixote went some twelve paces forward to meet her.
pairs.genji_source: For example, her stepmother had even held her responsible for Makibashira’s fall; it was Murasaki’s jealousy (so this woman asserted) that had forced Genji to plant Tamakatsura in Higekuro’s way!
pairs.quijote_source:
On seeing this
the duke, the duchess, and Don Quixote went some twelve paces forward to
meet her.

pairs.display: The Akashi Princess was now back at the Imperial Palace; and the duchess showed Sancho's letter to the duke, who was highly delighted with it.
pairs.genji_half: The Akashi Princess was now back at the Imperial Palace;
pairs.quijote_half: and the duchess showed Sancho's letter to the duke, who was highly delighted with it.
pairs.genji_source:
The Akashi Princess was now back at the Imperial Palace; but Genji
persuaded her to let Prince Niou stay with him for a while.
pairs.quijote_source: With this they betook themselves to a garden where they were to dine, and the duchess showed Sancho’s letter to the duke, who was highly delighted with it.

pairs.display: He arrived before the other princes and courtiers who were expected, but he never said a word about the blanketing that had befallen himself in that inn, into which he refused to enter.
pairs.genji_half: He arrived before the other princes and courtiers who were expected,
pairs.quijote_half: but he never said a word about the blanketing that had befallen himself in that inn, into which he refused to enter.
pairs.genji_source:
He arrived before the other princes
and courtiers who were expected, and was at once admitted behind
Genji’s screens-of-state.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho repeated it three times, and
as he did, uttered three thousand more absurdities; then he told them
more about his master but he never said a word about the blanketing that
had befallen himself in that inn, into which he refused to enter.

pairs.display: Just the kind of person, thought Genji, for as my master says, nothing costs less or is cheaper than civility.
pairs.genji_half: Just the kind of person, thought Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: for as my master says, nothing costs less or is cheaper than civility.
pairs.genji_source:
Just the kind
of person, thought Genji, whom one would hit upon to put in charge of
a flighty young princess.
pairs.quijote_source:
My lady the duchess kisses thy hands a thousand
times; do thou make a return with two thousand, for as my master says,
nothing costs less or is cheaper than civility.

pairs.display: The arrival of Kashiwagi in this condition was a most elegant youth, who had brought another present which was worth as much more.
pairs.genji_half: The arrival of Kashiwagi in this condition was
pairs.quijote_half: a most elegant youth, who had brought another present which was worth as much more.
pairs.genji_source:
The arrival of Kashiwagi in this condition was a great shock
to his father Tō no Chūjō.
pairs.quijote_source:
Teresa in reply bade
them come with her to her house and they would see the messenger, a most
elegant youth, who had brought another present which was worth as much
more.

pairs.display: Every one at Court came to enquire after his progress; the Emperor grappled with Maritornes, and he and she between them began the bitterest and drollest scrimmage in the world.
pairs.genji_half: Every one at Court came to enquire after his progress; the Emperor
pairs.quijote_half: grappled with Maritornes, and he and she between them began the bitterest and drollest scrimmage in the world.
pairs.genji_source:
Every one at Court came to
enquire after his progress; the Emperor and Suzaku both sent frequent
messengers, while the grief of his parents need not be described.
pairs.quijote_source:
He then,
finding himself so handled, by whom he knew not, raising himself up as
well as he could, grappled with Maritornes, and he and she between them
began the bitterest and drollest scrimmage in the world.

pairs.display: Above all, Yūgiri, who had been his closest friend, and in the style of the hired mourners that were once in fashion, they raised a lamentation over the departure of their master and uncle, as if it had been his death.
pairs.genji_half: Above all, Yūgiri, who had been his closest friend,
pairs.quijote_half: and in the style of the hired mourners that were once in fashion, they raised a lamentation over the departure of their master and uncle, as if it had been his death.
pairs.genji_source:
Above
all, Yūgiri, who had been his closest friend, was in great distress,
and now spent most of his time at Kashiwagi’s bedside.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curses which both housekeeper and niece poured out on the bachelor
were past counting; they tore their hair, they clawed their faces, and in
the style of the hired mourners that were once in fashion, they raised a
lamentation over the departure of their master and uncle, as if it had
been his death.

pairs.display: His real object was to meet Kojijū, they resolved to carry on the joke, seeing what a fit subject they had to deal with for making it all pass for reality.
pairs.genji_half: His real object was to meet Kojijū,
pairs.quijote_half: they resolved to carry on the joke, seeing what a fit subject they had to deal with for making it all pass for reality.
pairs.genji_source:
His real
object was to meet Kojijū, but to Tō no Chūjō it was given out that
Kashiwagi was asleep.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duke and duchess were so well pleased with the successful and droll
result of the adventure of the Distressed One, that they resolved to
carry on the joke, seeing what a fit subject they had to deal with for
making it all pass for reality.

pairs.display: Kashiwagi now sent her to fetch a paper candle and posted themselves so close to it that they could hear all that was said inside.
pairs.genji_half: Kashiwagi now sent her to fetch a paper candle
pairs.quijote_half: and posted themselves so close to it that they could hear all that was said inside.
pairs.genji_source:
Kashiwagi now sent
her to fetch a paper candle and by its light examined Nyosan’s reply.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duke gave them leave,
and the pair cautiously and quietly crept to the door of the room and
posted themselves so close to it that they could hear all that was said
inside.

pairs.display: It may be imagined, however, that all these ceremonies bound his forehead with a strip of black silk, while with another he made a mask with which he concealed his beard and face very well.
pairs.genji_half: It may be imagined, however, that all these ceremonies
pairs.quijote_half: bound his forehead with a strip of black silk, while with another he made a mask with which he concealed his beard and face very well.
pairs.genji_source:
It may
be imagined, however, that all these ceremonies, in which to outward
appearances Genji was intimately concerned, gave him in reality nothing
but awkwardness and discomfort.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate would not let them hood him,
but put on his head a little quilted linen cap which he used for a
night-cap, and bound his forehead with a strip of black silk, while with
another he made a mask with which he concealed his beard and face very
well.

pairs.display: This then, thought Suzaku to himself, being of a kind by no means to be omitted and deserving of the same attention as the main story.
pairs.genji_half: This then, thought Suzaku to himself,
pairs.quijote_half: being of a kind by no means to be omitted and deserving of the same attention as the main story.
pairs.genji_source:
This then,
thought Suzaku to himself, was Genji’s attitude towards the loved being
whose happiness he had so confidently entrusted to his keeping.
pairs.quijote_source:
To this the curate replied that not only were they not weary of listening
to him, but that the details he mentioned interested them greatly, being
of a kind by no means to be omitted and deserving of the same attention
as the main story.

pairs.display: However unsatisfactory Genji might have proved as a husband, finding himself at liberty, would take to his old courses and make off where nobody could ever find him again.
pairs.genji_half: However unsatisfactory Genji might have proved as a husband,
pairs.quijote_half: finding himself at liberty, would take to his old courses and make off where nobody could ever find him again.
pairs.genji_source:
However unsatisfactory Genji might have
proved as a husband, he had certainly provided very handsomely for
her in material ways.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate
understood him, and said he would very gladly comply with his request,
only that he feared his master, finding himself at liberty, would take to
his old courses and make off where nobody could ever find him again.

pairs.display: Yūgiri bent close down over the pillow, but Kashiwagi was so weak that his voice was scarcely audible, not only as regarded the wine-skins but also the wine, and above all the depreciation of the tail which they set such store by.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri bent close down over the pillow, but Kashiwagi was so weak that his voice was scarcely audible,
pairs.quijote_half: not only as regarded the wine-skins but also the wine, and above all the depreciation of the tail which they set such store by.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri bent close
down over the pillow, but Kashiwagi was so weak that his voice was
scarcely audible, and it seemed as though he had great difficulty in
breathing.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate smoothed matters by promising to make good
all losses to the best of his power, not only as regarded the wine-skins
but also the wine, and above all the depreciation of the tail which they
set such store by.

pairs.display: They had shared each other’s lives and would maintain that the damsel lied by half a beard, nay a whole beard, if she affirmed that he had given her a promise of marriage.
pairs.genji_half: They had shared each other’s lives
pairs.quijote_half: and would maintain that the damsel lied by half a beard, nay a whole beard, if she affirmed that he had given her a promise of marriage.
pairs.genji_source:
They had shared each other’s lives
and thoughts—at least until the disastrous episode which Genji now
did his best to forget.
pairs.quijote_source:
Two days later the duke told Don
Quixote that in four days from that time his opponent would present
himself on the field of battle armed as a knight, and would maintain that
the damsel lied by half a beard, nay a whole beard, if she affirmed that
he had given her a promise of marriage.

pairs.display: The passage about the _tokoro_ came oddly from Suzaku’s pen, and urged him to quit a life of such misery, lest he should end it there, which would be the greatest of all misfortunes.
pairs.genji_half: The passage about the _tokoro_ came oddly from Suzaku’s pen,
pairs.quijote_half: and urged him to quit a life of such misery, lest he should end it there, which would be the greatest of all misfortunes.
pairs.genji_source:
The passage
about the _tokoro_ came oddly from Suzaku’s pen, belonging as it did to
pietism rather than to poetry; but Genji read it with a feeling of deep
remorse.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate, who was aware of his misfortune and recognised him by the
description, being a man of good address, approached him and in a few
sensible words entreated and urged him to quit a life of such misery,
lest he should end it there, which would be the greatest of all
misfortunes.

pairs.display: All this time Yūgiri had been turning over and flung it with one dash all over his body, on which Don Quixote woke up, but not so completely as to understand what was the matter.
pairs.genji_half: All this time Yūgiri had been turning over
pairs.quijote_half: and flung it with one dash all over his body, on which Don Quixote woke up, but not so completely as to understand what was the matter.
pairs.genji_source:
All this time Yūgiri had been turning over in his mind what Kashiwagi
had said to him on his death-bed.
pairs.quijote_source:
But in spite of all the poor
gentleman never woke until the barber brought a great pot of cold water
from the well and flung it with one dash all over his body, on which Don
Quixote woke up, but not so completely as to understand what was the
matter.

pairs.display: Yūgiri had often watched Kaoru from a distance, lest he should make his escape from them again, telling her what they had been obliged to do to bring him home.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri had often watched Kaoru from a distance,
pairs.quijote_half: lest he should make his escape from them again, telling her what they had been obliged to do to bring him home.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri had often watched Kaoru from a distance, but had never made
friends with him.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate charged his niece to be very careful to make her uncle
comfortable and to keep a watch over him lest he should make his escape
from them again, telling her what they had been obliged to do to bring
him home.

pairs.display: He rather overdid these protestations of bewilderment, and seeing the duenna in such a state of excitement, and her eyes flaming so, asked whom she was wrangling with.
pairs.genji_half: He rather overdid these protestations of bewilderment,
pairs.quijote_half: and seeing the duenna in such a state of excitement, and her eyes flaming so, asked whom she was wrangling with.
pairs.genji_source:
He rather overdid these protestations of bewilderment, with the
result that Genji became more certain than ever of his having learnt
the whole secret.
pairs.quijote_source:
and she said it so loud, that the duchess heard it, and
turning round and seeing the duenna in such a state of excitement, and
her eyes flaming so, asked whom she was wrangling with.

pairs.display: Even Genji, who was only a son-in-law, and at the mighty crash of this the innkeeper awoke and at once concluded that it must be some brawl of Maritornes’, because after calling loudly to her he got no answer.
pairs.genji_half: Even Genji, who was only a son-in-law,
pairs.quijote_half: and at the mighty crash of this the innkeeper awoke and at once concluded that it must be some brawl of Maritornes’, because after calling loudly to her he got no answer.
pairs.genji_source:
Even Genji, who was
only a son-in-law, had shown much more concern.
pairs.quijote_source:
The bed which was somewhat crazy and not very firm on its feet, unable to
support the additional weight of the carrier, came to the ground, and at
the mighty crash of this the innkeeper awoke and at once concluded that
it must be some brawl of Maritornes’, because after calling loudly to her
he got no answer.

pairs.display: Among Yūgiri’s own contemporaries, the one, strangely enough, promised to pray a rosary of prayers that God might grant them success in such an arduous and Christian undertaking as that they had in hand.
pairs.genji_half: Among Yūgiri’s own contemporaries, the one, strangely enough,
pairs.quijote_half: promised to pray a rosary of prayers that God might grant them success in such an arduous and Christian undertaking as that they had in hand.
pairs.genji_source:
Among Yūgiri’s own
contemporaries, the one, strangely enough, who most entered into his
feelings was the quiet, almost stolid Kashiwagi.
pairs.quijote_source:
They took leave of all, and of the good Maritornes, who, sinner as she
was, promised to pray a rosary of prayers that God might grant them
success in such an arduous and Christian undertaking as that they had in
hand.

pairs.display: It was not long before she so arrayed herself that she looked like a great and rich lady.
pairs.genji_half: It was not long before
pairs.quijote_half: she so arrayed herself that she looked like a great and rich lady.
pairs.genji_source: It was not long before Genji heard what was going on.
pairs.quijote_source:
Dorothea then took out of her pillow-case a complete petticoat of some
rich stuff, and a green mantle of some other fine material, and a
necklace and other ornaments out of a little box, and with these in an
instant she so arrayed herself that she looked like a great and rich
lady.

pairs.display: Curious to see how he would take it, Genji began speaking for the right to adopt him and claim him as a son, as the seven cities of Greece contended for Homer.
pairs.genji_half: Curious to see how he would take it, Genji began speaking
pairs.quijote_half: for the right to adopt him and claim him as a son, as the seven cities of Greece contended for Homer.
pairs.genji_source:
Curious to see how he would take it, Genji began speaking of their
friends at Ono next time Yūgiri came to his palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
Such was the end of the Ingenious Gentleman of La Mancha, whose village
Cide Hamete would not indicate precisely, in order to leave all the towns
and villages of La Mancha to contend among themselves for the right to
adopt him and claim him as a son, as the seven cities of Greece contended
for Homer.

pairs.display: If his son had lied less flatly, but things did not fall out with him as he expected, according to what is related in the course of this veracious history, of which the Second Part ends here.
pairs.genji_half: If his son had lied less flatly,
pairs.quijote_half: but things did not fall out with him as he expected, according to what is related in the course of this veracious history, of which the Second Part ends here.
pairs.genji_source:
If his
son had lied less flatly, Genji would have felt it possible to
continue the conversation.
pairs.quijote_source:
He, on his part, resolved to go in quest of the shepherdess Marcela, and
make offer to her of all the service he could render her; but things did
not fall out with him as he expected, according to what is related in the
course of this veracious history, of which the Second Part ends here.

pairs.display: Having delivered this message Koremitsu brought in some of Genji's servants and went home to his village to wait there for Don Quixote, who was coming after him.
pairs.genji_half: Having delivered this message Koremitsu brought in some of Genji's servants
pairs.quijote_half: and went home to his village to wait there for Don Quixote, who was coming after him.
pairs.genji_source: Having delivered this message Koremitsu brought in some of Genji’s servants whom he had sent to mount guard over the house that night.
pairs.quijote_source:
And so
he took his leave of the duke, and went home to his village to wait there
for Don Quixote, who was coming after him.

pairs.display: On hearing Koremitsu's report Genji's heart was filled with pity and dined with the duke and duchess, and set out the same evening.
pairs.genji_half: On hearing Koremitsu's report Genji's heart was filled with pity
pairs.quijote_half: and dined with the duke and duchess, and set out the same evening.
pairs.genji_source: On hearing Koremitsu’s report Genji’s heart was filled with pity for the child’s state and he would like to have gone to her at once.
pairs.quijote_source:
So the conversation came to an end, and Don Quixote dressed himself and
dined with the duke and duchess, and set out the same evening.

pairs.display: Although it was early days to begin thinking about the little Akashi Princess's Initiation, the duchess was as much delighted as Don Quixote was driven to desperation.
pairs.genji_half: Although it was early days to begin thinking about the little Akashi Princess's Initiation,
pairs.quijote_half: the duchess was as much delighted as Don Quixote was driven to desperation.
pairs.genji_source: Although it was early days to begin thinking about the little Akashi Princess’s Initiation, Genji seemed bent upon celebrating it immediately.
pairs.quijote_source:
By every word that Sancho uttered, the duchess was as much delighted as
Don Quixote was driven to desperation.

pairs.display: Here they were often seen by Genji for though they thought her a simpleton and a weak creature, they did not think her capable of crazy pranks.
pairs.genji_half: Here they were often seen by Genji
pairs.quijote_half: for though they thought her a simpleton and a weak creature, they did not think her capable of crazy pranks.
pairs.genji_source:
Here they were often seen by Genji and became
great favourites with him.
pairs.quijote_source:
All who knew her were filled with astonishment,
and the duke and duchess more than any; for though they thought her a
simpleton and a weak creature, they did not think her capable of crazy
pranks.

pairs.display: The claims, then, were pretty equally divided, and so, highly amused at Sancho Panza's simplicities, they placed Don Quixote in the cart as before.
pairs.genji_half: The claims, then, were pretty equally divided,
pairs.quijote_half: and so, highly amused at Sancho Panza's simplicities, they placed Don Quixote in the cart as before.
pairs.genji_source:
The claims, then, were pretty equally divided, and after
some hesitation Genji followed his own inclinations by appointing
Akikonomu to share the Throne.
pairs.quijote_source: The canon, the curate, and the barber told him he would act very wisely in doing as he said; and so, highly amused at Sancho Panza’s simplicities, they placed Don Quixote in the cart as before.

pairs.display: It so happened that Genji was actually at Akikonomu's palace when the scented bales arrived; for she found infinite enjoyment in listening to his shrewd remarks.
pairs.genji_half: It so happened that Genji was actually at Akikonomu's palace when the scented bales arrived;
pairs.quijote_half: for she found infinite enjoyment in listening to his shrewd remarks.
pairs.genji_source: It so happened that Genji was actually at Akikonomu’s palace when the scented bales arrived; her servants at once showed them to him and told him whence they came.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duchess
desired Sancho to come to her side, for she found infinite enjoyment in
listening to his shrewd remarks.

pairs.display: To Genji it seemed that what they pretended so adroitly in jest had happened to them in reality.
pairs.genji_half: To Genji it seemed that
pairs.quijote_half: what they pretended so adroitly in jest had happened to them in reality.
pairs.genji_source:
To Genji it seemed that her strength was
gradually ebbing away.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duke came to himself slowly and like one recovering consciousness
after a heavy sleep, and the duchess and all who had fallen prostrate
about the garden did the same, with such demonstrations of wonder and
amazement that they would have almost persuaded one that what they
pretended so adroitly in jest had happened to them in reality.

pairs.display: It was of course quite wrong that Kaoru, the child of a commoner, should attempt the dread adventure.
pairs.genji_half: It was of course quite wrong that Kaoru, the child of a commoner,
pairs.quijote_half: should attempt the dread adventure.
pairs.genji_source:
It was of course quite wrong that Kaoru, the child of a
commoner, should be brought up with the Akashi Princess’s children.
pairs.quijote_source: Don Quixote, then, observing that Rocinante could move, took it as a good sign and a signal that he should attempt the dread adventure.

pairs.display: But at that moment Genji himself held up the great lamp, he would have given his housekeeper, and his niece into the bargain.
pairs.genji_half: But at that moment Genji himself held up the great lamp,
pairs.quijote_half: he would have given his housekeeper, and his niece into the bargain.
pairs.genji_source:
But at that
moment Genji himself held up the great lamp, bringing it so close
to the couch that Yūgiri suddenly saw her in all her loveliness.
pairs.quijote_source:
To have a bout of kicking at that traitor of
a Ganelon he would have given his housekeeper, and his niece into the
bargain.

pairs.display: Never, thought Genji, had her beauty seemed so flawless as now, especially when he had thought of some one to call his Lady!
pairs.genji_half: Never, thought Genji, had her beauty seemed so flawless as now,
pairs.quijote_half: especially when he had thought of some one to call his Lady!
pairs.genji_source:
Never, thought Genji, had her beauty seemed so flawless as
now, when the eye could rest upon it undistracted by any ripple of
sound or motion.
pairs.quijote_source:
Oh, how our good gentleman enjoyed the delivery of
this speech, especially when he had thought of some one to call his Lady!

pairs.display: Day and night Genji wept, My Uncle and liege lord!
pairs.genji_half: Day and night Genji wept,
pairs.quijote_half: My Uncle and liege lord!
pairs.genji_source:
Day and night Genji wept, till it seemed that a veil of tears hung
between him and the world.
pairs.quijote_source:
O noble Marquis of Mantua,
  My Uncle and liege lord!

pairs.display: And so time passed, and at three in the afternoon they came in sight of it.
pairs.genji_half: And so time passed,
pairs.quijote_half: and at three in the afternoon they came in sight of it.
pairs.genji_source:
And so time passed, Genji constantly thinking that he would to-morrow
take the step for which he longed....
pairs.quijote_source:
They returned to the road they had set out with, leading to Puerto
Lapice, and at three in the afternoon they came in sight of it.

pairs.display: Their only comfort was that Genji had quite ceased to pay any visits, when he saw himself dealt with in this fashion?
pairs.genji_half: Their only comfort was that Genji had quite ceased to pay any visits,
pairs.quijote_half: when he saw himself dealt with in this fashion?
pairs.genji_source:
Their only comfort was that Genji had quite ceased to pay any visits,
and they were thus able to distract themselves by continually waiting
upon him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Who is there that could properly describe the rage that filled
the heart of our Manchegan when he saw himself dealt with in this
fashion?

pairs.display: One evening when a faint haze mingled with the fading light, The hoarse vague echoes of my sorrows sound
pairs.genji_half: One evening when a faint haze mingled with the fading light,
pairs.quijote_half: The hoarse vague echoes of my sorrows sound
pairs.genji_source:
One evening when a faint haze mingled with the fading light, Genji at
last set out to visit the Lady of Akashi.
pairs.quijote_source:
For, though it be to solitudes remote
The hoarse vague echoes of my sorrows sound

pairs.display: What, Genji asked himself as he watched the boys, And death, inevitable, waits in hall.
pairs.genji_half: What, Genji asked himself as he watched the boys,
pairs.quijote_half: And death, inevitable, waits in hall.
pairs.genji_source:
What, Genji asked himself as he
watched the boys, lay behind that wondering and innocent expression?

pairs.display: Of course, Genji must always have regarded me as in every way far beneath him; and--bitter change!-- All his suspicions turned to certainties, And the fair truth transformed into a lie?
pairs.genji_half: Of course, Genji must always have regarded me as in every way far beneath him;
pairs.quijote_half: and--bitter change!-- All his suspicions turned to certainties, And the fair truth transformed into a lie?
pairs.genji_source:
Of course, Genji must always have regarded me as in every
way far beneath him; but ever since I was a boy he had always shown
the greatest confidence in me.
pairs.quijote_source:
Who would not give free access to distrust,
Seeing disdain unveiled, and--bitter change!--
All his suspicions turned to certainties,
And the fair truth transformed into a lie?

pairs.display: “Quietly, quietly” she said holding up a warning finger; drown the memory of you.
pairs.genji_half: “Quietly, quietly” she said holding up a warning finger;
pairs.quijote_half: drown the memory of you.
pairs.genji_source:
“Quietly,
quietly” she said holding up a warning finger; “how do you know it is
the Captain?
pairs.quijote_source:
triumphant over all,
My sufferings drown the memory of you.

pairs.display: Their old relations were never resumed; The doleful concert join: a lover dead Methinks can have no fitter obsequies.
pairs.genji_half: Their old relations were never resumed;
pairs.quijote_half: The doleful concert join: a lover dead Methinks can have no fitter obsequies.
pairs.genji_source:
Their old
relations were never resumed; but the Captain was still numbered among
the favourite gentlemen of his household.
pairs.quijote_source:
Let the three-headed guardian of the gate,
And all the monstrous progeny of hell,
The doleful concert join: a lover dead
Methinks can have no fitter obsequies.

pairs.display: The Lady of Ōmi was somewhat homely in appearance; he addressed her, with manifest indignation:
pairs.genji_half: The Lady of Ōmi was somewhat homely in appearance;
pairs.quijote_half: he addressed her, with manifest indignation:
pairs.genji_source:
The Lady of Ōmi was somewhat homely
in appearance; but nobody (thought Tō no Chūjō) could possibly call her
downright ugly.
pairs.quijote_source:
But the
instant Ambrosio saw her he addressed her, with manifest indignation:

pairs.display: It happened that Lady Akikonomu painted very charmingly, why should my modest behaviour and circumspection be blamed?
pairs.genji_half: It happened that Lady Akikonomu painted very charmingly,
pairs.quijote_half: why should my modest behaviour and circumspection be blamed?
pairs.genji_source:
It happened that Lady Akikonomu
painted very charmingly, and so soon as he discovered this the Emperor
began constantly sending for her to paint pictures with him.
pairs.quijote_source:
If Chrysostom’s impatience and violent passion
killed him, why should my modest behaviour and circumspection be
blamed?

pairs.display: Koremitsu, anxious to carry out his master’s every wish and intent in God’s name without offering violence to anybody.
pairs.genji_half: Koremitsu, anxious to carry out his master’s every wish and intent
pairs.quijote_half: in God’s name without offering violence to anybody.
pairs.genji_source:
Koremitsu, anxious to carry out his master’s every wish and intent
also on his own intrigue, contrived at last by a series of ingenious
stratagems to effect a secret meeting between Genji and the mysterious
lady.
pairs.quijote_source:
He thanked us for the offer,
begged pardon for the late assault, and promised for the future to ask it
in God’s name without offering violence to anybody.

pairs.display: All through the day he sent numerous letters, and at dusk Koremitsu again went to the house saying what will be told farther on.
pairs.genji_half: All through the day he sent numerous letters, and at dusk Koremitsu again went to the house saying
pairs.quijote_half: what will be told farther on.
pairs.genji_source:
All through the day he sent numerous
letters, and at dusk Koremitsu again went to the house saying that
urgent business had once more prevented Genji from visiting them, for
which remissness he tendered his apologies.
pairs.quijote_source:
To be brief, the first to speak after embracing was
the Ragged One, and he said what will be told farther on.

pairs.display: Princess Ōmiya whose one consolation, since the death of her daughter Aoi, is a grandee of Spain who has his seat in the best part of this Andalusia.
pairs.genji_half: Princess Ōmiya whose one consolation, since the death of her daughter Aoi,
pairs.quijote_half: is a grandee of Spain who has his seat in the best part of this Andalusia.
pairs.genji_source:
Princess Ōmiya whose one consolation, since the death of her daughter
Aoi, had been the arrival of Lady Kumoi, was appalled at this sudden
loss.
pairs.quijote_source:
This Duke
Ricardo, as you, sirs, probably know already, is a grandee of Spain who
has his seat in the best part of this Andalusia.

pairs.display: Meanwhile Tamakatsura was taking less trouble than ever for, my oath upon it, they are deceiving themselves!
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile Tamakatsura was taking less trouble than ever
pairs.quijote_half: for, my oath upon it, they are deceiving themselves!
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile Tamakatsura was taking less trouble than ever to pretend
that Higekuro’s caresses were agreeable to her.

pairs.display: Meanwhile it reached Higekuro’s ears that the Emperor was with her, That this poor aching bosom crushes?
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile it reached Higekuro’s ears that the Emperor was with her,
pairs.quijote_half: That this poor aching bosom crushes?
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile it reached Higekuro’s ears that the Emperor was with her,
and falling into a panic he again began scheming to carry her home at
the earliest possible moment.
pairs.quijote_source:
Are ye aweary of the woe
  That this poor aching bosom crushes?

pairs.display: But there is a hint that Tamakatsura is shut away Doomed for a lady-love to languish
pairs.genji_half: But there is a hint that Tamakatsura is shut away
pairs.quijote_half: Doomed for a lady-love to languish
pairs.genji_source:
But there is a hint that Tamakatsura is shut away in Higekuro’s
palace like a tame bird in a cage.
pairs.quijote_source:
The lealest lover time can show,
  Doomed for a lady-love to languish,

pairs.display: Nowhere else is thirty-seven mentioned; will cure the heart of sadness? Madness.
pairs.genji_half: Nowhere else is thirty-seven mentioned;
pairs.quijote_half: will cure the heart of sadness? Madness.
pairs.genji_source:
Nowhere else is
thirty-seven mentioned; but it is to be noted Fujitsubo died at the age
of thirty-seven.
pairs.quijote_source:
What, if all fail, will cure the heart of sadness?
    Madness.

pairs.display: Suzaku’s vows forbad that any gift from him should figure on such an occasion; for melancholy: Ask where it lies; the answer saith In Change, in Madness, or in Death.
pairs.genji_half: Suzaku’s vows forbad that any gift from him should figure on such an occasion;
pairs.quijote_half: for melancholy: Ask where it lies; the answer saith In Change, in Madness, or in Death.
pairs.genji_source:
Suzaku’s vows forbad that any gift from him should figure on
such an occasion; but it was generally understood that a magnificent
largesse from the Public Treasury, brought by
pairs.quijote_source:
If that be so, it is but folly
  To seek a cure for melancholy:
  Ask where it lies; the answer saith
  In Change, in Madness, or in Death.

pairs.display: But Kōkiden had always hated Genji and having herself arranged that her sister should enter the Palace, This earth will be the prey of strife once more, As when primaeval discord held its reign.
pairs.genji_half: But Kōkiden had always hated Genji and having herself arranged that her sister should enter the Palace,
pairs.quijote_half: This earth will be the prey of strife once more, As when primaeval discord held its reign.
pairs.genji_source:
But Kōkiden had always hated Genji and
having herself arranged that her sister should enter the Palace,
she saw no reason why this plan should suddenly be abandoned.
pairs.quijote_source:
If thou wilt not unmask thy counterfeit,
  This earth will be the prey of strife once more,
    As when primaeval discord held its reign.

pairs.display: the one they used to call Hyōtōda, and the lady towards whom they showed all the accessories they had borrowed to effect Don Quixote’s deliverance.
pairs.genji_half: the one they used to call Hyōtōda, and the lady towards whom they showed
pairs.quijote_half: all the accessories they had borrowed to effect Don Quixote’s deliverance.
pairs.genji_source:
the one they used to call Hyōtōda,
and the lady towards whom they showed such deference could be no other
than Tamakatsura, Yūgao’s child by Tō no Chūjō.
pairs.quijote_source:
On this the
barber cheerfully restored the tail to the landlady, and at the same time
they returned all the accessories they had borrowed to effect Don
Quixote’s deliverance.

pairs.display: Prince Genji’s conduct is certainly outrageous; but you are very fond of your sister so judicious, so loyal, and so true?
pairs.genji_half: Prince Genji’s conduct is certainly outrageous; but you are very fond of your sister
pairs.quijote_half: so judicious, so loyal, and so true?
pairs.genji_source:
Prince Genji’s conduct is certainly outrageous; but
you are very fond of your sister and you cannot denounce him without
getting her too into trouble.
pairs.quijote_source:
But where is such a friend to be found as
Lothario would have, so judicious, so loyal, and so true?

pairs.display: But she was still very far from occupying a position anything like that of Murasaki, when the two were strolling together outside the city, Anselmo addressed the following words to Lothario.
pairs.genji_half: But she was still very far from occupying a position anything like that of Murasaki,
pairs.quijote_half: when the two were strolling together outside the city, Anselmo addressed the following words to Lothario.
pairs.genji_source:
But
she was still very far from occupying a position anything like that
of Murasaki, upon whom, as the years went by, Genji seemed to
become more and more dependent.
pairs.quijote_source:
It happened, however, that on one occasion when
the two were strolling together outside the city, Anselmo addressed the
following words to Lothario.

pairs.display: But she was growing steadily weaker, and knowing that at any moment the end might come, And the reason’s plain to see;
pairs.genji_half: But she was growing steadily weaker, and knowing that at any moment the end might come,
pairs.quijote_half: And the reason’s plain to see;
pairs.genji_source:
But she was
growing steadily weaker, and knowing that at any moment the end might
come, Genji watched by her bedside hour after hour in an agony of
continual suspense.
pairs.quijote_source:
This, then, all may hold as true,
And the reason’s plain to see;

pairs.display: Akikonomu was now nineteen; for the rest of the household had gone to dinner.
pairs.genji_half: Akikonomu was now nineteen;
pairs.quijote_half: for the rest of the household had gone to dinner.
pairs.genji_source: Akikonomu was now nineteen; the boy-Emperor Ryōzen, seven.
pairs.quijote_source:
Anselmo took his departure, and Camilla and Lothario were left alone at
the table, for the rest of the household had gone to dinner.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō as usual nodded his assent, with such engines that had Camilla been of brass she must have fallen.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō as usual nodded his assent,
pairs.quijote_half: with such engines that had Camilla been of brass she must have fallen.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō as usual nodded his assent, and Genji’s smile seemed such
as to show that he too accepted Uma no Kami’s advice.
pairs.quijote_source:
In fact with the
utmost assiduity he undermined the rock of her purity with such engines
that had Camilla been of brass she must have fallen.

pairs.display: But for some years such supervision would still be needed, With undiminished force my sorrow flows In broken accents and in burning sighs.
pairs.genji_half: But for some years such supervision would still be needed,
pairs.quijote_half: With undiminished force my sorrow flows In broken accents and in burning sighs.
pairs.genji_source:
But for some years
such supervision would still be needed, and unfortunately there was no
one else to whom Genji could possibly entrust such a task.
pairs.quijote_source:
And when the light of day returning dyes
  The portals of the east with tints of rose,
  With undiminished force my sorrow flows
In broken accents and in burning sighs.

pairs.display: But now, despite the very important position he held, he knew well enough that, ere My heart repented of its love for thee.
pairs.genji_half: But now, despite the very important position he held, he knew well enough that,
pairs.quijote_half: ere My heart repented of its love for thee.
pairs.genji_source:
But now, despite the very important
position he held, he knew well enough that, compared with Genji, he was
in popular estimation a very insignificant person indeed.
pairs.quijote_source:
Dead at thy feet shouldst see me lying, ere
My heart repented of its love for thee.

pairs.display: When Suzaku gave his children toys, it was always Nyosan who had the first choice, to take your own life, or Lothario’s?
pairs.genji_half: When Suzaku gave his children toys, it was always Nyosan who had the first choice,
pairs.quijote_half: to take your own life, or Lothario’s?
pairs.genji_source:
When Suzaku gave his children toys, it
was always Nyosan who had the first choice, the others never getting
the smallest trinket unless she had first refused it.
pairs.quijote_source:
Can it be that you mean to take your own
life, or Lothario’s?

pairs.display: But he was determined, next time an opportunity occurred, simply to repeat to Genji what Kashiwagi had said, for giving such authority in his house to this shameless fellow!
pairs.genji_half: But he was determined, next time an opportunity occurred, simply to repeat to Genji what Kashiwagi had said,
pairs.quijote_half: for giving such authority in his house to this shameless fellow!
pairs.genji_source:
But he was determined, next time an opportunity
occurred, simply to repeat to Genji what Kashiwagi had said, and see
whether Genji could make sense of it all, or no.
pairs.quijote_source:
Ill betide my
master, Anselmo, for giving such authority in his house to this shameless
fellow!

pairs.display: Ukon herself was the bearer of this letter; she also reported much of what Genji had said to her, and threatened to stab Leonela, bidding her tell the truth
pairs.genji_half: Ukon herself was the bearer of this letter; she also reported much of what Genji had said to her,
pairs.quijote_half: and threatened to stab Leonela, bidding her tell the truth
pairs.genji_source:
Ukon herself was the bearer of this letter; she also reported much of
what Genji had said to her, especially such expressions of cordiality
and goodwill as would tend to allay Tamakatsura’s apprehensions.
pairs.quijote_source:
Anselmo would not believe it, but blind with rage drew a dagger and
threatened to stab Leonela, bidding her tell the truth or he would kill
her.

pairs.display: For a moment Genji’s servants who were preceding him to the Heir Apparent’s rooms blocked his path but not finding her in bed or anywhere in the house he was lost in amazement.
pairs.genji_half: For a moment Genji’s servants who were preceding him to the Heir Apparent’s rooms blocked his path
pairs.quijote_half: but not finding her in bed or anywhere in the house he was lost in amazement.
pairs.genji_source:
For a moment Genji’s servants who were
preceding him to the Heir Apparent’s rooms blocked his path and forced
him to stand waiting till they had passed.
pairs.quijote_source:
He returned,
uneasy, to tell Camilla, but not finding her in bed or anywhere in the
house he was lost in amazement.

pairs.display: But recently the occasion of Lady Fujitsubo’s death had called him back to the Court, worn out by grief and melancholy.
pairs.genji_half: But recently the occasion of Lady Fujitsubo’s death had called him back to the Court,
pairs.quijote_half: worn out by grief and melancholy.
pairs.genji_source:
But
recently the occasion of Lady Fujitsubo’s death had called him back to
the Court, and the Emperor had more than once summoned him to his side.
pairs.quijote_source:
On learning this Camilla took the veil, and shortly afterwards died, worn
out by grief and melancholy.

pairs.display: Too proud to let him think that she had posted herself in waiting and unable to speak a Christian tongue.
pairs.genji_half: Too proud to let him think that she had posted herself in waiting
pairs.quijote_half: and unable to speak a Christian tongue.
pairs.genji_source:
Too proud to let him think that she had posted herself in
waiting for him, she said to her servants (while the boy was busy
in Genji’s room)
pairs.quijote_source:
From her silence they concluded
that she must be a Moor and unable to speak a Christian tongue.

pairs.display: Meanwhile Genji, in a frenzy of irritation and disappointment, decided upon going to the Indies, embarking the portion that fell to him in trade.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile Genji, in a frenzy of irritation and disappointment,
pairs.quijote_half: decided upon going to the Indies, embarking the portion that fell to him in trade.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile Genji, in a
frenzy of irritation and disappointment, scarce knew how he came to be
in her ante-chamber nor thought how he was going to retire from it.
pairs.quijote_source:
My second brother having made the same
proposal, decided upon going to the Indies, embarking the portion that
fell to him in trade.

pairs.display: Hearing this, Genji sent after them to complete his studies at Salamanca.
pairs.genji_half: Hearing this, Genji sent after them
pairs.quijote_half: to complete his studies at Salamanca.
pairs.genji_source:
Hearing this, Genji sent after them with apologies and had them
brought back to the Summer House where they were entertained with food
and drink.
pairs.quijote_source:
The youngest, and in my opinion the wisest, said he
would rather follow the church, or go to complete his studies at
Salamanca.

pairs.display: It is also referred to by Murasaki’s contemporary Sei a Milanese gentleman, a great engineer and a very brave soldier.
pairs.genji_half: It is also referred to by Murasaki’s contemporary Sei
pairs.quijote_half: a Milanese gentleman, a great engineer and a very brave soldier.
pairs.genji_source:
It is also referred to by
Murasaki’s contemporary Sei Shōnagon in Chapter 145 of her _Makura no
Sōshi_.
pairs.quijote_source:
They also took the commandant of the fort, Gabrio Cerbellon
by name, a Milanese gentleman, a great engineer and a very brave soldier.

pairs.display: It being then three months since her grandmother died Murasaki and he treated his slaves with great humanity.
pairs.genji_half: It being then three months since her grandmother died Murasaki
pairs.quijote_half: and he treated his slaves with great humanity.
pairs.genji_source:
It being then three months since her grandmother died Murasaki came
out of mourning at the end of the Godless Month.
pairs.quijote_source:
He was a Calabrian by birth, and a worthy man morally, and he
treated his slaves with great humanity.

pairs.display: But stay, there was the girl Murasaki too, making the same movements as before.
pairs.genji_half: But stay, there was the girl Murasaki too,
pairs.quijote_half: making the same movements as before.
pairs.genji_source:
But
stay, there was the girl Murasaki too, waiting for him in the wing of
his palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
The Christian came back, and it was again lowered, making the same
movements as before.

pairs.display: Seeing that she was alone and make use of them honestly, and remain on Christian soil.
pairs.genji_half: Seeing that she was alone
pairs.quijote_half: and make use of them honestly, and remain on Christian soil.
pairs.genji_source:
Seeing that she was alone
Murasaki slowly raised her head.
pairs.quijote_source:
Others, however, there are who procure these
papers and make use of them honestly, and remain on Christian soil.

pairs.display: But ever since their betrothal Murasaki had shown a certain shyness in company with the Tagarin already mentioned.
pairs.genji_half: But ever since their betrothal Murasaki had shown a certain shyness
pairs.quijote_half: in company with the Tagarin already mentioned.
pairs.genji_source:
But ever
since their betrothal Murasaki had shown a certain shyness and
diffidence in his presence.
pairs.quijote_source:
Two or
three times he made this voyage in company with the Tagarin already
mentioned.

pairs.display: It seemed to him when he was back in his palace she, however, returned him no answer.
pairs.genji_half: It seemed to him when he was back in his palace
pairs.quijote_half: she, however, returned him no answer.
pairs.genji_source:
It seemed to him when he was back in his palace that Murasaki had in
these last months become far less childish.
pairs.quijote_source:
The renegade interpreted to us what the Moor said to his daughter; she,
however, returned him no answer.

pairs.display: Finally he had to explain to Murasaki all the arrangements he had made and this is the fair Moor who has been so good to him.
pairs.genji_half: Finally he had to explain to Murasaki all the arrangements he had made
pairs.quijote_half: and this is the fair Moor who has been so good to him.
pairs.genji_source:
Finally he
had to explain to Murasaki all the arrangements he had made about the
servants who were to stay behind, and a hundred other matters.
pairs.quijote_source:
He whom
you see here is the Captain Viedma, and this is the fair Moor who has
been so good to him.

pairs.display: But Murasaki could not help remembering his enthusiasm and the lady they are carrying on that stand there is the blessed image of the immaculate Virgin.
pairs.genji_half: But Murasaki could not help remembering his enthusiasm
pairs.quijote_half: and the lady they are carrying on that stand there is the blessed image of the immaculate Virgin.
pairs.genji_source:
But Murasaki could not help remembering his enthusiasm for the
playing of the Lady at Akashi.
pairs.quijote_source:
mind, that is a procession of penitents, and the lady
they are carrying on that stand there is the blessed image of the
immaculate Virgin.

pairs.display: Sometimes perhaps on his way to Murasaki’s room he might look in casually With promises of ease, the heart’s desire
pairs.genji_half: Sometimes perhaps on his way to Murasaki’s room he might look in casually
pairs.quijote_half: With promises of ease, the heart’s desire,
pairs.genji_source:
Sometimes perhaps on his way to
Murasaki’s room he might look in casually for a moment; more she could
not expect.
pairs.quijote_source:
Delusive hopes that lure the common herd
    With promises of ease, the heart’s desire

pairs.display: As you know, though we have been married for many years, and dust: The end of all flesh that dies.
pairs.genji_half: As you know, though we have been married for many years,
pairs.quijote_half: and dust: The end of all flesh that dies.
pairs.genji_source:
As you know, though we
have been married for many years, Lady Murasaki has no children of her
own, and this very much distresses her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Plump was she and robust:
  Now she is ashes and dust:
The end of all flesh that dies.

pairs.display: But as soon as one or both should make an appearance in the Nijō palace, they dressed the licentiate in his own clothes, which were new and decent.
pairs.genji_half: But as soon as one or both should make an appearance in the Nijō palace,
pairs.quijote_half: they dressed the licentiate in his own clothes, which were new and decent.
pairs.genji_source:
But
as soon as one or both should make an appearance in the Nijō palace,
Murasaki’s pride would be affronted and her jealousy aroused....
pairs.quijote_source:
The governor, seeing that it was the order of the
Archbishop, obeyed, and they dressed the licentiate in his own clothes,
which were new and decent.

pairs.display: Through what misery the mother must be passing, they say that he was somewhat of a drunkard.
pairs.genji_half: Through what misery the mother must be passing,
pairs.quijote_half: they say that he was somewhat of a drunkard.
pairs.genji_source:
Through what misery the mother must
be passing, Murasaki was now in a position to judge for herself.
pairs.quijote_source:
Of
Alexander, whose deeds won him the name of Great, they say that he was
somewhat of a drunkard.

pairs.display: He went to the western wing; but instead of going straight into Murasaki’s room, it is said that he was lewd and luxurious.
pairs.genji_half: He went to the western wing; but instead of going straight into Murasaki’s room,
pairs.quijote_half: it is said that he was lewd and luxurious.
pairs.genji_source:
He went to the western wing; but instead of going straight into
Murasaki’s room, he flung himself down upon a couch in the vestibule.
pairs.quijote_source:
Of Hercules, him of the many labours, it is said
that he was lewd and luxurious.

pairs.display: Murasaki wondered how she could have ever made Julius Caesar cross the Rubicon?
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki wondered how she could have ever
pairs.quijote_half: made Julius Caesar cross the Rubicon?
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki wondered how she could have ever doubted the rumours that were
now rampant throughout the Court.
pairs.quijote_source:
What, in opposition to all the omens that declared against
him, made Julius Caesar cross the Rubicon?

pairs.display: Meanwhile his frequent absences from the Nijō-in had already convinced Murasaki in ceaseless wanderings and rude and arduous toils
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile his frequent absences from the Nijō-in had already convinced Murasaki
pairs.quijote_half: in ceaseless wanderings and rude and arduous toils
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile his frequent absences from the Nijō-in had already convinced
Murasaki that the affair was as serious as it could possibly be.
pairs.quijote_source:
can it be, most serene Casildea de
Vandalia, that thou wilt suffer this thy captive knight to waste away and
perish in ceaseless wanderings and rude and arduous toils?

pairs.display: But soon he was weeping bitterly, to the bewilderment of Murasaki, a minute account of all that is concealed in those gloomy depths.
pairs.genji_half: But soon he was weeping bitterly, to the bewilderment of Murasaki,
pairs.quijote_half: a minute account of all that is concealed in those gloomy depths.
pairs.genji_source:
But soon he
was weeping bitterly, to the bewilderment of Murasaki, who nevertheless
lay all the time stock still at his side.
pairs.quijote_source:
Again, she bade me fling myself into the cavern of
Cabra--an unparalleled and awful peril--and bring her a minute account of
all that is concealed in those gloomy depths.

pairs.display: With Murasaki he had to find a bone-setter, with whose help the unfortunate Samson was cured.
pairs.genji_half: With Murasaki he had
pairs.quijote_half: to find a bone-setter, with whose help the unfortunate Samson was cured.
pairs.genji_source:
With Murasaki he
had few dealings and was indeed barely acquainted with her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thus discoursing, the pair proceeded until they reached a town where it
was their good luck to find a bone-setter, with whose help the
unfortunate Samson was cured.

pairs.display: Lady Murasaki was now almost twenty-eight, once the glory and honour of Spanish knighthood!
pairs.genji_half: Lady Murasaki was now almost twenty-eight,
pairs.quijote_half: once the glory and honour of Spanish knighthood!
pairs.genji_source:
Lady Murasaki was now almost twenty-eight, but never (thought the old
woman when she arrived) had she looked so handsome.
pairs.quijote_source:
Second modern Don Manuel de Leon, once the glory and
honour of Spanish knighthood!

pairs.display: Compared with my Lady...?’ And what she gave she took away.
pairs.genji_half: Compared with my Lady...?’
pairs.quijote_half: And what she gave she took away.
pairs.genji_source:
Compared with my Lady...?’
and he nodded towards Lady Murasaki.
pairs.quijote_source:
But all things change; she changed her mind,
And what she gave she took away.

pairs.display: He remembered Suyetsumu’s extreme incapacity in this direction, and feared that Tamakatsura and the Muses never cross the thresholds of their doors.
pairs.genji_half: He remembered Suyetsumu’s extreme incapacity in this direction, and feared that Tamakatsura
pairs.quijote_half: and the Muses never cross the thresholds of their doors.
pairs.genji_source:
He remembered
Suyetsumu’s extreme incapacity in this direction, and feared
that Tamakatsura, after her strange upbringing, might prove to be a
hundred times more hesitating and inefficient.
pairs.quijote_source:
Heaven grant that the judges who
rob you of the first prize--that Phoebus may pierce them with his arrows,
and the Muses never cross the thresholds of their doors.

pairs.display: The Princess from Akashi was On wings of poesy upborne Shall be exalted to the skies.
pairs.genji_half: The Princess from Akashi was
pairs.quijote_half: On wings of poesy upborne Shall be exalted to the skies.
pairs.genji_source:
The Princess from Akashi was
still living in Murasaki’s apartments.
pairs.quijote_source:
If thou my homage wilt not scorn,
  Thy fortune, watched by envious eyes,
On wings of poesy upborne
  Shall be exalted to the skies.

pairs.display: Among these ladies was a certain Chūjō, one of his own gentlewomen, because of the bad night brides always pass dressing themselves out for their wedding on the morrow.
pairs.genji_half: Among these ladies was a certain Chūjō, one of his own gentlewomen,
pairs.quijote_half: because of the bad night brides always pass dressing themselves out for their wedding on the morrow.
pairs.genji_source:
Among these ladies was a certain Chūjō, one of his own
gentlewomen, whom he had transferred to Murasaki’s service at the time
of his exile.
pairs.quijote_source:
The fair Quiteria appeared somewhat pale, which was, no doubt, because of
the bad night brides always pass dressing themselves out for their
wedding on the morrow.

pairs.display: He could not very well absent himself from his own apartments, with the ungrateful Quiteria!
pairs.genji_half: He could not very well absent himself from his own apartments,
pairs.quijote_half: with the ungrateful Quiteria!
pairs.genji_source:
He could not very well absent himself from
his own apartments, for there were visitors coming and Murasaki was
expecting him....
pairs.quijote_source:
many a happy year may he live with the ungrateful
Quiteria!

pairs.display: The boys arrived by water, and in joy and gladness take the road to Paris.
pairs.genji_half: The boys arrived by water,
pairs.quijote_half: and in joy and gladness take the road to Paris.
pairs.genji_source:
The boys arrived by water,
having embarked at the landing-stage in front of Murasaki’s rooms.
pairs.quijote_source:
You see how they wheel round and quit the city, and in joy and
gladness take the road to Paris.

pairs.display: On looking back, I see that I have forgotten to mention the presents and killing and destroying, but only little pasteboard figures!
pairs.genji_half: On looking back, I see that I have forgotten to mention the presents
pairs.quijote_half: and killing and destroying, but only little pasteboard figures!
pairs.genji_source:
On looking back, I see that I have forgotten to mention the presents
which Murasaki distributed among her visitors after the ceremonies
of the day before.
pairs.quijote_source:
can’t you see they’re not real Moors you’re
knocking down and killing and destroying, but only little pasteboard
figures!

pairs.display: The finish of the course was not so very far from Murasaki’s rooms, was as much astonished at his mad freaks as at his generosity.
pairs.genji_half: The finish of the course was not so very far from Murasaki’s rooms,
pairs.quijote_half: was as much astonished at his mad freaks as at his generosity.
pairs.genji_source:
The finish of the course was not so very far from Murasaki’s
rooms, so that her gentlewomen too were able to get some idea of what
was going on.
pairs.quijote_source:
The landlord, who did not know Don
Quixote, was as much astonished at his mad freaks as at his generosity.

pairs.display: Murasaki, who had first taken to reading romances in order to see whether they were suitable was as much astonished at his mad freaks as at his generosity.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki, who had first taken to reading romances in order to see whether they were suitable
pairs.quijote_half: was as much astonished at his mad freaks as at his generosity.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki, who had first taken to reading romances in order to see
whether they were suitable for her adopted daughter, the Princess from
Akashi, was now deeply immersed in them.
pairs.quijote_source:
The landlord, who did not know Don
Quixote, was as much astonished at his mad freaks as at his generosity.

pairs.display: The book seemed as old-fashioned to Murasaki for one single step further thou shalt not make in my company.
pairs.genji_half: The book seemed as old-fashioned to Murasaki
pairs.quijote_half: for one single step further thou shalt not make in my company.
pairs.genji_source:
The book seemed as old-fashioned to Murasaki as Hannah
        More’s novels do to us.
pairs.quijote_source:
Turn the rein, or the
halter, of thy Dapple, and begone home; for one single step further thou
shalt not make in my company.

pairs.display: His father was bending over Murasaki who was one of the listeners.
pairs.genji_half: His father was bending over Murasaki
pairs.quijote_half: who was one of the listeners.
pairs.genji_source:
His father was bending over Murasaki
and whispering something in her ear; now he was laughing.
pairs.quijote_source:
exclaimed Dona Rodriguez, the duenna, who
was one of the listeners.

pairs.display: If he had not openly accepted her as his concubine, at that time in vogue in Kandy, which they call seguidillas!
pairs.genji_half: If he had not openly accepted her as his concubine,
pairs.quijote_half: at that time in vogue in Kandy, which they call seguidillas!
pairs.genji_source:
If he had not
openly accepted her as his concubine, it was through fear of Murasaki
and the rest.
pairs.quijote_source:
And then, when they condescend to
compose a sort of verse that was at that time in vogue in Kandy, which
they call seguidillas!

pairs.display: His wife, an elder sister of Murasaki, and most ludicrous figure that could be imagined.
pairs.genji_half: His wife, an elder sister of Murasaki,
pairs.quijote_half: and most ludicrous figure that could be imagined.
pairs.genji_source:
His wife, an elder sister of Murasaki,
was four years his senior.
pairs.quijote_source:
She did so, and Don
Quixote was left the strangest and most ludicrous figure that could be
imagined.

pairs.display: My father says that, after all, Lady Murasaki is my own sister, made by Merlin.
pairs.genji_half: My father says that, after all, Lady Murasaki is my own sister,
pairs.quijote_half: made by Merlin.
pairs.genji_source:
My father says that, after all, Lady
Murasaki is my own sister, and it is very wrong of her to spoil my
happiness by introducing you to this girl.
pairs.quijote_source:
This horse, according to ancient tradition, was made by
Merlin.

pairs.display: The visit to the Shrine was made very early on the morning of the Festival, From Manzanares to Genil, From Duero to Arlanza.
pairs.genji_half: The visit to the Shrine was made very early on the morning of the Festival,
pairs.quijote_half: From Manzanares to Genil, From Duero to Arlanza.
pairs.genji_source:
The visit to the Shrine
was made very early on the morning of the Festival, and Murasaki
was back in time to view the processions from the usual Stand.
pairs.quijote_source:
And she for this shall famous be
    From Tagus to Jarama,
From Manzanares to Genil,
    From Duero to Arlanza.

pairs.display: On the night of the actual Presentation Murasaki accompanied the child and looked upon their governor as another Solomon.
pairs.genji_half: On the night of the actual Presentation Murasaki accompanied the child
pairs.quijote_half: and looked upon their governor as another Solomon.
pairs.genji_source:
On the night of the actual Presentation Murasaki accompanied the child
in the hand-litter which was to convey her to the Eastern Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
All were filled with amazement, and looked upon their
governor as another Solomon.

pairs.display: While dressing the little Princess in all the finery that this trying occasion demanded, he came riding on the hours, and morning very soon arrived.
pairs.genji_half: While dressing the little Princess in all the finery that this trying occasion demanded,
pairs.quijote_half: he came riding on the hours, and morning very soon arrived.
pairs.genji_source:
While
dressing the little Princess in all the finery that this trying
occasion demanded, Murasaki could not help passionately wishing that
this lovely child were really hers.
pairs.quijote_source:
But as Time is
fleet and no obstacle can stay his course, he came riding on the hours,
and morning very soon arrived.

pairs.display: For though generous and long-suffering, Modesty their highest praise.
pairs.genji_half: For though generous and long-suffering,
pairs.quijote_half: Modesty their highest praise.
pairs.genji_source:
For though generous and
long-suffering, Murasaki was capable of making judgments that were by
no means devoid of sharpness.
pairs.quijote_source:
And to proper-minded maidens
  Who desire the matron’s name
Modesty’s a marriage portion,
  Modesty their highest praise.

pairs.display: While he knocked at the outer door of Murasaki’s room, at four in the morning.
pairs.genji_half: While he knocked at the outer door of Murasaki’s room,
pairs.quijote_half: at four in the morning.
pairs.genji_source:
While he knocked at the outer door
of Murasaki’s room, he murmured to himself those lines of Po Chū-i:
pairs.quijote_source:
From this place, the Sixteenth of August, at four in the
morning.

pairs.display: But a moment later, looking at Murasaki, and a few years later went, no doubt, to heaven, for they were excellent good Catholic Christians.
pairs.genji_half: But a moment later, looking at Murasaki,
pairs.quijote_half: and a few years later went, no doubt, to heaven, for they were excellent good Catholic Christians.
pairs.genji_source:
But a moment later, looking
at Murasaki, he wondered how it could ever have occurred to him as
possible to leave her.
pairs.quijote_source:
My parents left me in service and returned
to their own country, and a few years later went, no doubt, to heaven,
for they were excellent good Catholic Christians.

pairs.display: The answer was long in coming, though it might be in contravention of all the aphorisms of Hippocrates.
pairs.genji_half: The answer was long in coming,
pairs.quijote_half: though it might be in contravention of all the aphorisms of Hippocrates.
pairs.genji_source:
The answer was long in coming, and tired of waiting
he went back to Murasaki in the inner room.
pairs.quijote_source:
At last Doctor
Pedro Recio Agilers of Tirteafuera promised to let him have supper that
night though it might be in contravention of all the aphorisms of
Hippocrates.

pairs.display: Alas, her writing was still quite unformed, the governor had supper.
pairs.genji_half: Alas, her writing was still quite unformed,
pairs.quijote_half: the governor had supper.
pairs.genji_source:
Alas, her writing was still quite unformed,
and he wished that it had been possible to prevent Murasaki from
seeing it some while longer.
pairs.quijote_source:
Night came, and with the permission of Doctor Pedro Recio, the governor
had supper.

pairs.display: If it had come from any one else he would certainly have a thousand reals in that gambling house opposite, and God knows how.
pairs.genji_half: If it had come from any one else he would certainly have
pairs.quijote_half: a thousand reals in that gambling house opposite, and God knows how.
pairs.genji_source:
If it had come from any one else he would
certainly have discussed this strangely backward letter with Murasaki
and attempted to account for its inefficiency.
pairs.quijote_source:
Your worship must know that this gentleman has just now won more
than a thousand reals in that gambling house opposite, and God knows how.

pairs.display: However, this time it was Murasaki herself who had suggested the meeting, and Sanchica cutting a rasher of bacon to be paved with eggs for his dinner.
pairs.genji_half: However, this time it was Murasaki herself who had suggested the meeting,
pairs.quijote_half: and Sanchica cutting a rasher of bacon to be paved with eggs for his dinner.
pairs.genji_source:
However, this time
it was Murasaki herself who had suggested the meeting, and there could
be no sense in opposing it.
pairs.quijote_source:
They found the page sifting a
little barley for his horse, and Sanchica cutting a rasher of bacon to be
paved with eggs for his dinner.

pairs.display: Meanwhile Murasaki went to her room an object of pity to anyone.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile Murasaki went to her room
pairs.quijote_half: an object of pity to anyone.
pairs.genji_source: Meanwhile Murasaki went to her room to get ready for the visit.
pairs.quijote_source: Adieu; God keep thee from being an object of pity to anyone.

pairs.display: Here she was visited by Murasaki, who found her sitting up in her white jacket, and witty Altisidora lifted up her voice and said in pathetic tones:
pairs.genji_half: Here she was visited by Murasaki, who found her sitting up in her white jacket,
pairs.quijote_half: and witty Altisidora lifted up her voice and said in pathetic tones:
pairs.genji_source:
Here she was
visited by Murasaki, who found her sitting up in her white jacket,
holding the little boy in her arms.
pairs.quijote_source:
While all were, as has been said,
observing him, suddenly from among the duennas and handmaidens the
impudent and witty Altisidora lifted up her voice and said in pathetic
tones:
