pairs.display: But Tamakatsura had been too much surprised and shocked by Genji’s conduct to feel, for the moment, that the poor knight in spite of himself came down backwards off his horse.
pairs.genji_half: But Tamakatsura had been too much surprised and shocked by Genji’s conduct to feel, for the moment,
pairs.quijote_half: that the poor knight in spite of himself came down backwards off his horse.
pairs.genji_source:
But Tamakatsura had been too
much surprised and shocked by Genji’s conduct to feel, for the moment,
any gratitude for the more than parental solicitude by which Ateki was
so deeply impressed.
pairs.quijote_source:
Such was the force
of the first blow and of the second, that the poor knight in spite of
himself came down backwards off his horse.

pairs.display: And as for the other ladies in Genji’s household—their presence would not necessarily be a disadvantage; rearing up she flung her rider to the ground over her haunches.
pairs.genji_half: And as for the other ladies in Genji’s household—their presence would not necessarily be a disadvantage;
pairs.quijote_half: rearing up she flung her rider to the ground over her haunches.
pairs.genji_source:
And as for the other
ladies in Genji’s household—their presence would not necessarily be
a disadvantage; Genji could easily prevent that, if he was by way of
taking any trouble about her at all.
pairs.quijote_source:
The mule was shy, and was so frightened at her bridle being seized that
rearing up she flung her rider to the ground over her haunches.

pairs.display: And if it was with a slight feeling of disappointment that Yūgiri saw his hint so rapidly obeyed, and in two words the knight becomes king.
pairs.genji_half: And if it was with a slight feeling of disappointment that Yūgiri saw his hint so rapidly obeyed,
pairs.quijote_half: and in two words the knight becomes king.
pairs.genji_source:
And if it was with a slight feeling of
disappointment that Yūgiri saw his hint so rapidly obeyed, it may be
imagined how loud Kashiwagi’s heart had all this while been beating.
pairs.quijote_source:
The father dies, the princess inherits, and
in two words the knight becomes king.

pairs.display: But Genji always assured her that he too intended quite soon to quit the Court, and all who have aided him in rising to so exalted a rank.
pairs.genji_half: But Genji always assured her that he too intended quite soon to quit the Court,
pairs.quijote_half: and all who have aided him in rising to so exalted a rank.
pairs.genji_source:
But
Genji always assured her that he too intended quite soon to quit the
Court, and indeed would long ago have done so, had he not feared to
leave her in solitude.
pairs.quijote_source:
And here comes in at once the
bestowal of rewards upon his squire and all who have aided him in rising
to so exalted a rank.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō was anxious to show his good will towards Genji upon what appeared to be so auspicious an occasion; except that when he looked, his eyes turned in a little one towards the other.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō was anxious to show his good will towards Genji upon what appeared to be so auspicious an occasion;
pairs.quijote_half: except that when he looked, his eyes turned in a little one towards the other.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō was anxious to show
his good will towards Genji upon what appeared to be so auspicious an
occasion; but owing to Kashiwagi’s alarming condition he was unable to
appear in person.
pairs.quijote_source:
Behind all these there came a man of thirty, a very personable fellow,
except that when he looked, his eyes turned in a little one towards the
other.

pairs.display: Every day Genji had his unholy books brought him.
pairs.genji_half: Every day Genji
pairs.quijote_half: had his unholy books brought him.
pairs.genji_source:
Every day Genji
felt his loss more keenly.
pairs.quijote_source:
Strange to say, he stood up for her as if she were in
earnest his veritable born lady; to such a pass had his unholy books
brought him.

pairs.display: There was nothing that was no fault of his.
pairs.genji_half: There was nothing that
pairs.quijote_half: was no fault of his.
pairs.genji_source: There was nothing that Genji could usefully say.
pairs.quijote_source:
The goatherd replied that
he had said so, and that if he had not heard him, that was no fault of
his.

pairs.display: Perhaps after all it was rather soon to begin suspending his nightly visits; stood like an isolated peak among the others that surrounded it.
pairs.genji_half: Perhaps after all it was rather soon to begin suspending his nightly visits;
pairs.quijote_half: stood like an isolated peak among the others that surrounded it.
pairs.genji_source:
Perhaps after all it was rather soon to begin suspending
his nightly visits; he had better invent some fresh excuse and go
to Nyosan at any rate this one time more.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thus talking they reached the foot of a high mountain which stood like an
isolated peak among the others that surrounded it.

pairs.display: All this while Tō no Chūjō had been living at the Great Hall, these three verses were all that could be plainly and perfectly deciphered.
pairs.genji_half: All this while Tō no Chūjō had been living at the Great Hall,
pairs.quijote_half: these three verses were all that could be plainly and perfectly deciphered.
pairs.genji_source:
All this while Tō no Chūjō had been living at the Great Hall, with
very little indeed to amuse him.
pairs.quijote_source:
He wrote many
more, but, as has been said, these three verses were all that could be
plainly and perfectly deciphered.

pairs.display: In the eighth month of this year that he should have it copied on paper at the first village he came to.
pairs.genji_half: In the eighth month of this year
pairs.quijote_half: that he should have it copied on paper at the first village he came to.
pairs.genji_source:
In the
eighth month of this year Tō no Chūjō’s daughter came to Court.
pairs.quijote_source:
He said
it was written in a note-book, and that his master’s directions were that
he should have it copied on paper at the first village he came to.

pairs.display: He sent it by the hand of the Captain of the Senior Bodyguard, who with such frankness showed thee the secrets and the joys of his heart?
pairs.genji_half: He sent it by the hand of the Captain of the Senior Bodyguard,
pairs.quijote_half: who with such frankness showed thee the secrets and the joys of his heart?
pairs.genji_source:
He sent it by the hand of the Captain of the
Senior Bodyguard, who was one of his retainers.
pairs.quijote_source:
Traitor, cruel, vindictive, and
perfidious, wherein had this poor wretch failed in his fidelity, who with
such frankness showed thee the secrets and the joys of his heart?

pairs.display: He was assisted by Tō no Chūjō, by the Chief Secretary of Council, let us take up the broken thread of my unhappy story.
pairs.genji_half: He was assisted by Tō no Chūjō, by the Chief Secretary of Council,
pairs.quijote_half: let us take up the broken thread of my unhappy story.
pairs.genji_source:
He was assisted by Tō no Chūjō,
by the Chief Secretary of Council, the Clerk of the Board of Rites
and a few other friends.
pairs.quijote_source:
But
laying aside these useless and unavailing reflections, let us take up the
broken thread of my unhappy story.

pairs.display: Don’t you know that Madam fathomed or understood the wavering mind and unstable nature of a woman?
pairs.genji_half: Don’t you know that Madam
pairs.quijote_half: fathomed or understood the wavering mind and unstable nature of a woman?
pairs.genji_source:
Don’t you know that Madam
is Tō no Chūjō’s own daughter?
pairs.quijote_source:
But who is
there in the world that can boast of having fathomed or understood the
wavering mind and unstable nature of a woman?

pairs.display: It would oblige me if you would first help that adored enemy of mine?
pairs.genji_half: It would oblige me if you would first help
pairs.quijote_half: that adored enemy of mine?
pairs.genji_source:
It would oblige me if you would first
help us to inform Tō no Chūjō of Madam’s arrival.
pairs.quijote_source:
why bring before me now the
incomparable beauty of that adored enemy of mine?

pairs.display: At this moment Tō no Chūjō, drawn hither by the beauty of the autumn leaves, and the simplicity of the servant?
pairs.genji_half: At this moment Tō no Chūjō, drawn hither by the beauty of the autumn leaves,
pairs.quijote_half: and the simplicity of the servant?
pairs.genji_source:
At this moment Tō no Chūjō, drawn hither by the beauty of the autumn
leaves, came into the garden for a while on his way back from the
Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
Which of the bystanders could have helped laughing to see
the madness of the master and the simplicity of the servant?

pairs.display: But it was apparent that the end was at hand; and earth its shame can see.
pairs.genji_half: But it was apparent that the end was at hand;
pairs.quijote_half: and earth its shame can see.
pairs.genji_source:
But
it was apparent that the end was at hand; the Emperor’s visits became
more and more frequent and many affecting scenes were witnessed.
pairs.quijote_source: Though none but heaven and earth its shame can see.

pairs.display: It was whispered, in strictest confidence, from ear to ear; liable to be tarnished and dimmed by every breath that touches it.
pairs.genji_half: It was whispered, in strictest confidence, from ear to ear;
pairs.quijote_half: liable to be tarnished and dimmed by every breath that touches it.
pairs.genji_source:
It was whispered, in strictest confidence, from
ear to ear; before long it had reached the Palace, and was finally
recounted to the Emperor himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
A virtuous woman, too, is
like a mirror, of clear shining crystal, liable to be tarnished and
dimmed by every breath that touches it.

pairs.display: Next day, though it so happened that a large number of ladies-in-waiting could have suspected it was a pretence.
pairs.genji_half: Next day, though it so happened that a large number of ladies-in-waiting
pairs.quijote_half: could have suspected it was a pretence.
pairs.genji_source:
Next day, though it
so happened that a large number of ladies-in-waiting and other young
people had just returned from their holidays, Murasaki sent specially
for old Ukon, who was delighted by this compliment.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short he contrived to put so good a face on the reason,
or the folly, of his absence that no one could have suspected it was a
pretence.

pairs.display: But of these things he perceived he was to come out of it wearied and vanquished.
pairs.genji_half: But of these things
pairs.quijote_half: he perceived he was to come out of it wearied and vanquished.
pairs.genji_source:
But of these things no
echo reached Fujitsubo’s house.
pairs.quijote_source:
Lothario replied that now he had begun he would carry on the undertaking
to the end, though he perceived he was to come out of it wearied and
vanquished.

pairs.display: His agitation upon being admitted to Fujitsubo was not indeed such as it would have been some years ago; and new lovers were unable to conceal it.
pairs.genji_half: His agitation upon being admitted to Fujitsubo was not indeed such as it would have been some years ago;
pairs.quijote_half: and new lovers were unable to conceal it.
pairs.genji_source:
His agitation upon being admitted to Fujitsubo was not indeed such as
it would have been some years ago; but the meeting was an affecting one
and they had much to discuss together.
pairs.quijote_source:
Leonela alone knew of her mistress’s
weakness, for the two false friends and new lovers were unable to conceal
it.

pairs.display: These were considered the cleverest women of the day, and Fujitsubo knew not what course to take to undo the mischief or find some ready escape from it.
pairs.genji_half: These were considered the cleverest women of the day, and Fujitsubo
pairs.quijote_half: knew not what course to take to undo the mischief or find some ready escape from it.
pairs.genji_source:
These were considered the cleverest women of the day, and Fujitsubo
promised herself very good entertainment from such an interchange of
wit and knowledge as their rivalry was likely to afford.
pairs.quijote_source:
He cursed his want of sense, condemned his hasty resolution, and
knew not what course to take to undo the mischief or find some ready
escape from it.

pairs.display: And yet Ukon had care to do what he saw to be needful.
pairs.genji_half: And yet Ukon had
pairs.quijote_half: care to do what he saw to be needful.
pairs.genji_source: And yet Ukon had no fears for Yūgao’s daughter.
pairs.quijote_source:
Lothario pressed her to explain her intention fully, so that
he might with more certainty and precaution take care to do what he saw
to be needful.

pairs.display: Wife of the young Emperor to the loss of your reputation and good name.
pairs.genji_half: Wife of the young Emperor
pairs.quijote_half: to the loss of your reputation and good name.
pairs.genji_source:
Wife of the young Emperor Suzaku.

 I.e. in a monastery.
pairs.quijote_source:
for whichever you mean to do, it will lead to the
loss of your reputation and good name.

pairs.display: Moreover Suzaku was at the time in very bad health, and scornfully rejected and rebuked?
pairs.genji_half: Moreover Suzaku was at the time in very bad health,
pairs.quijote_half: and scornfully rejected and rebuked?
pairs.genji_source:
Moreover Suzaku was at the time in very bad health, and if
he should fail to recover, his dependants might be left in a precarious
position.
pairs.quijote_source:
When were not thy
professions of love sternly and scornfully rejected and rebuked?

pairs.display: As a matter of fact, and promised on his own part to aid him in raising a monument so glorious.
pairs.genji_half: As a matter of fact,
pairs.quijote_half: and promised on his own part to aid him in raising a monument so glorious.
pairs.genji_source:
As a matter of fact, Suzaku now
cares very little about such matters.
pairs.quijote_source:
Lothario commended his
purpose, and promised on his own part to aid him in raising a monument so
glorious.

pairs.display: This extravagant magnificence, besides relieving Suzaku’s feelings, in a thousand ways he became the author of his own dishonour, while he believed he was insuring his happiness.
pairs.genji_half: This extravagant magnificence, besides relieving Suzaku’s feelings,
pairs.quijote_half: in a thousand ways he became the author of his own dishonour, while he believed he was insuring his happiness.
pairs.genji_source:
This extravagant magnificence,
besides relieving Suzaku’s feelings, had another very definite object.
pairs.quijote_source:
But the befooled Anselmo said he would on no account
allow such a thing, and so in a thousand ways he became the author of his
own dishonour, while he believed he was insuring his happiness.

pairs.display: It was particularly intended to annoy the lady’s guardian, but none of them could give him any explanation.
pairs.genji_half: It was particularly intended to annoy the lady’s guardian,
pairs.quijote_half: but none of them could give him any explanation.
pairs.genji_source:
It was particularly intended to annoy the lady’s guardian, to whom, as
Suzaku very well knew, the contents of these packages would immediately
be shown.
pairs.quijote_source:
He asked the servants of the house about
her, but none of them could give him any explanation.

pairs.display: Judging from his own experience he knew that Suzaku’s complete lack of employment, opportunities for the contrivance of this complication of misfortune.
pairs.genji_half: Judging from his own experience he knew that Suzaku’s complete lack of employment,
pairs.quijote_half: opportunities for the contrivance of this complication of misfortune.
pairs.genji_source:
Judging from his own experience he knew that
Suzaku’s complete lack of employment, now that he had resigned
all his official duties, would gravely aggravate the case.
pairs.quijote_source:
After long reflection he resolved at last to go to his friend’s village,
where he had been staying when he afforded opportunities for the
contrivance of this complication of misfortune.

pairs.display: Fortunately the cherry blossom was unusually early this year and alone, for he desired this, and even that the door should be locked.
pairs.genji_half: Fortunately the cherry blossom was unusually early this year
pairs.quijote_half: and alone, for he desired this, and even that the door should be locked.
pairs.genji_source:
Fortunately the cherry blossom was unusually
early this year and in Suzaku’s gardens it already made a delightful
show.
pairs.quijote_source:
His wish was complied with and he was left
lying down and alone, for he desired this, and even that the door should
be locked.

pairs.display: The pavilion of the musicians was some way off, and Suzaku suggested that those about him should send the horses away to the stable.
pairs.genji_half: The pavilion of the musicians was some way off, and Suzaku suggested that those about him should send
pairs.quijote_half: the horses away to the stable.
pairs.genji_source:
The pavilion of
the musicians was some way off, and Suzaku suggested that those about
him should send for their instruments and make a little music of their
own.
pairs.quijote_source:
The attendants on foot then led the
horses away to the stable.

pairs.display: The Procession left the Suzaku gate at the hour of the Hare, a disposition by no means advantageous to a married man who has children to succeed to his name and position.
pairs.genji_half: The Procession left the Suzaku gate at the hour of the Hare,
pairs.quijote_half: a disposition by no means advantageous to a married man who has children to succeed to his name and position.
pairs.genji_source:
The
Procession left the Suzaku gate at the hour of the Hare, turning
to the right when it reached the Great Highway of the Fifth Ward.
pairs.quijote_source:
My father
went beyond liberality and bordered on prodigality, a disposition by no
means advantageous to a married man who has children to succeed to his
name and position.

pairs.display: Knowing that the maple leaves would be particularly lovely at this season, above all when surrounded by a host of determined enemies in their own country?
pairs.genji_half: Knowing that the maple leaves would be particularly lovely at this season,
pairs.quijote_half: above all when surrounded by a host of determined enemies in their own country?
pairs.genji_source:
Knowing that the maple leaves would be
particularly lovely at this season, he invited the ex-Emperor Suzaku to
accompany him.
pairs.quijote_source:
And how
is it possible to help losing a stronghold that is not relieved, above
all when surrounded by a host of determined enemies in their own country?

pairs.display: Suzaku was deeply moved at hearing it and muttering to himself as he translated it.
pairs.genji_half: Suzaku was deeply moved at hearing
pairs.quijote_half: it and muttering to himself as he translated it.
pairs.genji_source:
Suzaku
was deeply moved at hearing the familiar tones of Uda no Hōshi.
pairs.quijote_source:
He opened it and remained some time examining it and muttering
to himself as he translated it.

pairs.display: But Suzaku regarded him as weak, and at the end of two hours came to himself.
pairs.genji_half: But Suzaku regarded him as weak,
pairs.quijote_half: and at the end of two hours came to himself.
pairs.genji_source:
But Suzaku regarded him as weak,
frivolous, irresponsible; and there were stories....
pairs.quijote_source:
We turned him upon his face and he voided a great quantity
of water, and at the end of two hours came to himself.

pairs.display: That night at any rate, he thought, matters might be left as they were, so much had the sea now got up.
pairs.genji_half: That night at any rate, he thought, matters might be left as they were,
pairs.quijote_half: so much had the sea now got up.
pairs.genji_source:
That night at any rate, he thought,
matters might be left as they were, and he made no reference to his
conversation with Suzaku.
pairs.quijote_source:
For us, however, it was not the resting-place of
the wicked woman but a haven of safety for our relief, so much had the
sea now got up.

pairs.display: Tō no Ben and Senji, although he would not accept any of their generous offers.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Ben and Senji,
pairs.quijote_half: although he would not accept any of their generous offers.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Ben and Senji,
two officials of the Imperial Purse, was due to Suzaku’s instigation.
pairs.quijote_source:
For all this the captive returned thanks very courteously, although
he would not accept any of their generous offers.

pairs.display: He ought at this very time to have been supervising the arrangements and the ladies, who supped by themselves in their own room.
pairs.genji_half: He ought at this very time to have been supervising the arrangements
pairs.quijote_half: and the ladies, who supped by themselves in their own room.
pairs.genji_source:
He ought at this very
time to have been supervising the arrangements for the celebration of
Suzaku’s fiftieth birthday.
pairs.quijote_source:
By this time supper was ready, and they all took their seats at the
table, except the captive, and the ladies, who supped by themselves in
their own room.

pairs.display: No one owes more to Suzaku than I do; and deep silence reigned all through the inn.
pairs.genji_half: No one owes more to Suzaku than I do;
pairs.quijote_half: and deep silence reigned all through the inn.
pairs.genji_source:
No one owes more to Suzaku than I do; but I am afraid that, had
not my father reminded me, I should have entirely forgotten about his
fiftieth birthday.
pairs.quijote_source: With this they fell asleep, and deep silence reigned all through the inn.

pairs.display: A messenger was sent to Suzaku’s mountain temple, oh luminary of the three faces!
pairs.genji_half: A messenger was sent to Suzaku’s mountain temple,
pairs.quijote_half: oh luminary of the three faces!
pairs.genji_source:
A messenger was sent
to Suzaku’s mountain temple, it being thought right he should know
that she was continually asking for him.
pairs.quijote_source: Give me tidings of her, oh luminary of the three faces!

pairs.display: It was now nearing dawn, and as Suzaku wished to be back in his monastery before daylight, my eyes were filled with tears.
pairs.genji_half: It was now nearing dawn, and as Suzaku wished to be back in his monastery before daylight,
pairs.quijote_half: my eyes were filled with tears.
pairs.genji_source:
It was now nearing
dawn, and as Suzaku wished to be back in his monastery before daylight,
it was necessary to act at once.
pairs.quijote_source:
She knows nothing more of my passion than what she may have
learned from having sometimes seen from a distance that my eyes were
filled with tears.

pairs.display: I do not want Suzaku to think that I have however wise it might be!
pairs.genji_half: I do not want Suzaku to think that I have
pairs.quijote_half: however wise it might be!
pairs.genji_source:
I do not want
Suzaku to think that I have left her with no one to keep an eye upon
her.’
pairs.quijote_source:
Why, this is a thing that would astonish a whole university,
however wise it might be!

pairs.display: It reached Suzaku’s ears that she intended to stay at Ono that their law is their sword, their charter their prowess, and their edicts their will?
pairs.genji_half: It reached Suzaku’s ears that she intended to stay at Ono
pairs.quijote_half: that their law is their sword, their charter their prowess, and their edicts their will?
pairs.genji_source:
It reached Suzaku’s ears that she intended to stay at Ono and become
a nun.
pairs.quijote_source:
Who was he that did not know that knights-errant are
independent of all jurisdictions, that their law is their sword, their
charter their prowess, and their edicts their will?

pairs.display: Where Murasaki would not yield herself up wholly to his will and pleasure?
pairs.genji_half: Where Murasaki would not
pairs.quijote_half: yield herself up wholly to his will and pleasure?
pairs.genji_source: Where Murasaki would not be likely to come.
pairs.quijote_source:
What
damsel was not enamoured of him and did not yield herself up wholly to
his will and pleasure?

pairs.display: Next day the weather was wretched, and while storms of snow swept a sullen sky he sat with Murasaki, as he had yesterday, before they caged him.
pairs.genji_half: Next day the weather was wretched, and while storms of snow swept a sullen sky he sat with Murasaki,
pairs.quijote_half: as he had yesterday, before they caged him.
pairs.genji_source:
Next day the weather was wretched, and while storms of snow swept a
sullen sky he sat with Murasaki, laying plans for the future, and
recalling many episodes of their common past.
pairs.quijote_source:
He is in his full
senses, he eats and he drinks, and he has his calls like other men and as
he had yesterday, before they caged him.

pairs.display: The girl had grown very good-looking, and Murasaki, to whom her existence had once been a reminder of unpleasant things, was satisfied by getting fame though it was infamy.
pairs.genji_half: The girl had grown very good-looking, and Murasaki, to whom her existence had once been a reminder of unpleasant things,
pairs.quijote_half: was satisfied by getting fame though it was infamy.
pairs.genji_source:
The girl had grown very good-looking, and Murasaki,
to whom her existence had once been a reminder of unpleasant things, no
longer felt in her society anything but the most unfeigned pleasure.
pairs.quijote_source:
The poet did as she bade him, and left her without a shred
of reputation, and she was satisfied by getting fame though it was
infamy.

pairs.display: Yūgiri was certainly, as regards birth and general advantages, more than the equal of Kumoi; indeed, attributing to one saint the miracles of another!
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri was certainly, as regards birth and general advantages, more than the equal of Kumoi; indeed,
pairs.quijote_half: attributing to one saint the miracles of another!
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri was
certainly, as regards birth and general advantages, more than the equal
of Kumoi; indeed, she could not conceive that any lady would not feel
proud to have him as her husband.
pairs.quijote_source:
What
apocryphal, ill-devised incidents, attributing to one saint the miracles
of another!

pairs.display: Meanwhile Kumoi, convinced that Yūgiri’s engagement to the Nakatsukasa girl would soon be announced, cannot keep quiet?
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile Kumoi, convinced that Yūgiri’s engagement to the Nakatsukasa girl would soon be announced,
pairs.quijote_half: cannot keep quiet?
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile Kumoi, convinced that Yūgiri’s engagement to the Nakatsukasa
girl would soon be announced, was doing her best to wipe out from her
thoughts all memory of the lover who had betrayed her.
pairs.quijote_source:
But what else can it be except that you are a she, and cannot
keep quiet?

pairs.display: Despite all the precautions of her family Kumoi’s early friendship with Yūgiri had become known, and at the age of sixteen she was most lovely.
pairs.genji_half: Despite all the precautions of her family Kumoi’s early friendship with Yūgiri had become known,
pairs.quijote_half: and at the age of sixteen she was most lovely.
pairs.genji_source:
Despite all the precautions of her family
Kumoi’s early friendship with Yūgiri had become known, and innocent
though it had been, she would inevitably share in the discredit
which attaches to the jilted.
pairs.quijote_source:
As a child she was
beautiful, she continued to grow in beauty, and at the age of sixteen she
was most lovely.

pairs.display: Lady Rokujō’s daughter by her late husband Prince Zembō of little substance and less worth.
pairs.genji_half: Lady Rokujō’s daughter by her late husband Prince Zembō
pairs.quijote_half: of little substance and less worth.
pairs.genji_source:
Lady Rokujō’s daughter by her late
husband Prince Zembō was chosen to be the new Vestal Virgin at Ise.
pairs.quijote_source:
To-day he would appear in one gay dress, to-morrow in another; but all
flimsy and gaudy, of little substance and less worth.

pairs.display: Rokujō’s old palace in the Sixth Ward in which the licentiate had entered the house given to him.
pairs.genji_half: Rokujō’s old palace in the Sixth Ward
pairs.quijote_half: in which the licentiate had entered the house given to him.
pairs.genji_source:
Rokujō’s old palace in the Sixth Ward had been
admirably repaired and redecorated, and life there was in these days
by no means intolerable.
pairs.quijote_source:
Yielding to this conviction, the worthy chaplain
begged the governor to have the clothes in which the licentiate had
entered the house given to him.

pairs.display: With these memories were blended others; so they went upstairs, and with them some of those who were present.
pairs.genji_half: With these memories were blended others;
pairs.quijote_half: so they went upstairs, and with them some of those who were present.
pairs.genji_source:
With these
memories were blended others; thoughts, for example, of her mother Lady
Rokujō and of the long exile which they had shared.
pairs.quijote_source:
The chaplain said he would go with him to see what madmen
there were in the house; so they went upstairs, and with them some of
those who were present.

pairs.display: It did not escape the notice of Princess Ōmiya’s gentlewomen that Chūjō was anxious to keep Yūgiri through a sieve.
pairs.genji_half: It did not escape the notice of Princess Ōmiya’s gentlewomen that Chūjō was anxious to keep Yūgiri
pairs.quijote_half: through a sieve.
pairs.genji_source:
It did not escape
the notice of Princess Ōmiya’s gentlewomen that Chūjō was anxious to
keep Yūgiri and his little daughter as far as possible apart.
pairs.quijote_source: how blind is he who cannot see through a sieve.

pairs.display: Quite unconscious of all the fuss that had been going on at the Great Hall, reviving in itself the happy time when the order of knight-errantry was in the field.
pairs.genji_half: Quite unconscious of all the fuss that had been going on at the Great Hall,
pairs.quijote_half: reviving in itself the happy time when the order of knight-errantry was in the field.
pairs.genji_source:
Quite unconscious of all the fuss that had been going on at the Great
Hall, Yūgiri a few days afterwards again presented himself at his
grandmother’s apartments.
pairs.quijote_source:
My
only endeavour is to convince the world of the mistake it makes in not
reviving in itself the happy time when the order of knight-errantry was
in the field.

pairs.display: You and his Excellency may take it from me that you are mightily mistaken.
pairs.genji_half: You and his Excellency may take it from me that
pairs.quijote_half: you are mightily mistaken.
pairs.genji_source:
You and his Excellency may take
it from me that Yūgiri is going to be the finest gentleman in the
land....’
pairs.quijote_source:
He has carried me all over the world, and you are mightily
mistaken.

pairs.display: When he had played for a while, a dozen lace-bobbins dares to wag her tongue and criticise the histories of knights-errant?
pairs.genji_half: When he had played for a while,
pairs.quijote_half: a dozen lace-bobbins dares to wag her tongue and criticise the histories of knights-errant?
pairs.genji_source:
When he had played for a while,
Yūgiri began to improvise on his flute in the Banshiki mode.
pairs.quijote_source:
can it be that a young hussy that hardly
knows how to handle a dozen lace-bobbins dares to wag her tongue and
criticise the histories of knights-errant?

pairs.display: Yūgiri’s own neighbour, for example—the Lady from the Village of Falling is the gift of great geniuses.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri’s own neighbour, for example—the Lady from the Village of Falling
pairs.quijote_half: is the gift of great geniuses.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri’s
own neighbour, for example—the Lady from the Village of Falling
Flowers—might easily be by this time in a pitiable state of panic.
pairs.quijote_source:
To give expression to humour, and write in a strain of
graceful pleasantry, is the gift of great geniuses.

pairs.display: Despite his friendship for the little Princess, he who would make people take him for a fool, must not be one.
pairs.genji_half: Despite his friendship for the little Princess,
pairs.quijote_half: he who would make people take him for a fool, must not be one.
pairs.genji_source:
Despite his friendship for the little Princess, Yūgiri had shown no
interest in the recent proceedings at the New Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
The cleverest
character in comedy is the clown, for he who would make people take him
for a fool, must not be one.

pairs.display: Yūgiri’s new estate required more spacious quarters than were available in his father’s palace, the departure should take place in three days from that time.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri’s new estate required more spacious quarters than were available in his father’s palace,
pairs.quijote_half: the departure should take place in three days from that time.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri’s new estate required more spacious quarters than were available
in his father’s palace, and soon after his promotion he moved into
his grandmother’s old house in the Third Ward.
pairs.quijote_source:
They agreed upon this, and that the departure should take place in three
days from that time.

pairs.display: But I heard my servitors saying you going to look for her?
pairs.genji_half: But I heard my servitors saying
pairs.quijote_half: you going to look for her?
pairs.genji_source:
But I heard my
servitors saying to one another: “There goes Prince Yūgiri.
pairs.quijote_source: Well, and for whom are you going to look for her?

pairs.display: In point of fact the arrangements for the service of the Forty-ninth Day were made almost entirely by Yūgiri, it would be just and right
pairs.genji_half: In point of fact the arrangements for the service of the Forty-ninth Day were made almost entirely by Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: it would be just and right
pairs.genji_source:
In point of fact the arrangements for the service of the Forty-ninth
Day were made almost entirely by Yūgiri, who took the utmost pains in
planning every detail.
pairs.quijote_source: And does it strike you that it would be just and right

pairs.display: When supper was over and everything had quieted down, robed and adorned--in fact, like what she is.
pairs.genji_half: When supper was over and everything had quieted down,
pairs.quijote_half: robed and adorned--in fact, like what she is.
pairs.genji_source:
When supper was over and everything had quieted down, Yūgiri went to
Shōshō no Kimi and commenced his usual appeal.
pairs.quijote_source:
Come, señor, push on, and you will see the princess our mistress
coming, robed and adorned--in fact, like what she is.

pairs.display: Yūgiri was convinced that if he presented himself at the Great Hall, and saw the three village lasses close at hand.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri was convinced that if he presented himself at the Great Hall,
pairs.quijote_half: and saw the three village lasses close at hand.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri was convinced that if he presented
himself at the Great Hall, his father-in-law would behave in the most
unreasonable way.
pairs.quijote_source:
By this time they had cleared the wood, and saw the three village lasses
close at hand.

pairs.display: Yūgiri had fallen in love with her preventing their companion from going on.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri had fallen in love with her
pairs.quijote_half: preventing their companion from going on.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri had fallen in love
with her when she was at the Palace as a Gosechi dancer.
pairs.quijote_source:
The country girls, at the same time, were
astonished to see these two men, so different in appearance, on their
knees, preventing their companion from going on.

pairs.display: Some of the professors and doctors whose own part in the ceremony was over had also left the palace, emperors, pontiffs, knights, ladies, and divers other personages were introduced?
pairs.genji_half: Some of the professors and doctors whose own part in the ceremony was over had also left the palace,
pairs.quijote_half: emperors, pontiffs, knights, ladies, and divers other personages were introduced?
pairs.genji_source:
Some of the professors and doctors whose own part in
the ceremony was over had also left the palace, and Genji now brought
them back and made them compose poem after poem.
pairs.quijote_source:
Come, tell me, hast thou not seen a
play acted in which kings, emperors, pontiffs, knights, ladies, and
divers other personages were introduced?

pairs.display: Genji happened to be there when Hyōbukyō arrived and that what seems to be abuse in the expression is high praise?
pairs.genji_half: Genji happened to be there when Hyōbukyō arrived
pairs.quijote_half: and that what seems to be abuse in the expression is high praise?
pairs.genji_source:
Genji happened to be there when Hyōbukyō arrived and they fell
to talking of old times.
pairs.quijote_source:
how well he has done it!’
and that what seems to be abuse in the expression is high praise?

pairs.display: It would have been impossible for anyone to speak more kindly than Genji had just spoken; the other did no more than bring it to his nose.
pairs.genji_half: It would have been impossible for anyone to speak more kindly than Genji had just spoken;
pairs.quijote_half: the other did no more than bring it to his nose.
pairs.genji_source:
It would have been impossible for anyone to speak more kindly than
Genji had just spoken; but Kashiwagi, though fully sensible of this
fact, still felt as uncomfortable as when he first entered the room.
pairs.quijote_source:
One of them tried it with the tip of his tongue, the
other did no more than bring it to his nose.

pairs.display: Thus he remonstrated; and Genji, though at first he had been somewhat put out by finding that he was not alone, had a nice taste in devising things of the sort.
pairs.genji_half: Thus he remonstrated; and Genji, though at first he had been somewhat put out by finding that he was not alone,
pairs.quijote_half: had a nice taste in devising things of the sort.
pairs.genji_source:
Thus he remonstrated; and Genji, though at first he had been somewhat
put out by finding that he was not alone, when he recognized Tō no
Chūjō could not help being rather amused.
pairs.quijote_source:
She replied that it was a beneficiary of the town who had a
nice taste in devising things of the sort.

pairs.display: I heard yesterday that to-day they are choosing the dancers and musicians to break out in ribald satires or soulless sonnets.
pairs.genji_half: I heard yesterday that to-day they are choosing the dancers and musicians
pairs.quijote_half: to break out in ribald satires or soulless sonnets.
pairs.genji_source:
I heard yesterday that to-day they are choosing the dancers and
musicians for the celebrations of the Emperor’s visit to the Suzaku-in
and I am going home to tell my father of this.
pairs.quijote_source:
He that possesses her
must keep her within bounds, not permitting her to break out in ribald
satires or soulless sonnets.

pairs.display: The child looked so exquisitely beautiful as it lay crowing to itself and smiling and at such a time!
pairs.genji_half: The child looked so exquisitely beautiful as it lay crowing to itself and smiling
pairs.quijote_half: and at such a time!
pairs.genji_source:
The child looked so exquisitely beautiful as it lay
crowing to itself and smiling that, hideous as the situation was,
Genji could not help feeling glad it was thought to be like him.
pairs.quijote_source:
to
me whelps of lions, and at such a time!

pairs.display: At such a time it was impossible for Genji to leave her and there were many who knew he was under a delusion.
pairs.genji_half: At such a time it was impossible for Genji to leave her and there were many who
pairs.quijote_half: knew he was under a delusion.
pairs.genji_source:
At such a time it was impossible for Genji to leave her and there were
many who though his feelings had not in reality cooled towards them
felt that they were being neglected.
pairs.quijote_source:
The gentleman
in return entreated him to reflect, for he knew he was under a delusion.

pairs.display: This was all, and it was written hastily, but to Genji giving up his insane project.
pairs.genji_half: This was all, and it was written hastily, but to Genji
pairs.quijote_half: giving up his insane project.
pairs.genji_source:
This was
all, and it was written hastily, but to Genji, who for so long had
received no news from her, it gave as much pleasure as the longest and
most ingenious epistle.
pairs.quijote_source:
To these he added some further words that banished all hope of his
giving up his insane project.

pairs.display: The feeling that he had successfully insisted upon Genji’s recall quite braced him, frisking and gambolling all over the meadow.
pairs.genji_half: The feeling that he had successfully insisted upon Genji’s recall quite braced him,
pairs.quijote_half: frisking and gambolling all over the meadow.
pairs.genji_source:
The feeling that he had successfully insisted
upon Genji’s recall quite braced him, and the pain in his eyes, which
had till recently been very troublesome, now began to show signs of
improvement.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, it seemed as though mirth and
gaiety were frisking and gambolling all over the meadow.

pairs.display: The Emperor himself was greatly relieved to see that the boy was shaping so well, and he without a farthing; is there nothing else?
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor himself was greatly relieved to see that the boy was shaping so well,
pairs.quijote_half: and he without a farthing; is there nothing else?
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor himself was greatly relieved to see that the boy was
shaping so well, and he now began to prepare Lady Kōkiden for the
news that he intended to vacate the Throne.
pairs.quijote_source:
To make a grand match for himself, and he without a
farthing; is there nothing else?

pairs.display: Fortunately Murasaki no longer showed any jealousy on this score, looking for dainties in the bottom of the sea.
pairs.genji_half: Fortunately Murasaki no longer showed any jealousy on this score,
pairs.quijote_half: looking for dainties in the bottom of the sea.
pairs.genji_source:
Fortunately Murasaki no longer showed any jealousy on this
score, feeling, as it seemed, that the surrender of so exquisite a
child needed whatever recompense Genji found it in his heart to bestow.
pairs.quijote_source:
Faith, señor, it’s my opinion the poor
man should be content with what he can get, and not go looking for
dainties in the bottom of the sea.

pairs.display: Murasaki had a peculiar talent in such matters, for a good cast of the bar or a neat thrust of the sword.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki had a peculiar talent in such matters,
pairs.quijote_half: for a good cast of the bar or a neat thrust of the sword.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki had a peculiar talent in such matters,
and there was not a woman in all the world who chose her dyes with a
subtler feeling for colour, as Genji very well knew.
pairs.quijote_source:
They won’t give a pint of wine at the tavern for a good cast
of the bar or a neat thrust of the sword.

pairs.display: The attentions which his brother had hitherto paid to Tamakatsura were, he suspected, like the heaps of corn one sees on the threshing-floors.
pairs.genji_half: The attentions which his brother had hitherto paid to Tamakatsura were, he suspected,
pairs.quijote_half: like the heaps of corn one sees on the threshing-floors.
pairs.genji_source:
The attentions which his brother had hitherto paid
to Tamakatsura were, he suspected, due solely to the fact that Sochi
had accepted the current story and imagined her indeed to be Genji’s
daughter.
pairs.quijote_source:
There were, besides, piles of the
whitest bread, like the heaps of corn one sees on the threshing-floors.

pairs.display: The news that she was not Genji’s daughter hardly came as a surprise to him, all clean, brisk, and blithe.
pairs.genji_half: The news that she was not Genji’s daughter hardly came as a surprise to him,
pairs.quijote_half: all clean, brisk, and blithe.
pairs.genji_source:
The news that she was not Genji’s
daughter hardly came as a surprise to him, for his father’s manner
towards her had in the course of the last months continually aroused
his suspicion.
pairs.quijote_source:
Of cooks and cook-maids there were over fifty, all clean,
brisk, and blithe.

pairs.display: Being Murasaki’s brother, he was constantly at her apartments, and his present despondency seemed to prove that, and all lay open to view in a great chest.
pairs.genji_half: Being Murasaki’s brother, he was constantly at her apartments, and his present despondency seemed to prove that,
pairs.quijote_half: and all lay open to view in a great chest.
pairs.genji_source:
Being Murasaki’s brother,
he was constantly at her apartments, and his present despondency seemed
to prove that, according to the information to be gathered in that
quarter, Lady Tamakatsura’s fate was already decided.
pairs.quijote_source:
The spices of different kinds did not seem to have been
bought by the pound but by the quarter, and all lay open to view in a
great chest.

pairs.display: For his reply Genji chose paper of the same colour as the blossom she had sent, but abundant enough to feed an army.
pairs.genji_half: For his reply Genji chose paper of the same colour as the blossom she had sent,
pairs.quijote_half: but abundant enough to feed an army.
pairs.genji_source:
For his reply Genji chose paper of the same colour as the blossom she
had sent, and attached the letter to a spray from the aforementioned
trees in front of his own window.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, all the preparations made for the wedding were in
rustic style, but abundant enough to feed an army.

pairs.display: But even when Genji is away at the Nijō-in there are always a lot of people gathered round her devising things of the sort.
pairs.genji_half: But even when Genji is away at the Nijō-in there are always a lot of people gathered round her
pairs.quijote_half: devising things of the sort.
pairs.genji_source:
But even when Genji is
away at the Nijō-in there are always a lot of people gathered round her
curtains-of-state, and even by her chair there is generally some one in
attendance.
pairs.quijote_source:
She replied that it was a beneficiary of the town who had a
nice taste in devising things of the sort.

pairs.display: Of Tō no Chūjō’s sons, only the two eldest, in what seemed to be a loose black coat garnished with crimson patches like flames.
pairs.genji_half: Of Tō no Chūjō’s sons, only the two eldest,
pairs.quijote_half: in what seemed to be a loose black coat garnished with crimson patches like flames.
pairs.genji_source:
Of Tō
no Chūjō’s sons, only the two eldest, Kashiwagi and Kōbai, had been
let into the secret.
pairs.quijote_source:
At these words all turned round, and
perceived that the speaker was a man clad in what seemed to be a loose
black coat garnished with crimson patches like flames.

pairs.display: Master Kashiwagi here thought it was a very clever thing to take a poor girl away from her village and in his hand he held a long staff.
pairs.genji_half: Master Kashiwagi here thought it was a very clever thing to take a poor girl away from her village
pairs.quijote_half: and in his hand he held a long staff.
pairs.genji_source:
Master Kashiwagi here thought it was a very clever
thing to take a poor girl away from her village and make a joke of her
in this fine house.
pairs.quijote_source:
He was crowned (as
was presently seen) with a crown of gloomy cypress, and in his hand he
held a long staff.

pairs.display: It will be remembered that Kashiwagi’s return of faintness would take his life with it.
pairs.genji_half: It will be remembered that Kashiwagi’s
pairs.quijote_half: return of faintness would take his life with it.
pairs.genji_source:
It will be remembered that Kashiwagi’s brother Kōbai was famous
for his voice.
pairs.quijote_source:
While uttering these words he showed such weakness that the bystanders
expected each return of faintness would take his life with it.

pairs.display: He was led into the house by a band as it pleases him.
pairs.genji_half: He was led into the house by a band
pairs.quijote_half: as it pleases him.
pairs.genji_source:
He was led into the house by a band of
some seven or eight young men, headed by Kashiwagi.
pairs.quijote_source:
Camacho is rich, and can
purchase his pleasure when, where, and as it pleases him.

pairs.display: Kashiwagi was to-day the representative of the Imperial Bodyguard, as he was very hungry.
pairs.genji_half: Kashiwagi was to-day the representative of the Imperial Bodyguard,
pairs.quijote_half: as he was very hungry.
pairs.genji_source:
Kashiwagi was
to-day the representative of the Imperial Bodyguard, and it was at
his father’s house that the gentlemen who now accompanied him had that
morning assembled.
pairs.quijote_source: He then begged them to give him something to eat, as he was very hungry.

pairs.display: Three days after Nyosan’s Initiation for the inn, which they reached a little before nightfall.
pairs.genji_half: Three days after Nyosan’s Initiation
pairs.quijote_half: for the inn, which they reached a little before nightfall.
pairs.genji_source:
Three days after Nyosan’s Initiation the ex-Emperor received the
tonsure.
pairs.quijote_source:
Accordingly they
mounted and all three took the direct road for the inn, which they
reached a little before nightfall.

pairs.display: But he has had very little experience, proceeding at a rapid pace, and beating a mule loaded with lances and halberds.
pairs.genji_half: But he has had very little experience,
pairs.quijote_half: proceeding at a rapid pace, and beating a mule loaded with lances and halberds.
pairs.genji_source:
But he has
had very little experience, and I doubt whether in any case I could
recommend him as sole protector to a girl in Nyosan’s position.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this point they saw approaching the spot where they stood a man on
foot, proceeding at a rapid pace, and beating a mule loaded with lances
and halberds.

pairs.display: About the middle of the second if they liked cheap water she would give it with great pleasure.
pairs.genji_half: About the middle of the second
pairs.quijote_half: if they liked cheap water she would give it with great pleasure.
pairs.genji_source: About the middle of the second month Nyosan moved into the New Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
She replied that her master had
none, but that if they liked cheap water she would give it with great
pleasure.

pairs.display: When night came he sent a note to Nyosan’s quarters saying that the snowy weather had upset him in the thickest part of the forest, they found him devoured by wolves?
pairs.genji_half: When night came he sent a note to Nyosan’s quarters saying that the snowy weather had upset him
pairs.quijote_half: in the thickest part of the forest, they found him devoured by wolves?
pairs.genji_source:
When night came he sent a note
to Nyosan’s quarters saying that the snowy weather had upset him and
that it was thought best he should remain where he was.
pairs.quijote_source:
How could the poor ill-starred brute have answered,
when, in the thickest part of the forest, they found him devoured by
wolves?

pairs.display: Evidently Nyosan’s people pointed out a place where the show might be fixed, which was done at once.
pairs.genji_half: Evidently Nyosan’s people
pairs.quijote_half: pointed out a place where the show might be fixed, which was done at once.
pairs.genji_source:
Evidently Nyosan’s
people were not best pleased with the excuse.
pairs.quijote_source:
As soon as he heard
this, the landlord, delighted beyond measure, pointed out a place where
the show might be fixed, which was done at once.

pairs.display: Meanwhile she on her side felt that he had treated Nyosan almost contemptuously, such as, perhaps, never was seen.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile she on her side felt that he had treated Nyosan almost contemptuously,
pairs.quijote_half: such as, perhaps, never was seen.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile she on her side felt that he had
treated Nyosan almost contemptuously, and wished that he would take the
trouble to manage things a little better....
pairs.quijote_source:
Observe, too, a new incident which now occurs,
such as, perhaps, never was seen.

pairs.display: After a short conversation she proceeded and it is for wise men to reserve themselves for better occasions.
pairs.genji_half: After a short conversation she proceeded
pairs.quijote_half: and it is for wise men to reserve themselves for better occasions.
pairs.genji_source:
After a short conversation she proceeded through the dividing door
into Nyosan’s apartments.
pairs.quijote_source:
When the brave man flees, treachery is manifest and it is for wise men to
reserve themselves for better occasions.

pairs.display: Henceforward they met very often, or when that was impossible, in the house of the man that has been hanged?
pairs.genji_half: Henceforward they met very often, or when that was impossible,
pairs.quijote_half: in the house of the man that has been hanged?
pairs.genji_source:
Henceforward they met very often, or when that was impossible,
exchanged little notes concerned exclusively with the behaviour of
Nyosan’s dolls and the fortunes or reverses of her other toys.
pairs.quijote_source:
Where hast thou learned that it is well done to mention
the rope in the house of the man that has been hanged?

pairs.display: At this moment the cat gave a frenzied scream, and Nyosan turned her head, honey is not for the mouth of the ass.
pairs.genji_half: At this moment the cat gave a frenzied scream, and Nyosan turned her head,
pairs.quijote_half: honey is not for the mouth of the ass.
pairs.genji_source:
At this moment
the cat gave a frenzied scream, and Nyosan turned her head, revealing
as she did so a singularly unconcerned and confident young face.
pairs.quijote_source:
Well, as thou thyself hast said before now, honey is not for the
mouth of the ass.

pairs.display: The cat was now straying at large, thou soul of a tame mouse?
pairs.genji_half: The cat was now straying at large,
pairs.quijote_half: thou soul of a tame mouse?
pairs.genji_source:
The
cat was now straying at large, and to distract his thoughts Kashiwagi
called it to him.
pairs.quijote_source: Who pursues or molests thee, thou soul of a tame mouse?

pairs.display: By the way, I think very much the opposite of what he contemplated.
pairs.genji_half: By the way, I think
pairs.quijote_half: very much the opposite of what he contemplated.
pairs.genji_source:
By the way, I think they said
that it is your sister Nyosan’s....’
pairs.quijote_source:
Fortune, however, ordered
matters after a fashion very much the opposite of what he contemplated.

pairs.display: But as a matter of fact he had already recognized Nyosan’s cat, on a pure white palfrey or hackney caparisoned with green trappings and a silver-mounted side-saddle.
pairs.genji_half: But as a matter of fact he had already recognized Nyosan’s cat,
pairs.quijote_half: on a pure white palfrey or hackney caparisoned with green trappings and a silver-mounted side-saddle.
pairs.genji_source:
But as a matter of fact he had already
recognized Nyosan’s cat, and was soon fondling it upon his lap.
pairs.quijote_source:
Coming closer, he distinguished among them a lady of graceful
mien, on a pure white palfrey or hackney caparisoned with green trappings
and a silver-mounted side-saddle.

pairs.display: But Kashiwagi was under no delusion that this alliance would in any way console him but the way of washing they have here is worse than doing penance.
pairs.genji_half: But Kashiwagi was under no delusion that this alliance would in any way console him
pairs.quijote_half: but the way of washing they have here is worse than doing penance.
pairs.genji_source:
But Kashiwagi was under no delusion that this alliance
would in any way console him for his failure with the younger sister.
pairs.quijote_source:
The customs of countries and
princes’ palaces are only good so long as they give no annoyance; but the
way of washing they have here is worse than doing penance.

pairs.display: And who is Nyosan that you should think it sacrilege that one of the carts with the creaking wheels reached the spot.
pairs.genji_half: And who is Nyosan that you should think it sacrilege
pairs.quijote_half: that one of the carts with the creaking wheels reached the spot.
pairs.genji_source:
And who is Nyosan that you
should think it sacrilege for any one to dream of her?
pairs.quijote_source:
This was done, and he came to
himself by the time that one of the carts with the creaking wheels
reached the spot.

pairs.display: Seeing that she was now awake, as they call it, reaching to the feet, while the head was covered with a black veil.
pairs.genji_half: Seeing that she was now awake,
pairs.quijote_half: as they call it, reaching to the feet, while the head was covered with a black veil.
pairs.genji_source:
Seeing that she was now awake, Kashiwagi began explaining his
presence.
pairs.quijote_source:
Beside her was a figure in a robe of
state, as they call it, reaching to the feet, while the head was covered
with a black veil.

pairs.display: But in the long run he would have to know, and had given himself five lashes overnight.
pairs.genji_half: But in the long run he would have to know,
pairs.quijote_half: and had given himself five lashes overnight.
pairs.genji_source:
But in the long run he would have to know,
and as soon as she was alone Nyosan burst into a flood of tears.
pairs.quijote_source: He said he had, and had given himself five lashes overnight.

pairs.display: It is nice to think of that poor care that nobody speaks evil of thee behind thy back.
pairs.genji_half: It is nice to think of that poor
pairs.quijote_half: care that nobody speaks evil of thee behind thy back.
pairs.genji_source:
It is nice to think of that poor
little Princess Nyosan coming into her own again.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thou art a governor’s wife; take care that nobody speaks evil
of thee behind thy back.

pairs.display: So saying, she unfolded the letter men clad in mourning robes so long and flowing that they trailed upon the ground.
pairs.genji_half: So saying, she unfolded the letter
pairs.quijote_half: men clad in mourning robes so long and flowing that they trailed upon the ground.
pairs.genji_source:
So saying, she unfolded the letter and had just
placed it in Nyosan’s hands, when there was a noise of footsteps.
pairs.quijote_source:
While they were still in uncertainty they saw advancing towards them
through the garden two men clad in mourning robes so long and flowing
that they trailed upon the ground.

pairs.display: Her heart beating wildly, through which might be descried a very long beard as white as snow.
pairs.genji_half: Her heart beating wildly,
pairs.quijote_half: through which might be descried a very long beard as white as snow.
pairs.genji_source:
Her
heart beating wildly, Nyosan stuffed it under the mattress of the couch
upon which she lay.
pairs.quijote_source:
He had his face covered with a transparent black veil, through
which might be descried a very long beard as white as snow.

pairs.display: But this seduction of Nyosan costs them little to make promises they have no intention or power of fulfilling.
pairs.genji_half: But this seduction of Nyosan
pairs.quijote_half: costs them little to make promises they have no intention or power of fulfilling.
pairs.genji_source:
But this seduction of Nyosan
was an entirely different matter.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then it is they
give a loose to their pens, for it costs them little to make promises
they have no intention or power of fulfilling.

pairs.display: That any one who belonged to him should turn elsewhere when there is so much to be said about my own?
pairs.genji_half: That any one who belonged to him should turn elsewhere
pairs.quijote_half: when there is so much to be said about my own?
pairs.genji_source:
That
any one who belonged to him should turn elsewhere for affection—and
to a person such as Kashiwagi—was more than he could be expected to
endure.
pairs.quijote_source:
What madness or folly leads me to speak
of the faults of others, when there is so much to be said about my own?

pairs.display: But he spoke to Kashiwagi for the delight and amusement of the dwellers on earth!
pairs.genji_half: But he spoke to Kashiwagi
pairs.quijote_half: for the delight and amusement of the dwellers on earth!
pairs.genji_source: But he spoke to Kashiwagi without a trace of coldness or disapproval.
pairs.quijote_source:
All and each, may ye live countless ages for the delight
and amusement of the dwellers on earth!

pairs.display: Even if Kashiwagi only stays there till he recovers, youth would not sooner become a nun than a duenna!
pairs.genji_half: Even if Kashiwagi only stays there till he recovers,
pairs.quijote_half: youth would not sooner become a nun than a duenna!
pairs.genji_source:
Even if
Kashiwagi only stays there till he recovers, the separation will be a
most painful one for my daughter.
pairs.quijote_source:
Ill betide the jade that in the flower of her youth would
not sooner become a nun than a duenna!

pairs.display: To see him now solemnly in green ivy bearing on their shoulders a great wooden horse.
pairs.genji_half: To see him now solemnly
pairs.quijote_half: in green ivy bearing on their shoulders a great wooden horse.
pairs.genji_source:
To see
him now solemnly and endlessly discussing Kashiwagi’s symptoms with
these grim practicants was a strange and saddening spectacle.
pairs.quijote_source:
suddenly there came into the garden four wild-men
all clad in green ivy bearing on their shoulders a great wooden horse.

pairs.display: That evening Nyosan was much indisposed, they retired with easy dignity the way they came.
pairs.genji_half: That evening Nyosan was much indisposed,
pairs.quijote_half: they retired with easy dignity the way they came.
pairs.genji_source:
That evening Nyosan was much indisposed, and the more experienced
among her gentlewomen at once recognized that her delivery was at
hand.
pairs.quijote_source:
With these words, leaving Clavileño behind them, they retired with easy
dignity the way they came.

pairs.display: But should the child, when it grew up, show a striking resemblance to Kashiwagi, strolling about on the winds?
pairs.genji_half: But should the child, when it grew up, show a striking resemblance to Kashiwagi,
pairs.quijote_half: strolling about on the winds?
pairs.genji_source:
But should the child, when it grew
up, show a striking resemblance to Kashiwagi, this would be far more
likely to attract notice in a boy than in a girl.
pairs.quijote_source:
What would my
islanders say when they heard their governor was going, strolling about
on the winds?

pairs.display: To begin with, Kashiwagi had repeatedly betrayed the pitcher, a blind man could see that.
pairs.genji_half: To begin with, Kashiwagi had repeatedly betrayed
pairs.quijote_half: the pitcher, a blind man could see that.
pairs.genji_source:
To begin with, Kashiwagi had
repeatedly betrayed by signs of one sort or another the fact that he
took a particular interest in her.
pairs.quijote_source:
and then, as for that about the stone
and the pitcher, a blind man could see that.

pairs.display: For her life with Kashiwagi was such sweet stimulator of the water-coolers!
pairs.genji_half: For her life with Kashiwagi was such
pairs.quijote_half: sweet stimulator of the water-coolers!
pairs.genji_source:
For her life with Kashiwagi was such that you could call her neither
wife nor maid.
pairs.quijote_source:
O perpetual discoverer of the antipodes, torch of the world, eye of
heaven, sweet stimulator of the water-coolers!

pairs.display: Meanwhile Kashiwagi’s loss continued to be severely set on fire and extinguished again, fixed on the end of a cane.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile Kashiwagi’s loss continued to be severely
pairs.quijote_half: set on fire and extinguished again, fixed on the end of a cane.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile Kashiwagi’s loss continued to be severely felt in the country
and at Court.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now they began to warm their faces, from a distance, with tow that
could be easily set on fire and extinguished again, fixed on the end of a
cane.

pairs.display: At such moments some of the old bitterness against Kashiwagi would provoked their laughter; however, the governor’s orders were after all executed.
pairs.genji_half: At such moments some of the old bitterness against Kashiwagi would
pairs.quijote_half: provoked their laughter; however, the governor’s orders were after all executed.
pairs.genji_source:
At such moments some of the old
bitterness against Kashiwagi would for a while return.
pairs.quijote_source:
If the previous decision about the cattle dealer’s purse excited the
admiration of the bystanders, this provoked their laughter; however, the
governor’s orders were after all executed.

pairs.display: He was given a zithern, standing by his side with a whalebone wand in his hand.
pairs.genji_half: He was given a zithern,
pairs.quijote_half: standing by his side with a whalebone wand in his hand.
pairs.genji_source:
He was given a zithern, and recognized it to be
Kashiwagi’s.
pairs.quijote_source:
A personage, who it appeared
afterwards was a physician, placed himself standing by his side with a
whalebone wand in his hand.

pairs.display: He dreamt that Kashiwagi appeared to him, and taking a paper from his bosom, placed it in the governor’s hands.
pairs.genji_half: He dreamt that Kashiwagi appeared to him,
pairs.quijote_half: and taking a paper from his bosom, placed it in the governor’s hands.
pairs.genji_source:
He dreamt
that Kashiwagi appeared to him, and picking up the flute examined it
curiously.
pairs.quijote_source:
The courier came in all sweating and flurried, and taking a paper from
his bosom, placed it in the governor’s hands.

pairs.display: He allowed Kashiwagi to take it away one day after the Lezpideza Concert, and having read the contents said the matter was one to be discussed in private.
pairs.genji_half: He allowed Kashiwagi to take it away one day after the Lezpideza Concert,
pairs.quijote_half: and having read the contents said the matter was one to be discussed in private.
pairs.genji_source:
He allowed Kashiwagi to take it away one day after the Lezpideza
Concert, knowing that he was such a fine player.
pairs.quijote_source:
The new-born secretary obeyed, and having read the contents
said the matter was one to be discussed in private.

pairs.display: He began to talk about Kashiwagi, a thousand leagues off was an honest fellow and a good soul.
pairs.genji_half: He began to talk about Kashiwagi,
pairs.quijote_half: a thousand leagues off was an honest fellow and a good soul.
pairs.genji_source:
He began to talk about Kashiwagi, and she now joined in the
conversation quite calmly and happily.
pairs.quijote_source:
The farmer now came in, a well-favoured man that one might see a thousand
leagues off was an honest fellow and a good soul.

pairs.display: He could not, however, forbear from reproaching her for showing more tenderness to the memory of Kashiwagi, uttered these words, the door opened.
pairs.genji_half: He could not, however, forbear from reproaching her for showing more tenderness to the memory of Kashiwagi,
pairs.quijote_half: uttered these words, the door opened.
pairs.genji_source:
He could not, however, forbear from reproaching her for showing
more tenderness to the memory of Kashiwagi, who neglected her, than
kindness to himself, who offered her an unbounded admiration.
pairs.quijote_source:
The very instant he had uttered these words, the door
opened.

pairs.display: As messenger he chose his son Ben no Shōshō, and ladies rode behind their squires.
pairs.genji_half: As messenger he chose his son Ben no Shōshō,
pairs.quijote_half: and ladies rode behind their squires.
pairs.genji_source:
As
messenger he chose his son Ben no Shōshō, who had known the house very
well in Kashiwagi’s time, and walked straight in.
pairs.quijote_source:
for in those days they did not use coaches or chairs, as they say
they do now, and ladies rode behind their squires.

pairs.display: There was the time and they directed their steps towards the house, which was not far off.
pairs.genji_half: There was the time
pairs.quijote_half: and they directed their steps towards the house, which was not far off.
pairs.genji_source:
There was the time when Nyosan first came to
live with them.
pairs.quijote_source:
The youth thanked the governor for his kind offer to take them home, and
they directed their steps towards the house, which was not far off.

pairs.display: The celebration of Suzaku’s fiftieth birthday, again and again deferred, for servants’ wages, which were becoming recklessly exorbitant.
pairs.genji_half: The celebration of Suzaku’s fiftieth birthday, again and again deferred,
pairs.quijote_half: for servants’ wages, which were becoming recklessly exorbitant.
pairs.genji_source:
The celebration of Suzaku’s fiftieth birthday, again and again
deferred, had now been fixed for the middle of the twelfth month.
pairs.quijote_source:
He established a fixed rate for servants’ wages, which were
becoming recklessly exorbitant.

pairs.display: The Emperor could hardly contain lewd or loose songs either by day or night.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor could hardly contain
pairs.quijote_half: lewd or loose songs either by day or night.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor could hardly contain himself during the days of
waiting.
pairs.quijote_source:
He laid extremely heavy penalties upon
those who sang lewd or loose songs either by day or night.

pairs.display: The Emperor repented bitterly that he had not long ago made her a Lady-in-Waiting, and not that poor scurvy knave.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor repented bitterly that he had not long ago made her a Lady-in-Waiting,
pairs.quijote_half: and not that poor scurvy knave.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor repented bitterly that he
had not long ago made her a Lady-in-Waiting, and that was why he now
raised her rank by one degree.
pairs.quijote_source:
It’s another sort of cat they must
throw in my face, and not that poor scurvy knave.

pairs.display: In the spring an heir to the Throne was to be proclaimed caviar, and made of the eggs of fish, a great thirst-wakener.
pairs.genji_half: In the spring an heir to the Throne was to be proclaimed
pairs.quijote_half: caviar, and made of the eggs of fish, a great thirst-wakener.
pairs.genji_source:
In the
spring an heir to the Throne was to be proclaimed and the Emperor was
sorely tempted to pass over the first-born prince in favour of the
young child.
pairs.quijote_source:
They also put down a black dainty called,
they say, caviar, and made of the eggs of fish, a great thirst-wakener.

pairs.display: Again the Emperor was in great distress; waiting to see what the haste with which he was putting on his clothes meant.
pairs.genji_half: Again the Emperor was in great distress;
pairs.quijote_half: waiting to see what the haste with which he was putting on his clothes meant.
pairs.genji_source:
Again the Emperor was in great distress; and this time the
boy, being now six years old, understood what had happened and wept
bitterly.
pairs.quijote_source:
He said no more, and
in silence began to dress himself, while all watched him, waiting to see
what the haste with which he was putting on his clothes meant.

pairs.display: So she spoke to the Emperor, if he stooped and squeezed himself into a small compass.
pairs.genji_half: So she spoke to the Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: if he stooped and squeezed himself into a small compass.
pairs.genji_source:
So she
spoke to the Emperor, and he, much wondering what truth there was in
it, listened with great attention.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now he perceived on one side of the pit a hole large enough to admit
a person if he stooped and squeezed himself into a small compass.

pairs.display: The Emperor could not deny that she had been badly treated and worse bruised.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor could not deny that she
pairs.quijote_half: had been badly treated and worse bruised.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor could not deny
that she bore an astonishing resemblance to his beloved.
pairs.quijote_source:
The
duchess also embraced him, and gave orders that he should be taken good
care of, as it was plain to see he had been badly treated and worse
bruised.

pairs.display: He sounded the Emperor upon this, and found that he would be very glad to obey him in everything.
pairs.genji_half: He sounded the Emperor upon this, and found that he would be very glad to
pairs.quijote_half: obey him in everything.
pairs.genji_source:
He sounded the Emperor upon this, and found that he would be very glad
to obtain for the boy the advantage of so powerful a connection.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don
Quixote bade his excellence arrange all matters connected with the affair
as he pleased, as on his part he would obey him in everything.

pairs.display: A chamberlain now came over to the Minister and his lacquey features were revealed to public gaze.
pairs.genji_half: A chamberlain now came over to the Minister
pairs.quijote_half: and his lacquey features were revealed to public gaze.
pairs.genji_source:
A chamberlain now came over to the Minister and
brought him a summons to wait upon His Majesty immediately.
pairs.quijote_source:
They removed
it in all haste, and his lacquey features were revealed to public gaze.

pairs.display: A poem by the Chinese writer Po Chü-i about the death of Yang Kuei-fei, they were very sorry he was leaving them.
pairs.genji_half: A poem by the Chinese writer Po Chü-i about the death of Yang Kuei-fei,
pairs.quijote_half: they were very sorry he was leaving them.
pairs.genji_source:
A poem by the Chinese writer Po Chü-i about the death of Yang
Kuei-fei, favourite of the Emperor Ming Huang.
pairs.quijote_source:
They gave it,
showing at the same time that they were very sorry he was leaving them.

pairs.display: The Emperor himself was full of tender concern, and though messengers kept him constantly informed, they found tables laid out, and choicely, plentifully, and neatly furnished.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor himself was full of tender concern, and though messengers kept him constantly informed,
pairs.quijote_half: they found tables laid out, and choicely, plentifully, and neatly furnished.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor himself was full of tender concern, and though messengers
kept him constantly informed, the gloomiest doubts and fancies passed
continually through his mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
They repaired to the tents,
where they found tables laid out, and choicely, plentifully, and neatly
furnished.

pairs.display: Overjoyed at her return, birds that deceived by the colour fell into the danger they were flying from.
pairs.genji_half: Overjoyed at her return,
pairs.quijote_half: birds that deceived by the colour fell into the danger they were flying from.
pairs.genji_source:
Overjoyed at her
return, the Emperor lavished boundless affection upon her.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now the
game was started, and the nets were filled with a variety of birds that
deceived by the colour fell into the danger they were flying from.

pairs.display: The words of the song were aimed at Aoi especially if the stripes fall upon an ill-nourished and worse-fed body.
pairs.genji_half: The words of the song were aimed at Aoi
pairs.quijote_half: especially if the stripes fall upon an ill-nourished and worse-fed body.
pairs.genji_source:
The words of the song were aimed at Aoi and he sang them with much
feeling.
pairs.quijote_source:
Let me tell your worship that for a man to whip himself in cold blood is
a hard thing, especially if the stripes fall upon an ill-nourished and
worse-fed body.

pairs.display: One day when he was at his apartments in the Emperor’s Palace, pushing on to reach an inn which was in sight, apparently a league off.
pairs.genji_half: One day when he was at his apartments in the Emperor’s Palace,
pairs.quijote_half: pushing on to reach an inn which was in sight, apparently a league off.
pairs.genji_source:
One day when he was at his
apartments in the Emperor’s Palace, Myōbu came to see him.
pairs.quijote_source:
They woke up rather late, mounted once more and resumed their journey,
pushing on to reach an inn which was in sight, apparently a league off.

pairs.display: I wonder what the Emperor will say for the kites had stolen them.
pairs.genji_half: I wonder what the Emperor will say
pairs.quijote_half: for the kites had stolen them.
pairs.genji_source:
I
wonder what the Emperor will say when I go back to the Palace?’
pairs.quijote_source: The landlord replied he had no chickens, for the kites had stolen them.

pairs.display: So moving and beautiful was this dance that at the end of it the Emperor’s eyes were wet, with four of the pistols they call petronels in that country at his waist.
pairs.genji_half: So moving and beautiful was this dance that at the end of it the Emperor’s eyes were wet,
pairs.quijote_half: with four of the pistols they call petronels in that country at his waist.
pairs.genji_source:
So moving and beautiful was this dance
that at the end of it the Emperor’s eyes were wet, and all the princes
and great gentlemen wept aloud.
pairs.quijote_source:
He was mounted upon a powerful horse, and had on a coat of mail, with
four of the pistols they call petronels in that country at his waist.

pairs.display: She was the only child of the Emperor’s sister to what desperate lengths dost thou lead those that give thee lodging in their bosoms!
pairs.genji_half: She was the only child of the Emperor’s sister
pairs.quijote_half: to what desperate lengths dost thou lead those that give thee lodging in their bosoms!
pairs.genji_source:
She was the only child of the Emperor’s sister and had been
brought up with a full sense of her own dignity and importance.
pairs.quijote_source:
O furious force of jealousy,
to what desperate lengths dost thou lead those that give thee lodging in
their bosoms!

pairs.display: When this was over his Majesty and the pilgrims did not at all enjoy seeing their property confiscated.
pairs.genji_half: When this was over his Majesty
pairs.quijote_half: and the pilgrims did not at all enjoy seeing their property confiscated.
pairs.genji_source:
When this was over his Majesty sent for
his valets and went with them into another room.
pairs.quijote_source:
The captains showed plainly the concern they felt, the regent’s lady was
downcast, and the pilgrims did not at all enjoy seeing their property
confiscated.

pairs.display: All this was seen by His Majesty who, his toilet long ago completed, watched them with wonder, and passed on.
pairs.genji_half: All this was seen by His Majesty who, his toilet long ago completed,
pairs.quijote_half: watched them with wonder, and passed on.
pairs.genji_source:
All this
was seen by His Majesty who, his toilet long ago completed, had
watched the ill-assorted pair with great amusement from behind his
Imperial screen.
pairs.quijote_source:
He went up to one case and asked what they were about
there; the workmen told him, he watched them with wonder, and passed on.
