pairs.display: These were so appropriate that they could hardly have been produced on the spur of the moment, where a vast multitude stood waiting for them, eager to see what they brought back.
pairs.genji_half: These were so appropriate that they could hardly have been produced on the spur of the moment,
pairs.quijote_half: where a vast multitude stood waiting for them, eager to see what they brought back.
pairs.genji_source:
These were so appropriate that they
could hardly have been produced on the spur of the moment, and it
almost seemed as though some hint of Murasaki’s intention had reached
the Empress’s quarters beforehand.
pairs.quijote_source:
The other two
galleys now joined company and all four returned with the prize to the
beach, where a vast multitude stood waiting for them, eager to see what
they brought back.

pairs.display: As for you, Niou, rashness is not valour?
pairs.genji_half: As for you, Niou,
pairs.quijote_half: rashness is not valour?
pairs.genji_source: As for you, Niou, you’re a little rascal.
pairs.quijote_source: Knowest thou not that rashness is not valour?

pairs.display: Koremitsu, who was near his coach, overheard these words, and regarding them as a command to him to produce writing materials he overthrew the virtuous resolves of Camilla and won the triumph he least expected and most longed for.
pairs.genji_half: Koremitsu, who was near his coach, overheard these words, and regarding them as a command to him to produce writing materials
pairs.quijote_half: he overthrew the virtuous resolves of Camilla and won the triumph he least expected and most longed for.
pairs.genji_source:
Koremitsu, who was near his coach, overheard these words, and
regarding them as a command to him to produce writing materials (a
duty for which he was often in request) he whipped out a short-handled
pen from the folds of his dress and as soon as Genji’s coach came to a
standstill handed it in to him.
pairs.quijote_source:
He wept, he
entreated, he promised, he flattered, he importuned, he pretended with so
much feeling and apparent sincerity, that he overthrew the virtuous
resolves of Camilla and won the triumph he least expected and most longed
for.

pairs.display: After the festival of the White Horse on the seventh day Genji left the Emperor’s presence at nightfall in dread of some catastrophe in consequence of his appearance at such a moment.
pairs.genji_half: After the festival of the White Horse on the seventh day Genji left the Emperor’s presence at nightfall
pairs.quijote_half: in dread of some catastrophe in consequence of his appearance at such a moment.
pairs.genji_source:
After the
festival of the White Horse on the seventh day Genji left the
Emperor’s presence at nightfall and went to his own apartments in the
Palace as though intending to stay the night there.
pairs.quijote_source:
As he approached he was recognised by everyone as the
gay Basilio, and all waited anxiously to see what would come of his
words, in dread of some catastrophe in consequence of his appearance at
such a moment.

pairs.display: Whereupon Chūjō danced the Park of Willow Flowers, giving a far more complete performance than Genji, and as he was one of those who were privy to the arrangement of the affair he knew not what to say in reply.
pairs.genji_half: Whereupon Chūjō danced the Park of Willow Flowers, giving a far more complete performance than Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: and as he was one of those who were privy to the arrangement of the affair he knew not what to say in reply.
pairs.genji_source:
Whereupon Chūjō danced the Park of Willow Flowers, giving a far more
complete performance than Genji, for no doubt he knew that he would be
called upon and had taken trouble to prepare his dance.
pairs.quijote_source:
The marshal of the field was lost in astonishment at the words of
Tosilos; and as he was one of those who were privy to the arrangement of
the affair he knew not what to say in reply.

pairs.display: However, it was a consolation to feel that Genji was now definitely re-embarking upon the path of prosperity, what bowels of brass, what a soul of mortar!
pairs.genji_half: However, it was a consolation to feel that Genji was now definitely re-embarking upon the path of prosperity,
pairs.quijote_half: what bowels of brass, what a soul of mortar!
pairs.genji_source:
However, it was a consolation to feel
that Genji was now definitely re-embarking upon the path of prosperity,
and that his partiality, should it continue, would be in the future
even more valuable than before.
pairs.quijote_source:
What a heart of marble,
what bowels of brass, what a soul of mortar!

pairs.display: Murasaki while she listened could not but remember how particularly unhappy she had been just at the very time and thus, nature combined with art, and art with nature, will produce a perfect poet.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki while she listened could not but remember how particularly unhappy she had been just at the very time
pairs.quijote_half: and thus, nature combined with art, and art with nature, will produce a perfect poet.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki while she listened could not but remember how
particularly unhappy she had been just at the very time when the
episodes which Genji was now recalling with such relish were taking
place at Akashi.
pairs.quijote_source:
The
reason is, that art does not surpass nature, but only brings it to
perfection; and thus, nature combined with art, and art with nature, will
produce a perfect poet.

pairs.display: The wet-nurse and other young persons whom at the time of the child’s birth Genji had sent helped him to bed, where they gave him something to eat and made him as comfortable as possible.
pairs.genji_half: The wet-nurse and other young persons whom at the time of the child’s birth Genji had sent
pairs.quijote_half: helped him to bed, where they gave him something to eat and made him as comfortable as possible.
pairs.genji_source:
The wet-nurse
and other young persons whom at the time of the child’s birth Genji
had sent from the Capital were beginning to become very restive and
the prospect of the coming journey delighted them.
pairs.quijote_source:
And the
good wenches (for that they undoubtedly were), the housekeeper and niece,
helped him to bed, where they gave him something to eat and made him as
comfortable as possible.

pairs.display: But women are not supposed to know anything about Chinese literature, and I will not and sell three, six, or ten thousand vassals while you would be talking about it!
pairs.genji_half: But women are not supposed to know anything about Chinese literature, and I will not
pairs.quijote_half: and sell three, six, or ten thousand vassals while you would be talking about it!
pairs.genji_source:
But women are not
supposed to know anything about Chinese literature, and I will not
shock your sense of propriety by quoting any of the poems—even that by
which Genji so deeply moved his hearers.
pairs.quijote_source:
Not unless you go to sleep and haven’t the wit or
skill to turn things to account and sell three, six, or ten thousand
vassals while you would be talking about it!

pairs.display: Although Nyosan would in Genji’s household grow up as one among many, and used my body as if it was an ill-washed rag, and, woe is me!
pairs.genji_half: Although Nyosan would in Genji’s household grow up as one among many,
pairs.quijote_half: and used my body as if it was an ill-washed rag, and, woe is me!
pairs.genji_source:
Although Nyosan would in Genji’s household grow up
as one among many, she would at least enjoy the advantage of having
come there as a child, an orphan whose interests Genji was pledged to
defend.
pairs.quijote_source:
Señor governor of my soul, this wicked man caught
me in the middle of the fields here and used my body as if it was an
ill-washed rag, and, woe is me!

pairs.display: With Nyosan, however, seeing them waiting on his words in mute expectation, began thus in a pleasant quiet voice.
pairs.genji_half: With Nyosan, however,
pairs.quijote_half: seeing them waiting on his words in mute expectation, began thus in a pleasant quiet voice.
pairs.genji_source:
With Nyosan, however,
this was not the case.
pairs.quijote_source:
These words made them
settle themselves in their places and preserve a deep silence, and he
seeing them waiting on his words in mute expectation, began thus in a
pleasant quiet voice.

pairs.display: Ukon, the brother of Ki no Kami, had relinquished his office of Treasurer, in the teeth of all the clowns in the world.
pairs.genji_half: Ukon, the brother of Ki no Kami, had relinquished his office of Treasurer,
pairs.quijote_half: in the teeth of all the clowns in the world.
pairs.genji_source:
Ukon, the brother of Ki no Kami, had relinquished his office of
Treasurer, and having been appointed Quiver-bearer to His Majesty had
this year been formally invested as an officer of the 5th rank.

pairs.display: It will be remembered that at the time of Lady Akikonomu’s departure for Ise the retired Emperor Suzaku had answered him so angrily and harshly that he had no heart to say anything more to her.
pairs.genji_half: It will be remembered that at the time of Lady Akikonomu’s departure for Ise the retired Emperor Suzaku had
pairs.quijote_half: answered him so angrily and harshly that he had no heart to say anything more to her.
pairs.genji_source:
It will be remembered that at the time of Lady Akikonomu’s departure
for Ise the retired Emperor Suzaku had, when presiding at the
magnificent farewell ceremony in the Daigoku Hall, been greatly struck
with her beauty.
pairs.quijote_source:
Lothario replied that he was not disposed to go
on with the business, for she had answered him so angrily and harshly
that he had no heart to say anything more to her.

pairs.display: But it was known that Genji, Tō no Chūjō and Higekuro, the three most powerful men in the country, were her sponsors; whom Camilla received with averted countenance, though with smiles in her heart.
pairs.genji_half: But it was known that Genji, Tō no Chūjō and Higekuro, the three most powerful men in the country, were her sponsors;
pairs.quijote_half: whom Camilla received with averted countenance, though with smiles in her heart.
pairs.genji_source:
But it was known that
Genji, Tō no Chūjō and Higekuro, the three most powerful men in the
country, were her sponsors; while she was actually attended at the time
of her arrival by Yūgiri and several of her elder brothers, so that
it was by no means likely that she would be overlooked.
pairs.quijote_source:
He himself, persuaded he was conducting the instrument of
his glory, led home by the hand him who had been the utter destruction of
his good name; whom Camilla received with averted countenance, though
with smiles in her heart.

pairs.display: And after some further description of the child’s attainments he proceeded to the Heir Apparent’s and then the third went forward, but with the same result as the first and second.
pairs.genji_half: And after some further description of the child’s attainments he proceeded to the Heir Apparent’s
pairs.quijote_half: and then the third went forward, but with the same result as the first and second.
pairs.genji_source:
And after some
further description of the child’s attainments he proceeded to the Heir
Apparent’s apartments.

There was a certain Tō no Bēn, a son of Kōkiden’s elder brother Tō
Dainagon.
pairs.quijote_source:
Another of my comrades went, and with him the same
happened as with the first, and then the third went forward, but with the
same result as the first and second.

pairs.display: In due time Princess Chūjō showed them to the Emperor, who was naturally very much interested that their owners were giants, and as tall as great towers; geometry puts this fact beyond a doubt.
pairs.genji_half: In due time Princess Chūjō showed them to the Emperor, who was naturally very much interested
pairs.quijote_half: that their owners were giants, and as tall as great towers; geometry puts this fact beyond a doubt.
pairs.genji_source:
In due time Princess Chūjō
showed them to the Emperor, who was naturally very much interested
and soon afterwards asked for them again, saying that he thought
Princess Akikonomu would like to see them.
pairs.quijote_source:
Likewise, in the
island of Sicily, there have been found leg-bones and arm-bones so large
that their size makes it plain that their owners were giants, and as tall
as great towers; geometry puts this fact beyond a doubt.

pairs.display: At the summons of the herald Genji and Tō no Chūjō now appeared and with them Genji’s half-brother, see what hideous countenances come to frighten us!
pairs.genji_half: At the summons of the herald Genji and Tō no Chūjō now appeared and with them Genji’s half-brother,
pairs.quijote_half: see what hideous countenances come to frighten us!
pairs.genji_source:
At the summons of the herald Genji and Tō no Chūjō now
appeared and with them Genji’s half-brother, Prince Sochi no Miya,
who among the various arts which he cultivated was particularly fond
of painting.
pairs.quijote_source:
See
what ruffians and villains come out against me; see what monsters oppose
me; see what hideous countenances come to frighten us!

pairs.display: It appeared that even after her partial recovery Genji had continued to be perhaps unduly nervous of leaving her; with as great a retinue of huntsmen and beaters as a crowned king.
pairs.genji_half: It appeared that even after her partial recovery Genji had continued to be perhaps unduly nervous of leaving her;
pairs.quijote_half: with as great a retinue of huntsmen and beaters as a crowned king.
pairs.genji_source:
It appeared that even after her partial recovery
Genji had continued to be perhaps unduly nervous of leaving her; and
this protracted neglect, for which Nyosan would see no adequate cause,
had no doubt tried her patience.
pairs.quijote_source:
Having,
therefore, instructed their servants in everything they were to do, six
days afterwards they took him out to hunt, with as great a retinue of
huntsmen and beaters as a crowned king.

pairs.display: The quiver-bearer’s daughter meant to cut my throat and shear my head clean off.
pairs.genji_half: The quiver-bearer’s daughter
pairs.quijote_half: meant to cut my throat and shear my head clean off.
pairs.genji_source: The quiver-bearer’s daughter found the Emperor still awake.
pairs.quijote_source:
This done, he drew from its
sheath a huge broad scimitar, and seizing me by the hair he made as
though he meant to cut my throat and shear my head clean off.

pairs.display: The old Emperor had constantly invited the widowed lady and her daughter to live with him in the Palace, so much so that, cutting a multitude of capers, they flung their masters to the ground.
pairs.genji_half: The old Emperor had constantly invited the widowed lady and her daughter to live with him in the Palace,
pairs.quijote_half: so much so that, cutting a multitude of capers, they flung their masters to the ground.
pairs.genji_source:
The old Emperor had constantly
invited the widowed lady and her daughter to live with him in the
Palace, but she was reluctant to accept this offer, which indeed was
somewhat impracticable.
pairs.quijote_source:
The poor beasts felt the strange spurs and added to their anguish
by pressing their tails tight, so much so that, cutting a multitude of
capers, they flung their masters to the ground.

pairs.display: There were some papers covered with Genji’s handwriting, and these the old man clasped with an eagerness so exactly that no trace of the joining was visible.
pairs.genji_half: There were some papers covered with Genji’s handwriting, and these the old man clasped with an eagerness
pairs.quijote_half: so exactly that no trace of the joining was visible.
pairs.genji_source:
There were
some papers covered with Genji’s handwriting, and these the old man
clasped with an eagerness that made some of the gentlewomen who had
followed him smile even in the midst of their grief.
pairs.quijote_source:
The head, which resembled a bust
or figure of a Roman emperor, and was coloured like bronze, was hollow
throughout, as was the table, into which it was fitted so exactly that no
trace of the joining was visible.

pairs.display: The Heir Apparent was to have come at the same time as the Emperor; the anxiety his fainting away had caused them was relieved by his returning to himself.
pairs.genji_half: The Heir Apparent was to have come at the same time as the Emperor;
pairs.quijote_half: the anxiety his fainting away had caused them was relieved by his returning to himself.
pairs.genji_source:
The Heir Apparent was to have come at
the same time as the Emperor; but it was thought that this arrangement
would be too tiring and the little boy was brought on another day.
pairs.quijote_source:
Those who had been concerned in the joke were now sorry
they had pushed it so far; however, the anxiety his fainting away had
caused them was relieved by his returning to himself.

pairs.display: It had not very often happened that a collateral descendant of the Emperor was chosen and with tears of joyful satisfaction returned thanks to God our Lord for all his incomparable goodness to us on our voyage.
pairs.genji_half: It had not very often happened that a collateral descendant of the Emperor was chosen
pairs.quijote_half: and with tears of joyful satisfaction returned thanks to God our Lord for all his incomparable goodness to us on our voyage.
pairs.genji_source:
It had not very often happened
that a collateral descendant of the Emperor was chosen for this post;
but on this occasion no other princess of suitable age and lineage was
available.
pairs.quijote_source:
We ran our boat up
on the sand, and all sprang out and kissed the ground, and with tears of
joyful satisfaction returned thanks to God our Lord for all his
incomparable goodness to us on our voyage.

pairs.display: The Emperor’s own ladies-in-waiting carried off as evidence of his bravery the standard of the Order of Malta.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor’s own ladies-in-waiting
pairs.quijote_half: carried off as evidence of his bravery the standard of the Order of Malta.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor’s own
ladies-in-waiting were divided in opinion.
pairs.quijote_source:
They carried me to Constantinople, where the Grand Turk, Selim, made my
master general at sea for having done his duty in the battle and carried
off as evidence of his bravery the standard of the Order of Malta.

pairs.display: Only Tamakatsura and her companions knew that in that rapidly approaching craft the number of the enemy killed in twenty-two general assaults exceeded twenty-five thousand.
pairs.genji_half: Only Tamakatsura and her companions knew that in that rapidly approaching craft
pairs.quijote_half: the number of the enemy killed in twenty-two general assaults exceeded twenty-five thousand.
pairs.genji_source:
Only Tamakatsura and
her companions knew that in that rapidly approaching craft there was
one who, by them at any rate, was far more to be dreaded than the most
ruthless pirate.
pairs.quijote_source:
The fort also fell; but the Turks had to win it inch by inch,
for the soldiers who defended it fought so gallantly and stoutly that the
number of the enemy killed in twenty-two general assaults exceeded
twenty-five thousand.

pairs.display: Looking round at Genji’s Fiftieth Day presents which lay about in astonishing profusion she realized the strange nature of Don Quixote’s madness, and how they were waiting for his squire, who had gone in search of him.
pairs.genji_half: Looking round at Genji’s Fiftieth Day presents which lay about in astonishing profusion she realized
pairs.quijote_half: the strange nature of Don Quixote’s madness, and how they were waiting for his squire, who had gone in search of him.
pairs.genji_source:
Looking round at Genji’s
Fiftieth Day presents which lay about in astonishing profusion
she realized how dark a day this would have been for her but for the
coming of this messenger from the City.
pairs.quijote_source:
He also explained to them in a few words the
object that had brought them there, and the strange nature of Don
Quixote’s madness, and how they were waiting for his squire, who had gone
in search of him.

pairs.display: Genji had often asked to see them, but hitherto she had refused, and there he would keep us all hanging open-mouthed on the stories he told us of his exploits.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had often asked to see them, but hitherto she had refused,
pairs.quijote_half: and there he would keep us all hanging open-mouthed on the stories he told us of his exploits.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had often asked to see them, but
hitherto she had refused, and on this occasion it was only in obedience
to her husband’s absolute command that she had brought them to the New
Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
He used to seat himself on a
bench under the great poplar in our plaza, and there he would keep us all
hanging open-mouthed on the stories he told us of his exploits.

pairs.display: While he wrote to the Lady of his palace her father was horror-stricken, not knowing how willingly she had placed herself in our hands.
pairs.genji_half: While he wrote to the Lady of his palace
pairs.quijote_half: her father was horror-stricken, not knowing how willingly she had placed herself in our hands.
pairs.genji_source:
While he wrote to
the Lady of his palace and again while he wrote to Fujitsubo in her
cloister he wept so bitterly that the letters had many times to be put
aside.
pairs.quijote_source:
When his daughter caught sight of him she covered her eyes so as
not to see him, and her father was horror-stricken, not knowing how
willingly she had placed herself in our hands.

pairs.display: It was on his conscience that he had not for a long while been to the Great Hall; but he would not on any account, saying that modesty became knights-errant just as much as valour.
pairs.genji_half: It was on his conscience that he had not for a long while been to the Great Hall;
pairs.quijote_half: but he would not on any account, saying that modesty became knights-errant just as much as valour.
pairs.genji_source:
It was on his
conscience that he had not for a long while been to the Great Hall;
but fearing that Murasaki too might be feeling very unhappy he first
went home to give her her lessons.
pairs.quijote_source:
They asked him to let himself be stripped that they
might put a shirt on him, but he would not on any account, saying that
modesty became knights-errant just as much as valour.

pairs.display: During the first three nights Murasaki saw nothing of him, and though this was quite natural under the circumstances, so time rolls with never-ceasing wheel.
pairs.genji_half: During the first three nights Murasaki saw nothing of him, and though this was quite natural under the circumstances,
pairs.quijote_half: so time rolls with never-ceasing wheel.
pairs.quijote_source:
The spring succeeds the
summer, the summer the fall, the fall the autumn, the autumn the winter,
and the winter the spring, and so time rolls with never-ceasing wheel.

pairs.display: But Yūgiri could not summon up much interest in the round of visits upon which his father had embarked, the said Vicente having returned from service as a soldier in Italy and divers other parts.
pairs.genji_half: But Yūgiri could not summon up much interest in the round of visits upon which his father had embarked,
pairs.quijote_half: the said Vicente having returned from service as a soldier in Italy and divers other parts.
pairs.genji_source:
But Yūgiri could not summon up much interest in the round of visits
upon which his father had embarked, and slipped away to the rooms of
his little sister, the Princess from Akashi.
pairs.quijote_source:
About this time there arrived in our town one Vicente de la Roca, the son
of a poor peasant of the same town, the said Vicente having returned from
service as a soldier in Italy and divers other parts.

pairs.display: Duly crowned, Genji went to his chamber and changing into man’s dress went down into the courtyard or thou knowest not what thou dost demand.
pairs.genji_half: Duly crowned, Genji went to his chamber and changing into man’s dress went down into the courtyard
pairs.quijote_half: or thou knowest not what thou dost demand.
pairs.genji_source:
Duly crowned, Genji went to his chamber and changing into man’s dress
went down into the courtyard and performed the Dance of Homage, which
he did with such grace that tears stood in every eye.
pairs.quijote_source:
Either thou dost not hold her to be what
thou sayest, or thou knowest not what thou dost demand.

pairs.display: It is on this day that the fifth book is read; despite the disapproval of Kōkiden and had been for ages lying forgotten in a corner eaten with rust and covered with mildew.
pairs.genji_half: It is on this day that the fifth book is read; despite the disapproval of Kōkiden
pairs.quijote_half: and had been for ages lying forgotten in a corner eaten with rust and covered with mildew.
pairs.genji_source:
It is on this
day that the fifth book is read; despite the disapproval of Kōkiden
and her flatterers, the ceremony was attended by the greater
part of those about the Court.
pairs.quijote_source:
The first thing he did was to clean up some armour that had belonged to
his great-grandfather, and had been for ages lying forgotten in a corner
eaten with rust and covered with mildew.

pairs.display: Genji, making furious signs at them to be quiet, put down a half cheese harder than if it had been made of mortar.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, making furious signs at them to be quiet,
pairs.quijote_half: put down a half cheese harder than if it had been made of mortar.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, making furious signs at them to be quiet, flung
himself on to a divan.
pairs.quijote_source:
The course of meat finished, they spread upon the
sheepskins a great heap of parched acorns, and with them they put down a
half cheese harder than if it had been made of mortar.

pairs.display: Genji, quietly surveying the scene, decided this was one of those wherewith they went as bravely and becomingly decked as our Court dames with all the rare and far-fetched artifices that idle curiosity has taught them.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, quietly surveying the scene, decided this was one of those
pairs.quijote_half: wherewith they went as bravely and becomingly decked as our Court dames with all the rare and far-fetched artifices that idle curiosity has taught them.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, quietly surveying the scene, decided this was
one of those middle-class families which in last night’s conversation
had been so highly commended.
pairs.quijote_source:
Nor were their ornaments like those in use to-day,
set off by Tyrian purple, and silk tortured in endless fashions, but the
wreathed leaves of the green dock and ivy, wherewith they went as bravely
and becomingly decked as our Court dames with all the rare and
far-fetched artifices that idle curiosity has taught them.

pairs.display: Genji began to doze, and waited till she was somewhat more advanced in age and could mate herself to her own liking.
pairs.genji_half: Genji began to doze,
pairs.quijote_half: and waited till she was somewhat more advanced in age and could mate herself to her own liking.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji began to doze, and his attendants sat
silent and motionless.
pairs.quijote_source:
At these, to all
appearance, reasonable excuses that she made, her uncle ceased to urge
her, and waited till she was somewhat more advanced in age and could mate
herself to her own liking.

pairs.display: Genji was rather disappointed that she at once set about tending Don Quixote, and made her young daughter, a very comely girl, help her in taking care of her guest.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was rather disappointed that she
pairs.quijote_half: at once set about tending Don Quixote, and made her young daughter, a very comely girl, help her in taking care of her guest.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was rather disappointed that she did not ask more
questions about him.
pairs.quijote_source:
The innkeeper
had a wife whose disposition was not such as those of her calling
commonly have, for she was by nature kind-hearted and felt for the
sufferings of her neighbours, so she at once set about tending Don
Quixote, and made her young daughter, a very comely girl, help her in
taking care of her guest.

pairs.display: Genji gave an astonished cry and the maid began to salute his ears with stones as big as one’s fist.
pairs.genji_half: Genji gave an astonished cry and the maid
pairs.quijote_half: began to salute his ears with stones as big as one’s fist.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji gave an
astonished cry and the maid, wondering who could have entered the
ante-room, began groping her way towards them.
pairs.quijote_source:
The shepherds and drovers
accompanying the flock shouted to him to desist; seeing it was no use,
they ungirt their slings and began to salute his ears with stones as big
as one’s fist.

pairs.display: Genji placed a screen so as to hide the bed where they found Camilla awaiting her husband anxiously and uneasily, for he was later than usual in returning that day.
pairs.genji_half: Genji placed a screen so as to hide the bed
pairs.quijote_half: where they found Camilla awaiting her husband anxiously and uneasily, for he was later than usual in returning that day.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji placed a screen
so as to hide the bed and called the man to him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Lothario agreed to all with an intention very different from
what Anselmo supposed, and with this understanding they returned to
Anselmo’s house, where they found Camilla awaiting her husband anxiously
and uneasily, for he was later than usual in returning that day.

pairs.display: Genji, hardly conscious of what went on around him and the intelligence the shepherd gave him of Angelica having slept more than two siestas with Medoro, a little curly-headed Moor, and page to Agramante.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, hardly conscious of what went on around him
pairs.quijote_half: and the intelligence the shepherd gave him of Angelica having slept more than two siestas with Medoro, a little curly-headed Moor, and page to Agramante.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, hardly conscious of what went on around him arrived at last
in ghostly pallor at his house.
pairs.quijote_source:
But putting the question of
his valour aside, let us come to his losing his wits, for certain it is
that he did lose them in consequence of the proofs he discovered at the
fountain, and the intelligence the shepherd gave him of Angelica having
slept more than two siestas with Medoro, a little curly-headed Moor, and
page to Agramante.

pairs.display: Genji flung himself upon his bed and delighted beyond measure at such intelligence he sent word to her not to leave his house on any account, as he would very shortly return.
pairs.genji_half: Genji flung himself upon his bed
pairs.quijote_half: and delighted beyond measure at such intelligence he sent word to her not to leave his house on any account, as he would very shortly return.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji flung himself upon
his bed and lay there in fever and pain for several days.
pairs.quijote_source:
Anselmo received this letter, and from it he gathered that Lothario had
already begun his task and that Camilla must have replied to him as he
would have wished; and delighted beyond measure at such intelligence he
sent word to her not to leave his house on any account, as he would very
shortly return.

pairs.display: Genji did not send in his name and was in close disguise, and he looked forward anxiously to meeting him in order to rejoice together over falsehood and truth the most craftily veiled that could be imagined.
pairs.genji_half: Genji did not send in his name and was in close disguise,
pairs.quijote_half: and he looked forward anxiously to meeting him in order to rejoice together over falsehood and truth the most craftily veiled that could be imagined.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji did not send in his name and was in close
disguise, but his face was well known and the priest at once
recognized him.
pairs.quijote_source:
He reflected how convinced Anselmo would be
that he had a second Portia for a wife, and he looked forward anxiously
to meeting him in order to rejoice together over falsehood and truth the
most craftily veiled that could be imagined.

pairs.display: Genji did not rise till the Turks acknowledged he did it merely for the sake of doing it, and because he was by nature murderously disposed towards the whole human race.
pairs.genji_half: Genji did not rise till
pairs.quijote_half: the Turks acknowledged he did it merely for the sake of doing it, and because he was by nature murderously disposed towards the whole human race.
pairs.genji_source: Genji did not rise till far on into the morning.
pairs.quijote_source:
Every day he hanged a man, impaled one,
cut off the ears of another; and all with so little provocation, or so
entirely without any, that the Turks acknowledged he did it merely for
the sake of doing it, and because he was by nature murderously disposed
towards the whole human race.

pairs.display: In her shabby dress made of some dark grey material she looked so charming set with so many diamonds that she told me afterwards her father valued them at ten thousand doubloons, and those she had on her wrists were worth as much more.
pairs.genji_half: In her shabby dress made of some dark grey material she looked so charming
pairs.quijote_half: set with so many diamonds that she told me afterwards her father valued them at ten thousand doubloons, and those she had on her wrists were worth as much more.
pairs.genji_source:
In her shabby dress
made of some dark grey material she looked so charming now that she
was laughing and playing, with all her woes forgotten, that Genji too
laughed with pleasure as he watched her.
pairs.quijote_source:
On her ankles, which as is customary were bare, she had carcajes
(for so bracelets or anklets are called in Morisco) of the purest gold,
set with so many diamonds that she told me afterwards her father valued
them at ten thousand doubloons, and those she had on her wrists were
worth as much more.

pairs.display: Genji had made them promise for a time at any rate to tell no one of what had happened but the renegade once more told him not to utter a word, or they would take his life.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had made them promise for a time at any rate to tell no one of what had happened
pairs.quijote_half: but the renegade once more told him not to utter a word, or they would take his life.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
had made them promise for a time at any rate to tell no one of what
had happened and Shōnagon had seemed to agree that this was best.
pairs.quijote_source:
It was barely two hours
after night set in when we were all on board the vessel, where the cords
were removed from the hands of Zoraida’s father, and the napkin from his
mouth; but the renegade once more told him not to utter a word, or they
would take his life.

pairs.display: Genji’s sympathy was aroused and as now almost two-thirds of the night were past, they resolved to retire to rest for the remainder of it.
pairs.genji_half: Genji’s sympathy was aroused
pairs.quijote_half: and as now almost two-thirds of the night were past, they resolved to retire to rest for the remainder of it.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji’s sympathy was aroused and he
began to question Myōbu about this unfortunate lady.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, everybody was well pleased and glad at
the captive’s good fortune; and as now almost two-thirds of the night
were past, they resolved to retire to rest for the remainder of it.

pairs.display: Genji pretended to be still looking out of the window, a hole in the wall of a straw-loft through which they used to throw out the straw.
pairs.genji_half: Genji pretended to be still looking out of the window,
pairs.quijote_half: a hole in the wall of a straw-loft through which they used to throw out the straw.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji pretended to be still looking out of the window, but presently
he managed to glance back into the room.
pairs.quijote_source:
As it so
happened there was not a window in the whole inn that looked outwards
except a hole in the wall of a straw-loft through which they used to
throw out the straw.

pairs.display: Genji was beginning to say politely a thing that still further surprised everybody.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was beginning to say politely
pairs.quijote_half: a thing that still further surprised everybody.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was beginning to say politely, when one of
the ladies handed him a fan with the corner bent down.
pairs.quijote_source:
She said, moreover, that the soldier, without robbing her of
her honour, had taken from her everything she had, and made off, leaving
her in the cave, a thing that still further surprised everybody.

pairs.display: Genji was afraid that his and their laughter acted like gunpowder on Don Quixote’s fury, for drawing his sword without another word he made a rush at the stand.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was afraid that his
pairs.quijote_half: and their laughter acted like gunpowder on Don Quixote’s fury, for drawing his sword without another word he made a rush at the stand.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was afraid
that his neighbour was going to prove troublesome.
pairs.quijote_source:
From these words all the hearers concluded that he must be a madman, and
began to laugh heartily, and their laughter acted like gunpowder on Don
Quixote’s fury, for drawing his sword without another word he made a rush
at the stand.

pairs.display: Koremitsu had patiently continued the enquiry and got his master's blessing, which Don Quixote gave him with tears, and he received blubbering.
pairs.genji_half: Koremitsu had patiently continued the enquiry
pairs.quijote_half: and got his master's blessing, which Don Quixote gave him with tears, and he received blubbering.
pairs.genji_source: Koremitsu had patiently continued the enquiry with which Genji entrusted him.
pairs.quijote_source: On taking leave he kissed the hands of the duke and duchess and got his master’s blessing, which Don Quixote gave him with tears, and he received blubbering.

pairs.display: The matter of Yūgiri and Kumoi speaking to the lady in the coach
pairs.genji_half: The matter of Yūgiri and Kumoi
pairs.quijote_half: speaking to the lady in the coach
pairs.genji_source: The matter of Yūgiri and Kumoi had not been so much as mentioned during Tō no Chūjō’s visit.
pairs.quijote_source: Don Quixote was, as has been said, speaking to the lady in the coach:
